suspicious_text: The findings presented are derived from an analysis of intrusion detection system (IDS) alerts generated by a home network between 2009 and 2011, supplemented by data from the KDD-Cup'99 dataset. The analysis focused on the 32 noisiest IDS rules, each triggering at least 50 alerts per cluster, a threshold selected to ensure results fell within 95% confidence intervals established through simulations. While this limited dataset may not accurately represent the privacy risks associated with large-scale monitoring by a professional Managed Security Service (MSS) provider, it is anticipated that such providers would encounter a broader range of IDS alerts, including those with higher alarm frequencies and a greater number of attack clusters. Additionally, commercial MSS providers may utilize a more comprehensive set of IDS rules to address emerging threats not covered by the standard Snort VRT ruleset used in this study, and their traffic analysis would not be influenced by synthetic data.

source_text: The main difference between our work and the work by Hara and Slade in [27] is that in our method, the loops creating the perturbative terms are made to consist of at least 4 bonds, while in the classical lace expansion they could consist of immediate reversals (2 bonds). This makes the perturbation considerably smaller, and allows for an analysis that is to a much larger extent model-independent as the analysis in [27] and its adaptation to percolation. It also explains why our method gives reasonable results for lattice trees and lattice animals, models that previously had not been attempted by Hara and Slade. In discussions with Takashi Hara, we have found that our bounds on e.g. the triangle diagram are slightly better in dimension $15$ , whereas he has a much more sophisticated and model-dependent analysis of the lace-expansion coefficients.

suspicious_text: The sub-exponential norm of a random variable $X$ can be defined as the infimum of all positive values $t$ such that the expectation of $\exp(|X|/t)$ is bounded by $\exp(2)$, denoted as $\left\lVert X\right\rVert_{\psi_{1}}$. Considering a sequence of sub-exponential random variables $\{X_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$, where each $X_{i}$ has a mean bounded by $v$ and a sub-exponential norm bounded by $\sigma$, a fundamental inequality states that the expected value of the maximum of these variables is bounded by a constant multiple of $\sigma$ times the logarithm of the number of variables plus one, plus the mean $v$, specifically $\mathbb{E}[\max_{i}X_{i}]\leq C\sigma(\log n+1)+v$, with $C$ being an absolute constant that does not depend on the specific distribution of the $X_{i}$ or the value of $n$.

Let $\left\lVert X\right\rVert_{\psi_{1}}:=\inf\{t>0:\exp(|X|/t)\leq\exp(2)\}$ denote the sub-exponential norm of $X$ . For a given sequence of sub-exponential variables $\{X_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$ with $\mathbb{E}[X_{i}]\leq v$ and $\left\lVert X_{i}\right\rVert_{\psi_{1}}\leq\sigma$ , we have 
 
 $\displaystyle\mathbb{E}[\max_{i}X_{i}]\leq C\sigma(\log n+1)+v,$ 
 where $C$ denotes an absolute constant.

Therefore $X$ is the surface of type 4 in the theorem and $p_{g}(X)=6-(1/2)\sum[m_{i}/2]([m_{i}/2]-1)=6$ . ∎

This condition stipulates that either the conjunction of $(b \cdot d)$, $(b \rightarrow e)$, and $(d \rightarrow c)$ yields the atom $a$, or it yields $0$, with the additional stipulation that either $a$ is less than or equal to $(b \cdot d)$ or $a$ is less than or equal to the conjunction of $(b \rightarrow e)$ and $(d \rightarrow c)$.
source_text: It is worth noting that we cannot simply demand that the union $L$ in Theorem 1.3 , part (2), is topologically dense in $K$ . If we let $K=[0,1]\times\{0,1\}$ be the lexicographically ordered ‘split interval’, then $K$ is separable. However, it is well known not to be fragmentable. We provide a proof of this at the beginning of section 2 . Alternatively, we can use Proposition 1.5 , since it is easy to show that $K$ is not second countable and does not contain any uncountable well ordered or conversely well ordered subsets. Incidentally, since $K$ is 0-dimensional, this example shows that the necessary condition of Theorem 1.2 is not sufficient. Moreover, it is not possible to deduce Proposition 1.5 from Theorem 1.2 .

source_text: Palomar 3 is a remote cluster at about 96 kpc from the Galactic center and an estimated orbital minimum distance from it of $\sim$ 82.5 kpc Dinescu et al. (1999) . Since its proper motion is uncertain, it is considered possible that it may not be bound to the Galaxy and that it may be falling onto it for the first time. Pal 3 is one of the most extended clusters with a half–light radius of about 24 pc and a truncation radius of about 130 pc. It is faint, with $M_{V}$ $\sim$ –5.7, and its destruction time is estimated at about 20 Hubble times Gnedin & Ostriker (1997) .

suspicious_text: In the limit as $k$ approaches infinity, the relationship $\alpha(k\textsf{AND}) \to 2\rho(k\textsf{AND}) = 2^{-(k-1)}$ holds. In this regime, our straightforward randomized algorithm (presented in Theorem 1.8) achieves performance equivalent to that of Trevisan’s [Tre98] parallelizable LP-based algorithm for the $k\textsf{AND}$ problem. To our knowledge, this was the first work addressing the general $k\textsf{AND}$ problem. Subsequent advancements, such as [Has04], which build upon [Tre98], employ more intricate techniques involving semidefinite programming. However, these methods share a structural similarity with our algorithm in Theorem 1.8: both involve "guessing" an assignment $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}_2^n$ and subsequently perturbing each bit with a constant probability.
source_text: Interestingly, recalling that $\alpha(k\textsf{AND})\to 2\rho(k\textsf{AND})=2^{-(k-1)}$ as $k\to\infty$ , in the large- $k$ limit our simple randomized algorithm (given in Theorem1.8 ) matches the performance of Trevisan’s [Tre98] parallelizable LP-based algorithm for $k\textsf{AND}$ , which (to the best of our knowledge) was the first work on the general $k\textsf{AND}$ problem! The subsequent works [Has04] superseding [Tre98] use more complex techniques involving semidefinite programming, but are structurally similar to our algorithm in Theorem1.8 : They all involve “guessing” an assignment $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{Z}_{2}^{n}$ and then perturbing each bit with constant probability.

In the realm of relativity, the interaction between a magnetic moment, denoted as $\mu$, and a magnetic field can be expressed in terms of the magnetic field itself, using the following established identity (as detailed in [18] ):

$j^{\mu}=\overline{\psi}_{2}\gamma^{\mu}\psi_{1}=\frac{1}{2m}\overline{\psi}_{2}({p_{1}}^{\mu}+{p_{2}}^{\mu})\psi_{1}-\frac{1}{2m}\overline{\psi}_{2}\sigma^{\mu\nu}k_{\nu}\psi_{1}\;,$ (8)

source_text: The surface motion of these membrane-targeting domains is well described by Lévy flights, a random walk where the step displacements have a heavy-tailed distribution. The heavy tail arises from the dissociation of molecules from the membrane, which then perform a three-dimensional random walk until they reach the surface again at another location. The process involves the first return to a surface and it converges to a power law according to the Sparre-Andersen theorem (41) . This type of Lévy flight dynamics is fundamentally different from Lévy walks induced by molecular motors in the cytoplasm because periods of active motion require an energy input, typically in the form of ATP hydrolysis, while bulk excursions occur spontaneously.

suspicious_text: In the limit as $k$ approaches infinity, the relationship $\alpha(k\textsf{AND}) \to 2\rho(k\textsf{AND}) = 2^{-(k-1)}$ holds. In this regime, our straightforward randomized algorithm (presented in Theorem 1.8) achieves performance equivalent to that of Trevisan’s [Tre98] parallelizable LP-based algorithm for the $k\textsf{AND}$ problem. To our knowledge, this was the first work addressing the general $k\textsf{AND}$ problem. Subsequent advancements, such as [Has04], which build upon [Tre98], employ more intricate techniques involving semidefinite programming. However, these methods share a structural similarity with our algorithm in Theorem 1.8: both involve "guessing" an assignment $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}_2^n$ and subsequently perturbing each bit with a constant probability.

This relationship is formally encapsulated by the equation $V^{\pi^{\prime}}(s)-V^{\pi}(s)=\tfrac{1}{1-\gamma}\mathbb{E}_{s^{\prime}\sim d_{s}^{\pi^{\prime}}}\left[\langle A^{\pi}(s^{\prime},\cdot),\pi^{\prime}(\cdot|s^{\prime})\rangle+h^{\pi^{\prime}}(s^{\prime})-h^{\pi}(s^{\prime})\right]$, where the advantage function $A^{\pi}(s^{\prime},a)$ is defined as the difference between the action-value function $Q^{\pi}(s^{\prime},a)$ and the value function $V^{\pi}(s^{\prime})$, yielding $A^{\pi}(s^{\prime},a)=Q^{\pi}(s^{\prime},a)-V^{\pi}(s^{\prime})$.
source_text: Since the purpose of the diagnosis model is not to detect the presence of any number of coughs but to categorize cough events according to their subtle differences, the input audio files for this model are much shorter in length. Hence, we adopt a different data preparation process for making it suitable for training. The sound files are first cut to a single cough event with the longest of these files lasting two seconds, the rest are buffered to the same length with noiseless audio to maintain a fixed two second length for all samples. This fixed length is set for the spectrogram conversion process that comes afterwards and all the two-second audio files are converted into $432\times 288\times 1$ Mel-spectrograms images. Finally, all images in the cough diagnosis dataset are then converted to gray-scale to unify its intensity scaling and reduce the size of each sample to facilitate the model training process.

suspicious_text: A notable anomaly in the trend of ionic radii is observed in the transition from barium (\textsuperscript{56}Ba, [Xe] $6s^{1}$) to lanthanum (\textsuperscript{57}La, [Xe] $5d^{2}$), where an increase in ionic radius occurs despite a decrease in the effective principal quantum number $n^{*}$. This counterintuitive phenomenon can be attributed to the varying extent of nuclear charge screening by inner-shell electrons, which is more pronounced when outer-shell electrons occupy $d$ orbitals, as in the case of lanthanum, compared to $s$ orbitals, as in barium. Consequently, the decrease in effective nuclear charge $Z^{*}$ experienced by the $5d$ electrons in lanthanum outweighs the reduction in $n^{*}$, resulting in an overall increase in ionic size. suspicious_text: The first two CSPs for which the $o(\sqrt{n})$-space streaming approximability was resolved are Max-2 XOR and Max-2 AND. Kapralov, Khanna, and Sudan [KKS15] demonstrated that Max-2 XOR is approximation-resistant to $o(\sqrt{n})$-space streaming algorithms. Subsequently, Chou, Golovnev, and Velusamy [CGV20], building on the work of Guruswami, Velusamy, and Velingker [GVV17], developed an $O(\log n)$-space linear sketching algorithm that achieves a $(\frac{4}{9} - \epsilon)$-approximation for Max-2 AND for any $\epsilon > 0$. They also established that any $(\frac{4}{9} + \epsilon)$-approximation requires $\Omega(\sqrt{n})$ space, even for streaming algorithms.

where $\tau$ represents the time interval between successive measurements, and $n = \Delta/\tau$. This method proves particularly advantageous when experimental data consists of a limited number of trajectories, a common scenario in single-molecule experiments. suspicious_text: For a smooth minimal complex projective surface of general type, denoted as $X$, consider a special divisor $L$ that is not linearly equivalent to the canonical divisor $K_X$. It has been established that the dimension of the space of global sections of $L$ is bounded by the inequality $h^0(L) \leq K_XL/2 + 1$. In the event that this inequality becomes an equality, the situation can be categorized into three distinct scenarios: firstly, $h^0(L) = 1$ and $L$ decomposes into a sum of $(-2)$-curves; secondly, the movable part of the linear system $|L|$ is base-point-free, and the morphism $\varphi_L: X \rightarrow \mathbf{P}^1$ induced by $L$ is a surjective projective morphism whose general fiber is a smooth irreducible curve of genus $2$; thirdly, the movable part of $|L|$ is base-point-free, and $\varphi_L$ is generically a $2$-to-$1$ map onto a surface of minimal degree in $\mathbf{P}^{h^0(L)-1}$.
source_text: Let $X$ be a smooth minimal complex projective surface of general type. Let $L$ be a special divisor on $X$ such that $L\nsim K_{X}$ , then $h^{0}(L)\leq K_{X}L/2+1$ . If the equality holds, then one of the following cases occurs. $1.$ $h^{0}(L)=1$ and $L$ is a sum of $(-2)$ -curves. $2.$ The movable part of $|L|$ has no base points and $\varphi_{L}:X\rightarrow\mathbf{P}^{1}$ is a projective surjective morphism, whose general fiber is an irreducible smooth curve of genus $2$ . $3.$ The movable part of $|L|$ has no base points and $\varphi_{L}$ is generically $2$ to $1$ onto a surface of minimal degree in $\mathbf{P}^{h^{0}(L)-1}$ .

suspicious_text: In the first author's thesis [15], the analysis was conducted using two distinct methods. The first utilized the $x$-space approach, originally developed by Hara and Slade in [27]. This method was employed by Hara and Slade to establish that mean-field behavior applies to self-avoiding walks (SAWs) in all dimensions $d \geq 5$, which is optimal since mean-field behavior for SAWs does not hold in $d \leq 4$. For further insights, see [11] and the references cited therein. Additionally, Hara and Slade adapted their methodology to percolation [24], resulting in the significant finding that mean-field behavior for percolation is valid in dimensions $d \geq 19$.
source_text: In the thesis of the first author [15] , the analysis was performed in two ways. The first was based on the $x$ -space approach, as originally worked out by Hara and Slade in [27] . This approach was used by Hara and Slade in [27] to obtain that mean-field behavior holds for self-avoiding walk (SAW) in all dimensions $d\geq 5$ . This is optimal in the sense that mean-field behavior for SAW in $d\leq 4$ should not be true. See [11] and references therein for results in this direction. Further, Hara and Slade adapted their method to percolation [24] , which led to the famous result that mean-field behavior for percolation holds for $d\geq 19$ .

suspicious_text: At half-integer values of $\mu$, the scattering length exhibits a negative magnitude, $|a_{s}|$, that substantially surpasses the size of the central region, $r_{0}$. Notably, this occurs when the potential $U_{s}(r)$ is nearly resonant, supporting a low-energy virtual state, which in turn corresponds to the steepest slope in the $\mu(a_{B}/2\pi a_{s})$ dependence. Furthermore, at this point, the derivative of $\chi$ with respect to $r$ evaluated at $r=r_{0}$ is approximately zero, implying that the antinode of the function $\chi$ coincides with the boundary of the inner region, $r_{0}$. This condition bears resemblance to the criterion established by Fano, Theodosiou, and Dehmer for the dependence of quantum defect $\mu$ on atomic number $Z$, although the size of the residual ion in a Rydberg atom does not fulfill the requirement $r_{0}\ll a_{B}$, an issue that warrants further examination.
source_text: The organization of the paper is as follows. In Section 1 , we prove Theorem 0.1 . In Section 2 , we will give some Clifford type inequalities on a surface (Propositions 2.1 and 2.3 ) and prove Theorem 0.2 . In Section 3 , we use these two inequalities to define two indices $\alpha(X)$ and $\beta(X)$ on $X$ like the Clifford index in the case of a curve. We study some basic properties of $\alpha(X)$ and $\beta(X)$ and give some bounds for them (Propositions 3.6 and 3.8 ). In Section 4 , we give a detailed description of $X$ , when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are zero (Theorems 4.1 and 4.2 ). In Section 5 , we use our inequalities to give some bounds for the number of moduli of surfaces (Theorem 5.2 ).

source_text: The distribution of displacements for longer times involves both a random number of jumps, each having a Cauchy distribution, and the Brownian motion on the surface. Chechkin et al. derived the full solution for the propagator of bulk-mediated diffusion (42) . For the case when $D_{s}=0$ and neglecting long distance corrections, the distribution of displacements is given by the Cauchy propagator, in agreement with scaling arguments (40) , $P(\textbf{r})=\frac{\gamma t}{2\pi[r^{2}+\left(\gamma t\right)^{2}]^{3/2}}.$ When the particles also diffuse on the surface, i.e. $D_{s}\neq 0$ the probability density of the displacements is given by the convolution of equation ( 5 ) with a normal distribution. Even though the full solution for long times is complicated, the tail of this distribution for large distances still scales as $P(\textbf{r})\sim r^{-3}$ . Due to this asymptotic behaviour, the exact distribution has similar properties to the Cauchy distribution.

suspicious_text: For a generic hypersurface $S_{d}$ of degree $d$ in $\mathbf{P}^{3}$, where $d \geq 5$, the surface $S_{d}$ is minimal and of general type, with its canonical divisor $K_{S_{d}}$ expressed as $(d-4)H$, where $H$ represents the hyperplane section of $S_{d}$. According to the Noether-Lefschetz theorem, this implies that the Picard group of $S_{d}$, denoted $\operatorname{Pic}(S_{d})$, is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}H$. Consequently, for $S_{5}$, both $\alpha(S_{5})$ and $\beta(S_{5})$ are infinite.
source_text: Let $S_{d}$ be a generic hypersurface of degree $d$ in $\mathbf{P}^{3}$ . $H$ denote the hyperplane section of $S_{d}$ . When $d\geq 5$ , $S_{d}$ is a minimal surface of general type and $K_{S_{d}}=(d-4)H$ . In this case, by the Noether-Lefschetz theorem, we have $\operatorname{Pic}(S_{d})\cong\mathbb{Z}H$ . Hence $\alpha(S_{5})=\beta(S_{5})=\infty$ .

Under either of these conditions, the rational map $\varphi_{L}$ exhibits generic behavior, specifically a two-to-one surjection onto a surface of minimal degree embedded in the projective space $\mathbf{P}^{h^{0}(L)-1}$.

Since $M$ is closed and contains $\min K$ and $\max K$, there exist points $u,v\in M$ such that $(u,v)$ does not intersect $M$ and contains $w$. Now, consider any $j$ with $k<j\leq n$ and suppose $(x,y)\in A_{j}$. It must hold that $f_{x,y}(u)=f_{x,y}(w)=f_{x,y}(v)$. This is because $x$ and $y$ are elements of $M$, and since $(u,v)$ is disjoint from $M$, either $x\leq u$ and $v\leq y$, or $y\leq u$, or $v\leq x$. The first scenario is impossible, however, as $w$ does not belong to $(x,y)$.

source_text: A general finding of recent years is that all globular clusters (GCs) so far spectroscopically examined contain multiple stellar populations. This is significatively shown in the recent analysis of Carretta et al. (2009) of about two thousand stars in 19 GCs, showing that all these clusters display the sodium – oxygen anticorrelation, signature of the presence of a population of stars sodium richer and oxygen poorer than the halo stars of the same metallicity. The Na–O anti–correlation is typical of GCs, whose constituent stars belong to two or more stellar populations differing in the abundances of the elements produced by the hot CNO cycle and by other proton– capture reactions on light nuclei. In fact, these chemical signatures are present also in turn–off stars and among the subgiants Gratton et al. source_text: Finally, we also construct a third controller. Combining the in-grasp manipulation and contact switching controller we construct a finger-gaiting controller. While a randomly selected fingertip breaks and makes contact, this controller also reorients the object with the fingertips in contact. Although this controller comes closer than the rest, it does not achieve the whole finger-gaiting skill we are looking to learn. Continuous object re-orientation is not possible with this controller as the fingers selected to switch contacts are still selected at random without any coordination.

source_text: The distribution of displacements for longer times involves both a random number of jumps, each having a Cauchy distribution, and the Brownian motion on the surface. Chechkin et al. derived the full solution for the propagator of bulk-mediated diffusion (42) . For the case when $D_{s}=0$ and neglecting long distance corrections, the distribution of displacements is given by the Cauchy propagator, in agreement with scaling arguments (40) , $P(\textbf{r})=\frac{\gamma t}{2\pi[r^{2}+\left(\gamma t\right)^{2}]^{3/2}}.$ When the particles also diffuse on the surface, i.e. $D_{s}\neq 0$ the probability density of the displacements is given by the convolution of equation ( 5 ) with a normal distribution. Even though the full solution for long times is complicated, the tail of this distribution for large distances still scales as $P(\textbf{r})\sim r^{-3}$ . Due to this asymptotic behaviour, the exact distribution has similar properties to the Cauchy distribution.

suspicious_text: The processing of lengthy sequence data poses significant challenges due to the inherent trade-off between preserving global context and maintaining local details within smaller sequence segments, while also requiring efficient computational management of these competing demands. To overcome these hurdles in real-time data, we introduce the ChunkFormer, a novel convolutional block transformer architecture designed to effectively balance and optimize the capture of both global and local information.
source_text: Dexterous in-hand manipulation has long been of interest and numerous methods have been proposed ranging from simple low-level feedback primitive controllers to trajectory optimization for the complete task. Early model-based work on finger-gaiting [6] and finger-pivoting [9] propose controllers using simplified models. [10] use model-based online trajectory optimization and demonstrate finger-gaiting in simulation. [12] combine trajectory optimization and tree search to follow a desired object pose trajectory.

Suppose $Y$ is a closed subscheme of $Z$ that is both irreducible and reduced, with $\mathcal{I}_{Y}$ denoting its ideal sheaf. Given the conditions that the difference in global sections of $L$ and its restriction to $Y$ is positive, as well as the analogous condition for $D$, a relationship between the dimensions of certain cohomology groups emerges. Specifically, when $h^{0}(L)$ exceeds $h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L))$ and $h^{0}(D)$ exceeds $h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(D))$, the sum of these excesses is bounded above by the corresponding excess for the sum of the divisors $L$ and $D$, plus one, as expressed by the inequality $h^{0}(L)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L))+h^{0}(D)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(D))\leq h^{0}(L+D)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L+D))+1$.

On the other hand, the rate of convergence associated with the solution sequence of the PMD method for general RL problems cannot be established unless more structural properties of the RL problems can be further explored.

To address this issue, one potential solution is to adjust the entropy values by multiplying them by the length of the IDS alarm, thereby correcting for the data length, and then calculating the standard deviation of these corrected entropy values. However, this correction may be overly aggressive, as it would introduce a constant bias for random uniform data, resulting in the metric failing to converge to zero for encrypted traffic, thus highlighting the need for a more nuanced approach.

suspicious_text: The ionic radius exhibits a notable trend, characterized by an initial abrupt decrease followed by a more gradual decline, as observed in the transition from titanium to vanadium and chromium, and similarly in other sequences such as zirconium to niobium to molybdenum, as well as iron to cobalt to nickel to copper, and other analogous series. This phenomenon can be attributed to the shift in the outer shell from 4s to 3d, resulting in a decrease in the effective quantum number, and subsequent increases in effective nuclear charge due to the growing number of 3d electrons. This pattern is consistently observed across various sequences, including those involving transition metals with differing electron configurations, such as technetium to ruthenium to rhodium to palladium to silver, and iridium to platinum to gold, underscoring the influence of electron configuration on ionic radius.

However as compared to the $6s$ electron of Ba, the effective $Z^{*}$ seen by one of La’s $5d$ electrons also decreases. This happens because the inner shell $5sp$ electrons screen the nuclear charge more effectively if the outer shell electrons are in a $d$ state (La) as compared to an $s$ state (Ba). As a result the decrease in $Z^{*}$ outweighs the decrease in $n^{*}$ thus leading to an increase of the ion size. suspicious_text: Assuming a constant learning rate $\eta$ and bounded variance terms $\varsigma$ and $\sigma$ in the SPMD method, we derive an upper bound on the expected suboptimality of the policy obtained after $k$ iterations. Specifically, we show that the expected difference between the objective function values of the learned policy and the optimal policy is bounded by a term that decreases with the number of iterations $k$, plus additional terms that depend on the initial suboptimality, variance bounds, and the learning rate. Furthermore, if the number of iterations is fixed and the learning rate is chosen optimally, the bound can be simplified to depend only on the initial suboptimality, variance bounds, and the number of iterations, with a convergence rate of $O(1/\sqrt{k})$.

suspicious_text: The ChunkFormer model builds upon the strengths of the Transformer architecture, integrating CNN-style structures to process lengthy sequence vectors, thereby leveraging the benefits of both local and global attention mechanisms. Unlike traditional Transformers, which solely focus on global attention and neglect local interactions, ChunkFormer calculates attention matrices for subdivided input sequences, enhancing efficiency without sacrificing accuracy. This approach enables the model to effectively capture long-term dependencies, outperforming LSTM-like models on lengthy sequences. By striking a balance between accuracy and operational efficiency, ChunkFormer achieves superior performance while consuming fewer resources than baseline models, making it a practical solution for real-world applications, although random block chunk size selection may occasionally impact accuracy.
source_text: $\bullet$ ChunkFormer inherits the advantages of the Transformer model class and makes improvements to this framework. It combines Transformer and CNN-style structures on long sequence vectors. CNN-style operations do well in associating the information with local areas, while Transformers have the strength in focusing on global attention. In contrast, the traditional Transformer only calculates the global attention score of the entire input sequence and fails in capturing the local interactive information in the long sequence data. In addition, the LSTM-like model does not perform well when trained on long data sequences that need to learn long-term time dependence. $\bullet$ ChunkFormer benefits from dividing the input sequence of each stage into different sub-regions (convolution-like block). suspicious_text: This research examines a broad category of reinforcement learning problems that incorporate convex or strongly convex regularizers into their cost functions, within the context of finite Markov decision processes. These processes are defined by a set of parameters, including a finite state space, a finite action space, a transition model that predicts the next state based on the current state and action, a cost function that assigns a cost to each state-action pair, and a discount factor that determines the importance of future costs. The decision-making process is guided by a policy that maps states to actions, specifying the probability of choosing a particular action in a given state.

source_text: The assumption that $\hat{a}$ is rotational symmetric is motivated by the following fact. If a process takes place in a homogeneous and isotropic medium, then - by symmetry - the fundamental solution $G_{0}$ of the respectively evolution equation must be rotational symmetric in $\mathbf{x}$ . And consequently, $\hat{a}$ is rotational symmetric, too. Since this paper is a first approach on causality of semigroups and its evolution equations, we focus on these special types of processes.

suspicious_text: The training dataset for the cough-detection CNN consists of diverse audio clips sourced from publicly accessible online archives [9]. These recordings, each initially containing at least one cough instance, have been edited to a uniform duration of five seconds, with the cough event occurring at an unspecified position within this timeframe. If a recording contained multiple coughs spanning beyond five seconds, it was divided into separate segments. The number of coughs per segment remains intentionally inconsistent. To achieve balance, the dataset is complemented with a second category of sounds, primarily comprising environmental and vocal audio from the ESC-50 collection. Additionally, this non-cough segment incorporates sound clips extracted from the original cough recordings that do not include cough events. The resultant dataset comprises a total of 993 cough samples and an equal number of non-cough samples.

suspicious_text: The entropy standard deviation metrics have a limitation in that their values decrease as the data length increases, which, although desirable for random uniform data, is not ideal for plaintext data as it creates an incentive for adversaries to transmit large data packets to minimize detected information leakage. To address this issue, one potential solution is to adjust the entropy values by multiplying them by the length of the IDS alarm, thereby correcting for the data length, and then calculating the standard deviation of these corrected entropy values. However, this correction may be overly aggressive, as it would introduce a constant bias for random uniform data, resulting in the metric failing to converge to zero for encrypted traffic, thus highlighting the need for a more nuanced approach.
source_text: A deficiency with the entropy standard deviation metrics, is that they decrease as the data length increases. This is the desired behaviour for random uniform data, however it is not necessarily desirable for plaintext data, since this means that the metric can not be considered incentive compatible: it will then pay off for an adversary to match as large plaintext data packets as possible, since this in effect reduces the measured information leakage. An obvious way to mitigate this problem might be to multiply the entropy values with the length $n_{i}=\left|y_{i}\right|$ of the IDS alarm, i.e. $n_{i}H_{\alpha}(y_{i})$ , and then take the standard deviation of the length corrected entropy values. This correction will however be too strong, since the expected bias for random uniform data of length $n_{i}$ then would be constant: $\sigma_{1}^{bias}\approx\frac{\gamma_{1}n_{i}}{n_{i}}=\gamma_{1}$ . This means that the metric would not converge to zero for encrypted traffic.

suspicious_text: We present the first classification of foreground (FG) clusters based on the morphology of their horizontal branches (HBs), utilizing photometric data from 106 clusters as recorded in published catalogues. Clusters are considered FG candidates if their HB can be explained by the evolution of a nearly singular mass. Our analysis reveals that fewer than 20% of clusters with [Fe/H] $<$ –0.8 qualify as FG, while approximately 10% likely possessed sufficient mass to form a second generation (SG). This small proportion supports the spectroscopic finding that the SG is the dominant component of contemporary clusters, indicating that its formation is a critical factor in the survival of globular clusters during their dynamical evolution within the Galactic tidal field.
source_text: We attempt a first classification of FG clusters, based on the morphology of their horizontal branches (HBs), as displayed in the published catalogues of photometric data for 106 clusters. We select, as FG candidates, the clusters in which the HB can be reproduced by the evolution of an almost unique mass. We find that less than 20% of clusters with [Fe/H] $<$ –0.8 appear to be FG, but only $\sim$ 10% probably had a mass sufficient to form at all an SG. This small percentage confirms on a wider database the spectroscopic result that the SG is a dominant constituent of today’s clusters, suggesting that its formation is an ingredient necessary for the survival of globular clusters during their dynamical evolution in the Galactic tidal field.

source_text: Since the purpose of the diagnosis model is not to detect the presence of any number of coughs but to categorize cough events according to their subtle differences, the input audio files for this model are much shorter in length. Hence, we adopt a different data preparation process for making it suitable for training. The sound files are first cut to a single cough event with the longest of these files lasting two seconds, the rest are buffered to the same length with noiseless audio to maintain a fixed two second length for all samples. This fixed length is set for the spectrogram conversion process that comes afterwards and all the two-second audio files are converted into $432\times 288\times 1$ Mel-spectrograms images. Finally, all images in the cough diagnosis dataset are then converted to gray-scale to unify its intensity scaling and reduce the size of each sample to facilitate the model training process.

source_text: Kleene lattices were introduced by J. A. Kalman ( [7] ) (under a different name) as a special kind of De Morgan lattices which serve as an algebraic axiomatization of a certain propositional logic satisfying the double negation law but not necessary the excluded middle law. If the underlying lattice is not distributive such lattices are called pseudo-Kleene (see e.g. [4] ). It is a question if certain binary operations can be introduced in a Kleene or pseudo-Kleene lattice such that they form an adjoint pair. To solve this problem, we apply an approach using the full twist-product construction and another construction extending a distributive lattice into a Kleene one.

source_text: After this first screening, we are left with 86 CM diagrams, among which FG clusters must be identified. Only one cluster is left with a red HB (NGC 6652). For the others, whose HB is blue, we select those in which the extension in V magnitude of the HB does not exceed 1 mag. We further exclude the clusters (with a short blue HB) for which the O–Na anticorrelation has been observed, namely: M30, NGC 6397 Carretta et al. (2009) and NGC 7492 Cohen & Melendez (2005) . When available, we checked the results on more than one CM diagram. These checks allowed to eliminate clusters that in a first moment looked like FG. A clear example is given by NGC 6535, that looks like hosting a blue HB of about 1 visual magnitude range in the data by Testa et al. (2001) . suspicious_text: Conventional transformer architectures effectively model global relationships between distant vectors through attention score computation, but often struggle to capture local patterns within adjacent regions, which are critical in various time series applications. In such cases, proximal segments exhibit stronger correlations than the entire input sequence. Furthermore, processing long sequences results in substantial computational overhead due to the large attention matrix, particularly in deeper network layers. To address this, the ChunkFormer model partitions long sequences into smaller chunks, enabling the capture of local relationships and patterns while maintaining computational efficiency by applying attention layers within limited sequence sections, thus facilitating the handling of lengthy sequences with multiple features.
source_text: A traditional transformer successfully captures the global relationships between distant vectors by computing attention score for all vector pairs. However, it often falls short on capturing patterns preserved within neighboring regions that are sometimes crucial to many applications in time series data. For such data, small segments in a sequence correlate more than when considering the entire input data. Moreover, for long sequence data, the attention matrix will consume more resources to maintain a large matrix, especially during the training of deep layers of the network. ChunkFormer model splits long sequence data to smaller chunks to capture these local relationships and patterns. As the attention layers are applied only within smaller sections of sequences, this approach ensures computational efficiently while working on long sequence lengths with many features.

 and given $\xi\in E$ , let $a_{\xi}$ be the unique element of $s\cap T_{\xi}$ . Define also 
 
 $F\;=\;\{{\eta\in E}\;|\;\,{\min a_{\xi}<\min a_{\eta}\mbox{
whenever }\xi<\eta}\}$ 
 and 
 
 $G\;=\;\{{\eta\in E}\;|\;\,{\max a_{\xi}>\max a_{\eta}\mbox{
whenever }\xi<\eta}\}.$ 
 It is clear that $\{{\min a}\;|\;\,{a\in s}\}$ and $F$ share the same order type, and likewise $\{{\max a}\;|\;\,{a\in s}\}$ has converse order type equal to that of $G$ . Moreover, $E=F\cup G$ . Indeed, if there exist $\xi,\xi^{\prime}<\eta$ such that $\min a_{\xi}=\min a_{\eta}$ and $\max a_{\xi^{\prime}}=\max a_{\eta}$ then, assuming as we can that $\xi\geq\xi^{\prime}$ , we have $a_{\xi}=a_{\eta}$ , which is not allowed in an admissible partition tree. The cardinality assertion follows immediately. ∎

suspicious_text: The primary distinction between our approach and that of Hara and Slade in [27] lies in the structure of the loops generating perturbative terms. In our method, these loops are required to contain a minimum of 4 bonds, whereas in the classical lace expansion, loops could consist of immediate reversals (just 2 bonds). This modification significantly reduces the perturbation, enabling a more model-independent analysis compared to [27] and its percolation adaptations. Consequently, our technique is effective for lattice trees and lattice animals, models that had not been previously addressed by Hara and Slade. During discussions with Takashi Hara, it was observed that our bounds on certain diagrams, such as the triangle diagram, are marginally tighter in dimension 15. However, Hara's analysis of lace-expansion coefficients is more intricate and model-specific.
source_text: The main difference between our work and the work by Hara and Slade in [27] is that in our method, the loops creating the perturbative terms are made to consist of at least 4 bonds, while in the classical lace expansion they could consist of immediate reversals (2 bonds). This makes the perturbation considerably smaller, and allows for an analysis that is to a much larger extent model-independent as the analysis in [27] and its adaptation to percolation. It also explains why our method gives reasonable results for lattice trees and lattice animals, models that previously had not been attempted by Hara and Slade. In discussions with Takashi Hara, we have found that our bounds on e.g. the triangle diagram are slightly better in dimension $15$ , whereas he has a much more sophisticated and model-dependent analysis of the lace-expansion coefficients.

suspicious_text: The training dataset for the cough-detection CNN consists of diverse audio clips sourced from publicly accessible online archives [9]. These recordings, each initially containing at least one cough instance, have been edited to a uniform duration of five seconds, with the cough event occurring at an unspecified position within this timeframe. If a recording contained multiple coughs spanning beyond five seconds, it was divided into separate segments. The number of coughs per segment remains intentionally inconsistent. To achieve balance, the dataset is complemented with a second category of sounds, primarily comprising environmental and vocal audio from the ESC-50 collection. Additionally, this non-cough segment incorporates sound clips extracted from the original cough recordings that do not include cough events. The resultant dataset comprises a total of 993 cough samples and an equal number of non-cough samples.
source_text: The database used for training the CNN in cough detection is composed of various modified audio clips gathered from open-source online sources [9] . Each of these audio files originally contained at least one cough event and are cropped to a length of five seconds with the full cough contained at some arbitrary point within. Audio that contained coughs in more than a five second period are separated into multiple files. The exact number of coughs per file is left intentionally non-standardized. This database is balanced by a collection of environmental and speech sounds, comprised primarily of audio from the ESC-50 database. In addition to ESC-50, this non-cough half of the database is supplemented by sound clips taken from unused portions of the original cough audio that did not contain cough events. The final database contains $993$ cough samples and $993$ non-cough samples.

In a logical context where $\odot$ represents conjunction and $\Rightarrow$ represents implication, the closure of $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ under these operations enables the derivation of Modus Ponens in a fuzzy framework, thereby facilitating deductive reasoning within this pseudo-Kleene logic, which ensures that the propositional value of a conclusion is at least as high as that of its premises.

source_text: A deficiency with the entropy standard deviation metrics, is that they decrease as the data length increases. This is the desired behaviour for random uniform data, however it is not necessarily desirable for plaintext data, since this means that the metric can not be considered incentive compatible: it will then pay off for an adversary to match as large plaintext data packets as possible, since this in effect reduces the measured information leakage. An obvious way to mitigate this problem might be to multiply the entropy values with the length $n_{i}=\left|y_{i}\right|$ of the IDS alarm, i.e. $n_{i}H_{\alpha}(y_{i})$ , and then take the standard deviation of the length corrected entropy values. This correction will however be too strong, since the expected bias for random uniform data of length $n_{i}$ then would be constant: $\sigma_{1}^{bias}\approx\frac{\gamma_{1}n_{i}}{n_{i}}=\gamma_{1}$ . This means that the metric would not converge to zero for encrypted traffic.

source_text: In the quantum simulator, noise is generated by applying a stochastic signal along the flux lines that are used to tune the excited state energies of the individual qubits. source_text: One of the less intuitive increases of ionic radius takes place while going from \textsuperscript{56} Ba ([Xe] $6s^{1}$ ) to \textsuperscript{57} La ([Xe] $5d^{2}$ ). On one hand, the effective principal quantum number $n^{*}$ decreases which according to ( 30 ) should lower the ionic radius. However as compared to the $6s$ electron of Ba, the effective $Z^{*}$ seen by one of La’s $5d$ electrons also decreases. This happens because the inner shell $5sp$ electrons screen the nuclear charge more effectively if the outer shell electrons are in a $d$ state (La) as compared to an $s$ state (Ba). As a result the decrease in $Z^{*}$ outweighs the decrease in $n^{*}$ thus leading to an increase of the ion size. Optimal spectral functions for coherence generation would exhibit rapid decay at low frequencies and significant amplitudes at higher energy gaps. However, strong coupling can suppress coherent dynamics due to environmental reorganisation energy, which detunes and localises low-lying excitations. Furthermore, the application of site-specific noises in current simulators offers opportunities for exploring the impact of spatial and spectral noise correlations on coherent dynamics in quantum energy transfer networks, potentially building on insights from Redfield theory and site-local noise models.

This bias is defined by considering each variable $x_i$ in $\Psi$, where $\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi)$ represents the difference in total weight between constraints where $x_i$ appears positively and negatively. The bias is then computed as $\textsf{bias}(\Psi)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mathrm{\small def}}}{{=}}\frac{1}{km}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi)|\in[0,1]$. A note clarifies that in [GVV17], normalization by $\frac{1}{kW}$ was not performed. In the context of sketching, the bias can be efficiently estimated using standard $\ell_{1}$-norm sketching algorithms [Ind06].

suspicious_text: In a comparable scenario, the short-range induced magnetic field encounters a balance of forces. When the radial magnetic fields of the electron and the nucleus, proportional to their respective charges, are in harmony, the angular momentum of the field is nullified due to mutual cancellation. It is important to note that, while the nucleus can be considered as a point-like entity for this discussion, it is not truly so. Instead, the electron-quark system should be the focus of analysis. It is crucial to emphasize that, in the absence of this cancellation, a predicament would arise: the magnetic field region's dimensions are minuscule (approximately $m^{-1}$ ), thereby impeding the fulfilment of the quantization condition for nonzero $K$. This observation supports the notion of the radial magnetic fields being proportionate to the electric charges.
source_text: A similar situation arises for the short-range induced magnetic field. If both the radial magnetic fields of the electron and the point-like nucleus are proportional to their charges then the angular momentum of the field will be zero due to a cancelation between the two contributions. (Actually, the nucleus is not point-like, therefore, strictly speaking, we should consider an electron-quark system here.) We stress that in the absence of such a cancelation there would be a problem: the size of the magnetic field region is very small ( $\sim m^{-1}$ ), and so we cannot satisfy the condition of angular momentum quantization for nonzero $K$ (this provides an argument for the proportionality of the radial magnetic fields to the electric charges).

suspicious_text: Approximately 75 years ago, J.C. Slater introduced a broadly applicable, empirical framework for approximating atomic wave functions analytically, which remains useful today for elements across all ionization stages. This approach involves selecting the radial component of single-electron wave functions in a specific form, akin to the asymptotic behavior of hydrogen-like wave functions at large distances, characterized by an effective quantum number and nuclear charge. By leveraging the underlying electronic structure, Slater's rules provide a systematic method for assigning these effective parameters to electrons in each atomic or ionic shell, thereby facilitating the construction of a comprehensive set of single-electron wave functions.
source_text:
Seventy five years ago J. C. Slater (42) gave a very useful, general, empirical set of rules to approximate analytically atomic wave functions for all the elements in any stage of ionization. The radial part of the single-electron wave function is selected in the form 
 
 $\psi(r)\propto r^{n^{*}-1}e^{-Z^{*}r/n^{*}a_{B}}$  (29) 
 which can be recognized as the large-distance asympotics of a Hydrogen-like wave function with an effective quantum number $n^{*}$ and an effective nuclear charge $Z^{*}e$ . Based on the underlying electronic structure, Slater’s rules assign values of $n^{*}$ and $Z^{*}$ to the electrons of each shell of an atom or ion, so that a complete set of single-electron wave functions can be constructed.

source_text: The nuclear symmetry energy, especially at suprasaturation densities, plays crucial roles in many astrophysical studies. However, nowadays the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy is still very controversial in nuclear community. To constrain the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy, neutron-rich nuclei collisions at medium energies are considered to be one of the most effective methods. While probing the high-density symmetry energy by using heavy-ion collisions, blind spots may exist. In the framework of the Isospin-dependent Boltzmann-Uehling-Uhlenbeck (IBUU) transport model, the blind spots of probing the high-density symmetry energy by the n/p ratio in the central Au+Au reaction at 300 MeV/nucleon are demonstrated. It is found that the nucleon observable neutron to proton ratio n/p in heavy-ion collisions cannot effectively probe the high-density symmetry energy when the high-density symmetry energy is less density-dependent.

suspicious_text: The study of the adjoint linear system $|K_{X}+L|$ on a smooth projective complex surface $X$ with divisor $L$ is a fundamental problem, with the behavior of this system largely dependent on the positivity of $L$. Established methods, such as Reider's approach for positive $L$ and Beauville's work for $L=0$, provide a framework for understanding the system under certain conditions. However, for negative $L$, the system is associated with special divisors, which are effective divisors $D$ satisfying $h^{0}(K_{X}-D)>0$, and a general methodology for analyzing these divisors on surfaces remains elusive. To develop a comprehensive approach for studying special linear systems on surfaces, it is essential to first establish a Clifford-type theorem that can provide insight into the properties of these systems.
source_text: Let $X$ be a smooth projective complex surface and $L$ a divisor on it. One of the fundamental problems in the surface case is to study the adjoint linear system $|K_{X}+L|$ . Roughly speaking, the behavior of this linear system depends on the positivity of $L$ . When $L$ is positive, we have a celebrated method of Reider [15] (see also [4] and [16] ). When $L$ is zero, the canonical system has also been studied systematically by Beauville [2] . When $L$ is negative, the linear system corresponds to the special divisors on a curve. Exactly, we say a divisor $D$ on $X$ a special divisor if it is effective and $h^{0}(K_{X}-D)>0$ . However, for surfaces, we have no general method to study such special divisors. In order to find a powerful method to study special linear systems in the surface case, we need to establish first a Clifford-type theorem.

suspicious_text: We propose a modified Transformer architecture comprising a series of compact transformer blocks arranged in a multi-stage configuration, enabling the capture of both short-term and long-term dependencies among input vectors at various time points. This design leverages a chunk-based approach to identify local patterns within proximal regions, while the multi-stage framework progressively integrates global information from more distant vectors, ultimately yielding a computationally efficient model suitable for handling high-dimensional data with extensive sequence lengths.
source_text: We revamp the Traditional Transformer model to a series of small transformer blocks with repeating stages to capture the short-term and long-term dependencies among input vectors at different time stamps. The chunk style architecture captures the local patterns hidden within neighboring regions while the Multi-Stage architecture gradually captures the global information within distant vectors. As the attention mechanism is calculated for small chunks of sequence, the overall model is computationally efficient for data with large number of features and sequence lengths.

Two vertices \(x\) and \(y\) are considered connected, denoted by \(x \longleftrightarrow y\), if there exists a path of open bonds linking them or if \(x = y\). The random set of vertices connected to \(x\) is denoted by \(\mathscr{C}(x)\), with its cardinality represented as \(|\mathscr{C}(x)|\). The two-point function \(\tau_p(x)\) is defined as the probability that \(0\) and \(x\) are connected, expressed as:

\[
\tau_p(x) = \mathbb{P}_p(0 \longleftrightarrow x). \quad (1.38)
\]

By translation invariance, \(\mathbb{P}_p(x \longleftrightarrow y) = \tau_p(x - y)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathbb{Z}^d\). The percolation susceptibility, or expected cluster size, is given by:

\[
\chi(p) = \sum_{x \in \mathbb{Z}^d} \tau_p(x) = \mathbb{E}_p\left[|\mathscr{C}(0)|\right]. \quad (1.39)
\]

Percolation occurs when there exists a cluster \(\mathscr{C}(x)\) with \(|\mathscr{C}(x)| = \infty\). The function \(\theta(p)\) represents the probability that the origin is part of an infinite cluster:

source_text: It can furthermore be observed that samples of encrypted user traffic, assuming that strong encryption is used, in itself does not leak any private or confidential information, hence can be expected to have low entropy variance. Ordinary non-encrypted user traffic, can however be expected to show a significant variance in entropy between different samples, as illustrated in Figure V.2 . This indicates that entropy variance may be an interesting metric for measuring whether IDS alarms leak information, in particular for buffer overflow type of attacks. However this metric does obviously not understand the semantics of the data traffic, and can therefore not be used to evaluate whether the leaked information is private or confidential.

suspicious_text: The primary distinction between our approach and that of Hara and Slade in [27] lies in the structure of the loops generating perturbative terms. In our method, these loops are required to contain a minimum of 4 bonds, whereas in the classical lace expansion, loops could consist of immediate reversals (just 2 bonds). This modification significantly reduces the perturbation, enabling a more model-independent analysis compared to [27] and its percolation adaptations. Consequently, our technique is effective for lattice trees and lattice animals, models that had not been previously addressed by Hara and Slade. During discussions with Takashi Hara, it was observed that our bounds on certain diagrams, such as the triangle diagram, are marginally tighter in dimension 15. However, Hara's analysis of lace-expansion coefficients is more intricate and model-specific.

suspicious_text: From our analysis, 9 clusters (~10%) in our sample show evidence of having potentially developed a stellar globular (SG) during their initial evolution. This observed fraction aligns with, and builds upon, the spectroscopic findings of Carretta et al. (2009), who reported a dominant SG presence (>50%) in their full sample of 19 clusters. It appears that only a small number of clusters capable of SG formation retain a significant portion of their initial mass, or conversely, that few clusters without SG formation survive the dynamical interactions with the Galactic tidal field. The development of a centrally concentrated SG system emerges as a critical factor for cluster survival during the early stages of cluster evolution, as previously highlighted by D’Antona & Ventura (2008).
source_text: In our list, we are left with 9 clusters ( $\sim$ 10%) that may have initially developed an SG. This small fraction is consistent with, and extends, the spectroscopic result by Carretta et al. (2009) , who find in their whole 19 clusters sample a predominant ( $>$ 50%) SG. We must conclude that very few of the clusters that may develop the SG do not lose a high fraction of their initial mass, or even that only a few clusters that do not form a SG survive to the dynamical interaction with the Galactic tidal field. The formation of a central compact SG system appears to be a key ingredient for the survival of a cluster to the first phases of cluster evolution D’Antona & Ventura (2008) .

Consequently, we establish that $p_{g}(X) = 5$, and furthermore, $K_{X}^{2} = 7 < 2p_{g}(X) = 10$. Given that the condition $K_{X}^{2} \geq 2p_{g}(X)$ is associated with irregular surfaces, as discussed in reference [7], we infer that the irregularity $q(X)$ of the surface must be $0$.

Chechkin et al. established the complete solution for the propagator of bulk-mediated diffusion (42). When $D_{s}=0$ and long-distance corrections are disregarded, the displacement distribution aligns with the Cauchy propagator, consistent with scaling arguments (40), expressed as $P(\textbf{r})=\frac{\gamma t}{2\pi[r^{2}+\left(\gamma t\right)^{2}]^{3/2}}$. If surface diffusion occurs ($D_{s}\neq 0$), the displacement probability density becomes the convolution of equation (5) with a normal distribution. Although the full long-time solution is intricate, the distribution's tail for large distances scales as $P(\textbf{r})\sim r^{-3}$. This asymptotic behavior implies that the exact distribution shares key properties with the Cauchy distribution.

Molecules perform a two-dimensional random walk, but at random times they jump due to a hypothetical bulk excursion. The surface residence times are assumed to be independent and identically distributed exponential random variables and the jumps are modelled according to the first return time to the surface given simple diffusion in a three-dimensional medium. These simulations are analysed in the same way as with experimental observations of the motion of membrane-targeting C2 domains on supported membranes. 500 realizations were simulated off-lattice with a surface diffusion coefficient $D_{s}=0.5$ and a dissociation coefficient $k=0.1$ . suspicious_text: In an integral commutative residuated lattice, several key properties hold for all elements. Specifically, the lattice exhibits monotonicity with respect to the monoid operation, as well as compatibility with the partial order. Additionally, the identity element behaves as a neutral element with respect to the implication operation, and the implication operation itself is closely tied to the partial order. The lattice also satisfies various distributivity and associativity properties, including the exchange of implication with meet and the Curry-Howard correspondence between the monoid operation and implication. Furthermore, the implication operation is antitone in its first argument and monotone in its second argument, which provides a foundation for further algebraic manipulations and reasoning within the lattice.

source_text: The phenomenon of Feshbach resonances in ultra cold atomic gases has attracted much attention. The Zeeman-field-driven two-body resonances between fermion pairs in open channels and bound molecules provide a fascinating way to tune the scattering length between atoms in open channels. Remarkably, this sort of simple two-body physics results in extremely rich quantum many-body states in atomic vapors which have not been explicitly observed in conventional solid state systems. Indeed, by varying the two-body scattering length near Feshbach resonances, several groups have successfully achieved fermionic superfluids in a strongly interacting regime (1) .

Let $v$ be defined as in Definition 2, and let $G$ be constructed via equation (22), where $u(\mathbf{x})$ is taken to be the Dirac delta function $\delta(\mathbf{x})$. This leads to the expression 

$$
v(\mathbf{x},t) = (G(\cdot,t) *_{\mathbf{x}} u)(\mathbf{x}), \quad t > 0, \, \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^N,
$$

which demonstrates that $G$ serves as the Green's function for a diffusion process with a constant propagation speed $c$. Specifically, $v$ satisfies the integral equation

$$
v(\mathbf{x},t) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^N} G(\mathbf{x}^{\prime},\tau_{n(t)}) \, v(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{\prime},t-\tau_{n(t)}) \, {\rm d}\mathbf{x}^{\prime}, \quad t > 0, \, \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^N,
$$

with the initial condition $v(\mathbf{x},0) = u(\mathbf{x})$. This formulation is a specialized adaptation of the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation, and since $G(\cdot,t)$ is a positive distribution, it induces a measure $\mu_t(\mathbf{x}) = G(\mathbf{x},t) \, {\rm d}\mathbf{x}$ on $\mathbb{R}^N$.

suspicious_text: The optimal $(\frac{4}{9}-\epsilon)$-approximation algorithm for $2$ AND [CGV20], similar to Guruswami, Velingker, and Velusamy's earlier $(\frac{2}{5}-\epsilon)$-approximation [GVV17], relies on calculating a measure called the bias of an instance $\Psi$, denoted as $\textsf{bias}(\Psi)$. This bias is defined by considering each variable $x_i$ in $\Psi$, where $\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi)$ represents the difference in total weight between constraints where $x_i$ appears positively and negatively. The bias is then computed as $\textsf{bias}(\Psi)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mathrm{\small def}}}{{=}}\frac{1}{km}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi)|\in[0,1]$. A note clarifies that in [GVV17], normalization by $\frac{1}{kW}$ was not performed. In the context of sketching, the bias can be efficiently estimated using standard $\ell_{1}$-norm sketching algorithms [Ind06].

suspicious_text: The application of time series analysis has accelerated in various industries, including telecommunications, advertising, online education, and sales, to uncover the underlying drivers of trends and patterns that emerge over time. Techniques rooted in deep learning, particularly those leveraging Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), have become the standard approach for analyzing sequential data. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) models, which are variants of RNNs, are commonly employed to identify dependencies within historical sequences and forecast the probability of future events. Nevertheless, traditional RNNs pose training challenges due to the issues of vanishing and exploding gradients, which can render the model unstable and hinder its ability to capture long-range relationships between input elements in extended sequential data.
source_text: Time series analysis has gained a lot of momentum in real-world use cases like telecommunication, advertisement click analysis, online education, and sales & retail to study the underlying causes of trends or systemic patterns captured over time. Deep learning techniques such as Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) based models are widely accepted as state-of-the-art approaches while dealing with sequential data. RNN based models such as LSTM and GRU are applied to extract the dependencies from the historical sequences to predict the likelihood of future events. However, regular RNNs are hard to train due to vanishing and exploding gradient problems making the model unstable and unable to learn global relationships between input items from long sequential data.

Still, our method enables learning despite using such sub-optimal controllers. source_text: To improve sample complexity, model-based controllers can be used with model-free reinforcement learning. The natural approach to leverage model-based controllers is to use them as low-level policies in hierarchical reinforcement learning. [5] derives model-based controllers for reposing, sliding, and flipping, and then learn a hierarchical policy. However, they demonstrate it only for 2D in-hand manipulation. [16] used a low-level controller to maintain grasp stability based on tactile feedback but only evaluated for in-grasp manipulation. These approaches all share a common limitation – the low-level controllers are necessary not only during training but also during deployment. This restricts the feedback used by low-level controllers to the sensing modalities available on the hand while simultaneously requiring the controller to be robust to the sim-to-real gap.

The angular wave function for the system is the Wigner $D$ -function, $D^{J}_{MK}(\bf{n})$ , where $M$ is the projection of ${\bf J}$ onto the z-axis (compare e.g. with ref. The orbital contribution to the EDM in such an angular state is proportional to the angular integral $\int|D^{J}_{MK}({\bf n})|^{2}{\bf n}_{z}\,d\Omega=KM/[J(J+1)]$ . This relation is especially simple for $K=0$ since the $D$ -function in this case coincides with the usual $Y_{LM}$ angular function which gives an (orbital) EDM of zero. Thus, we can consider the zero value of the electromagnetic field angular momentum in the electron-nucleus case to be an argument for the absence of an orbital contribution to the electric dipole moment.

By leveraging the foundational results established in Theorems 0.2 and 0.1, it becomes feasible to extend this notion to smooth minimal surfaces of general type, denoted as X, thereby yielding two distinct indices that adhere to the principles of Clifford type invariants.

suspicious_text: The ChunkFormer model builds upon the strengths of the Transformer architecture, integrating CNN-style structures to process lengthy sequence vectors, thereby leveraging the benefits of both local and global attention mechanisms. Unlike traditional Transformers, which solely focus on global attention and neglect local interactions, ChunkFormer calculates attention matrices for subdivided input sequences, enhancing efficiency without sacrificing accuracy. This approach enables the model to effectively capture long-term dependencies, outperforming LSTM-like models on lengthy sequences. By striking a balance between accuracy and operational efficiency, ChunkFormer achieves superior performance while consuming fewer resources than baseline models, making it a practical solution for real-world applications, although random block chunk size selection may occasionally impact accuracy.

suspicious_text: Let us define the function \( f(u) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{u^n}{n!} \) for \( |u| < 1 \). This function resembles the exponential generating function for a Poisson distribution. A key property of \( f(u) \) is that it satisfies the differential equation \( f'(u) = f(u) \), which can be verified by differentiating the series term by term. Additionally, \( f(0) = 1 \) since all terms except the constant term vanish when \( u = 0 \). This function plays a central role in many areas of probability theory, including percolation models where it is used to describe the probability generating function for branching processes. Understanding the behavior of \( f(u) \) is essential for analyzing the asymptotic properties of such models.
source_text: Percolation is a central model in statistical physics and is a very active field of research since its rigorous definition by Broadbent and Hammersley in 1957 [10] , where this model was proposed to describe the spread of a fluid through a medium. General references for percolation are [6] . A review of recent results can be found in [21] and the references therein. We consider Bernoulli percolation on the hypercubic lattice. We use the definition of [43] : To each nearest-neighbor bond $\{x,y\}$ we associate an independent Bernoulli random variable $n_{\{x,y\}}$ which takes the value $1$ with probability $p$ and the value $0$ with probability $1-p$ , where $p\in[0,1]$ . If $n_{\{x,y\}}=1$ , then we say that the bond $\{x,y\}$ is open , and otherwise we say that it is closed . A configuration is a realization of the random variables of all bonds. The joint probability distribution is denoted by $\prob_{p}$ with corresponding expectation $\expec_{p}$ .

Here, the input instance $\Psi$ is guaranteed to either have $\textsf{val}_{\Psi} \leq \beta$ or $\textsf{val}_{\Psi} \geq \gamma$, and the objective is to determine which condition holds with probability at least $\frac{2}{3}$.

source_text: Glass coverslips were cleaned by sonication in a detergent solution followed by soaking in 1M KOH. The coverslips were rinsed extensively in Milli-Q water and blown dry with a stream of nitrogen gas. Then, the coverslips were treated with an oxygen plasma. Immediately after the plasma cleaning, a perfusion chamber (CoverWell, Grace Bio-Labs) was adhered to the coverslip. In order to deposit the lipid bilayers a solution of SUVs (1.5-mM lipid) composed of phosphatidylcholine (PC) and phosphatidylserine (PS) at a 3:1 ratio in 1M NaCl and imaging buffer was introduced into the perfusion chamber and incubated for one hour at 4 \textsuperscript{∘} C. Refrigeration minimizes lipid oxidation. The surface was then rinsed with imaging buffer multiple times prior to addition of protein sample.

source_text: Percolation is a central model in statistical physics and is a very active field of research since its rigorous definition by Broadbent and Hammersley in 1957 [10] , where this model was proposed to describe the spread of a fluid through a medium. General references for percolation are [6] . A review of recent results can be found in [21] and the references therein. We consider Bernoulli percolation on the hypercubic lattice. We use the definition of [43] : To each nearest-neighbor bond $\{x,y\}$ we associate an independent Bernoulli random variable $n_{\{x,y\}}$ which takes the value $1$ with probability $p$ and the value $0$ with probability $1-p$ , where $p\in[0,1]$ . If $n_{\{x,y\}}=1$ , then we say that the bond $\{x,y\}$ is open , and otherwise we say that it is closed . A configuration is a realization of the random variables of all bonds. The joint probability distribution is denoted by $\prob_{p}$ with corresponding expectation $\expec_{p}$ .

This paper focuses on constructing and examining a causal model of diffusion. The study is divided into two main sections. In the first part, it is demonstrated that causal diffusion cannot adhere to the strongly continuous semigroup property or its corresponding evolution equation. Specifically, the paper shows that a process governed by the scalar-valued evolution equation

\[
\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} - \mathcal{A}v = 0 \quad \text{for } t > 0, \quad \text{with} \quad v|_{t=0} = u,
\]

where the space convolution operator \(\mathcal{A}\) serves as the infinitesimal generator of a strongly continuous semigroup (as referenced in [2]), fails to satisfy causality. This means there exists a fundamental solution \(G: \mathbb{R}^{N} \times \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) for the equation above that does not meet the condition

\[
\text{supp}(G) \subseteq \{(\mathbf{x}, t) \in \mathbb{R}^{N} \times [0, \infty) \, | \, |\mathbf{x}| \leq c_{0} t \},
\]

where \(c_{0}\) is a positive constant.

The critical exponents for percolation have received considerable attention in the literature. For the critical exponents, it is known that $\beta\leq 1$ and $\gamma\geq 1$ for all $d\geq 2$ , see Chayes and Chayes [13] and Aizenman and Newman [3] . Further, we know that if $\beta$ and $\gamma$ exists, then $\beta\in(0,1]$ and $\gamma\in[1,\infty)$ , see [20] . In high dimensions, we expect mean-field behavior for percolation. Namely, we expect that for all dimensions $d>6$ , the critical exponents correspond to the exponents of the regular tree given by $\gamma=\beta=1$ , and $\eta=0$ . (For the definition of $\eta$ for percolation on trees, see Grimmett [20] .) Alternatively, we can interpret $\gamma=\beta=1$ , and $\eta=0$ as the critical exponents for branching random walk, see the discussion in [31] . In the context of reinforcement learning, a stochastic operator is defined to update parameters iteratively, based on the interactions between an agent and its environment, which follows a specific policy. This operator, denoted as $\tilde{F}^{\pi}$, relies on the current state, action, next state, and next action, all of which are tied to the policy $\pi$. The update rule incorporates several key components, including a learning rate and a parameter that dictates how frequently updates occur, allowing for flexibility in the learning process. Notably, when the update frequency parameter is set to zero, this method simplifies to a traditional temporal difference learning approach.

suspicious_text: Inflationary theories often simplify the process by assuming a single scalar field governs the inflationary dynamics. Similarly, the mechanism responsible for converting inflationary energy into radiation is also typically modeled with a single field. However, this analysis introduces a novel phenomenon that could emerge if we consider multiple fields instead of just one. Specifically, in a multi-field framework where the inflationary potential exhibits broken symmetry, the quantum fluctuations that occur during inflation act as variations in the initial conditions for the inflaton's behavior in the post-inflationary phase. This leads to differences in the background dynamics across various regions of the universe after the slow-roll phase ends. Given that these background fields interact with other fields they eventually decay into, the fluctuations generated during the slow-roll period influence the subsequent decay process.

We then define two functions, $\alpha(X)$ and $\beta(X)$, which are determined by the minimum values of $\alpha(L)$ and $\beta(L)$ respectively, where L ranges over all line bundles in $\mathcal{S}$. If $\mathcal{S}$ is not empty, these functions take on the smallest value of $\alpha(L)$ and $\beta(L)$ for any L in $\mathcal{S}$; if $\mathcal{S}$ is empty, both functions are defined to be infinity.

source_text: To constrain the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy by heavy-ion collisions, one usually varies the density dependence of the symmetry energy in transport model simulations and make comparisons with experimental data. This operation is alright for those symmetry energies which evidently depend on the density. But for the symmetry energy which has a less dependence on the density, the above method is noneffective. Because according to the chemical equilibrium condition of nuclear matter formed in heavy-ion collisions (29) , only if the symmetry energy changes with density, the liquid-gas phase transition can occur. That is to say, if the value of the symmetry energy is less density-dependent (since the high-density is very controversial (4) , all the density dependences of the high-density symmetry energy are possible), a freeze-out observable in heavy-ion collisions cannot prove the information of the symmetry energy effectively.

suspicious_text: The hypothesis that M 53 is predominantly a field galaxy (FG) cluster has been supported by several arguments. Accepting this premise and assuming that its chemical and dynamical evolution occurred within its tidal radius, a compelling avenue of research emerges. Specifically, it would be possible to estimate the proportion of second-generation stars produced by this evolution, following an assessment of stellar losses resulting from two-body interactions over the cluster's Galactic lifetime. source_text: A Boolean maximum constraint satisfaction problem, $\textsf{Max-CSP}(f)$ , is specified by a predicate $f:\{-1,1\}^{k}\to\{0,1\}$ . An $n$ -variable instance of $\textsf{Max-CSP}(f)$ consists of a list of constraints, each of which applies $f$ to $k$ distinct literals drawn from the $n$ variables. For $k=2$ , Chou, Golovnev, and Velusamy [CGV20] obtained explicit ratios characterizing the $\sqrt{n}$ -space streaming approximability of every predicate. For $k\geq 3$ , Chou, Golovnev, Sudan, and Velusamy [CGSV21] proved a general dichotomy theorem for $\sqrt{n}$ -space sketching algorithms: For every $f$ , there exists $\alpha(f)\in(0,1]$ such that for every $\epsilon>0$ , $\textsf{Max-CSP}(f)$ is $(\alpha(f)-\epsilon)$ -approximable by an $O(\log n)$ -space linear sketching algorithm, but $(\alpha(f)+\epsilon)$ -approximation sketching algorithms require $\Omega(\sqrt{n})$ space.

suspicious_text: The chemical composition was analyzed by Koch et al. (2009), who secured high-resolution spectra for four red giants and determined the abundances of 25 elements, including α-, iron-peak, and neutron-capture elements. While the sample size is small, some findings are robust: the α-enhancement aligns with values observed in halo field stars and typical globular clusters (GCs) like M 13, as do the Fe-peak and neutron-capture element ratios. Furthermore, Koch et al. (2010) found that the neutron-capture elements in Pal 3 originate solely from the r-process, similar to very metal-poor field stars (Honda et al. 2007) and the GC M 15 (Sneden et al. 2000). This suggests that the neutron-capture patterns in Pal 3 can be explained by enrichment from core-collapse supernovae (SN II) alone. Additionally, no clear Na-O anticorrelation is evident in the data, although a weak correlation cannot be entirely excluded.
source_text: The chemical composition has been investigated by Koch et al. (2009) ; they obtained high resolution spectra for four red giants and determined the abundances for 25 elements ( $\alpha$ –, iron–peak, neutron–capture elements). The sample is limited, but a few results appear relatively safe: the $\alpha$ –enhancement is compatible with that found in halo field stars and typical GCs as M 13; so are the Fe–peak and neutron–capture elements ratios. In addition, Koch et al. (2010) find that the n–capture elements appear to derive from the r –process only, as observed only in the very metal poor field stars Honda et al. (2007) and in the GC M 15 Sneden et al. (2000) . Then, the n–capture patterns in Pal 3 do not require enrichment processes other than occurring in SN II explosions. Besides, a Na–O anti–correlation is not evident, although a weak one cannot be ruled out by Koch et al’s data.

Observations indicate that young clusters in nearby galaxies initially exhibit compact configurations, with a half-mass radius \( r_{\text{h}} < 1 \, \text{pc} \) (Lada & Lada 2003), which subsequently expand due to mass loss from stellar winds and supernovae of type II within the first 30 Myr (Bastian et al. 2008). This expansion is further amplified if the cluster is initially mass segregated (Vesperini et al. 2009). Should the SG form within a cooling flow, it would result in an even more condensed stellar distribution (D’Ercole et al. 2008), characterized by a smaller \( r_{\text{h}} \). Conversely, in the absence of SG formation, the cluster retains the larger \( r_{\text{h}} \) acquired during the initial expansion phase, potentially becoming tidally filling (Baumgardt et al. 2010) as a consequence of its lack of SG development. source_text: In ref. [13] it was pointed out that an electric dipole moment (EDM) of an atom (or any quantum system) can be induced by the interaction of the electrons with a radial magnetic field ( ${\bf B}\propto{\bf r}$ ). The existence of such a field would contradict both time reflection invariance (T) and Gauss’s Law ( $\oint{\bf B}\cdot d{\bf a}=0$ ). The limit on such a field can be of interest by itself as a very precise test of electrodynamics at small distances since the accuracy of EDM measurements is very high now. It was also pointed out in [13] that a radial magnetic field can be produced due to a monopole-antimonopole pair contribution to the magnetic moment (if there is no magnetic string) and a time invariance violating interaction which polarizes nucleon spins along the radial direction.
