Furthermore, assume that the set $P_a(\mathbf{L})$ exhibits a total order with respect to the pair $(a, a)$, in the sense that every element of $P_a(\mathbf{L})$ is comparable to $(a, a)$. Under these conditions, the structure $(P_a(\mathbf{L}), \sqcup, \sqcap, {}^\prime)$, where the operation ${}^\prime$ is defined as $(x, y)^\prime = (y, x)$ for all $(x, y) \in P_a(\mathbf{L})$, forms a Kleene lattice. Moreover, the operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ constitute an adjoint pair if and only if certain specific conditions, denoted as (3) and (4), are satisfied, thereby establishing a fundamental relationship between these operations within the lattice.

source_text: We evaluated our method with the challenging dexterous manipulation task of learning finger-gaiting in-hand manipulation with only fingertip grasps. The observed distribution is characterized by two distinct regions: a central peak extending up to approximately \( r \approx 1.5\ \mu\textrm{m} \), followed by a pronounced long-range tail. This behavior can be rationalized through the principles of bulk-mediated diffusion, as outlined by Bychuk and O’Shaughnessy (40). In the long-time limit, the distribution of first return times follows a power-law scaling, \( \psi(\tau) \sim \tau^{-1.5} \). source_text: The distribution of displacements $P(\textbf{r})$ at $\Delta=100\ \mathrm{ms}$ is shown in Figs. 3 d and e for C2A and GST-C2A, respectively. The distribution exhibits two different characteristic regimes: a central part up to a distance $r\approx 1.5\ \mu\textrm{m}$ and a long tail. This behaviour can be understood from the scaling properties of bulk-mediated diffusion as discussed by Bychuk and O’Shaughnessy (40) . Once a molecule dissociates from the surface, it performs a three-dimensional random walk until it returns. In the asymptotic limit, the first return time distribution scales as $\psi(\tau)\sim\tau^{-1.5}$ . For any given return time, the surface distance between the dissociation and return points has a Gaussian distribution $P(\textbf{r}_{j}|\tau)\sim\mathrm{exp}(-r_{j}^{2}/4D_{b}\tau)$ . Therefore, the distribution of jump lengths is $P(\textbf{r}_{j})\sim r_{j}^{-3}$ , as observed in Figs. 3 d and e for long distances.

suspicious_text: In the realm of ultra-cold atomic gases, Feshbach resonances have garnered significant interest. These are two-body resonances, driven by Zeeman fields, between fermionic pairs in open channels and bound molecules. This interaction allows for the tuning of the scattering length between atoms in open channels, leading to a remarkable array of quantum many-body states in atomic vapors. These states are not typically observed in conventional solid-state systems. Notably, by manipulating the two-body scattering length near Feshbach resonances, several research teams have successfully created fermionic superfluids in a strongly interacting regime (1).

source_text: The organization of this paper is as follows. In Section II we provide a short derivation of the Rydberg formula ( 2 ) and arrive at the expression for the quantum defect in terms of the dimensionless range of the inner potential and its scattering length. This general result is further analyzed in the $r_{0}\ll a_{B}$ limit and the main features of the Zel’dovich spectral reconstruction are recovered (Section IIA). In Section IIB we establish a relationship between the Zel’dovich effect and Levinson’s theorem of quantum mechanics. This is followed (Section IIC) by the analysis of the opposite $r_{0}\gg a_{B}$ limit where we demonstrate that the Zel’dovich effect manifests itself in the form of a spectral modulation whose origin still lies in the binding properties of the inner potential $U_{s}(r)$ . suspicious_text: We are addressing the challenge of in-hand manipulation through finger gaiting, with a focus on continuous reorientation around the z-axis within a hand-centric framework. Our approach involves learning a policy that optimizes the angular velocity of an object along the z-axis. Considering a dexterous hand with multiple fingers and degrees of freedom, our policy generates joint target updates based on observations that encompass current and target joint positions, contact positions and normals, and contact force magnitudes. By leveraging a reward structure that incentivizes angular velocity about the z-axis when at least three contacts are established between the object and fingertips, we train the policy to achieve effective in-hand manipulation.

This means that the population dynamics cannot be explained solely by the decay of coherences, but rather by a combination of coherent and incoherent processes. Furthermore, the non-adiabatic effects lead to an enhancement of the population transfer between the sites, resulting in a more efficient energy transport. The implications of these findings are significant, as they demonstrate the importance of considering non-adiabatic effects in the study of open quantum systems, particularly in the context of quantum information processing and energy transfer.

In the literature, different types of audio categorization has been well investigated and among the various machine learning based techniques, CNN based approaches are a popular choice for this task [8] . Using three publicly available datasets, ESC-10, ESC-50, and UrbanSound8K, that contain a large collection of environmental sounds, it is shown that a CNN model can successfully be applied to environmental sound classification [9] . The success of the CNN based approach in categorizing audio into the fifty categories of the ESC-50 set, one of these being cough sounds, suggests that the network model may be capable of doing so with just coughs specifically. Indeed, this approach has been attempted in several other recent works [4] .

suspicious_text: The instant preheating model (14) posits that the inflaton field $\phi$ interacts with the preheat field $\chi$ via the standard preheating coupling, expressed as $\mathcal{L}_{\phi\chi}=-\frac{1}{2}g^{2}|\phi|^{2}\chi^{2}$, and that the preheat field $\chi$ is further coupled to a fermion field $\psi$ through the interaction $\mathcal{L}_{\chi\psi}=h\bar{\psi}\psi\chi$. While the fermion field $\psi$ is assumed to be massless in this model, this assumption is not strictly necessary. The efficiency of the process $\phi\rightarrow\chi\rightarrow\psi$ depends on the coupling constants, enabling a rapid transfer of the energy density initially contained in the inflaton field into fermions within a single half-oscillation of the inflaton around the potential minimum.
source_text: The instant preheating model (14) assumes that the inflaton field $\phi$ is coupled to the preheat field $\chi$ through the standard (and simplest) preheating interaction, $\mathcal{L}_{\phi\chi}=-\frac{1}{2}g^{2}|\phi|^{2}\chi^{2}$ , and that the field $\chi$ is also coupled to a fermion field $\psi$ by the interaction $\mathcal{L}_{\chi\psi}=h\bar{\psi}\psi\chi$ . \footnote{ 7 We suppose the fermion field $\psi$ to be massless, but this assumption is not crucial.} Depending on the value of the coupling constants, the process $\phi\rightarrow\chi\rightarrow\psi$ can be very efficient and turn the energy density initially stored in the background field into fermions in a single half oscillation of the inflaton about the minimum of its potential.

suspicious_text: We analyze two clusters in detail: Pal 3, an example of an FG-only cluster, and M 53, which we consider as a primarily FG cluster. For comparison, we also examine NGC 2419, a cluster that, despite evolving entirely within its Jacobi radius, contains a significant extreme subgiant population, suggesting an unusual dynamical history. The horizontal branch (HB) diagrams for these three clusters are presented in Figure 1, alongside histograms of the color and magnitude distributions of HB stars. Pal 3's HB is notably short, while M 53's HB exhibits a sharply peaked color distribution with a red tail. In contrast, NGC 2419's HB displays a strongly peaked color and magnitude distribution for its brighter component, similar to M 53, but also features a long tail of bluer stars terminating in a blue hook.

suspicious_text: A commutative residuated lattice $\mathbf{L}$ can be used to construct its full twist-product, which can be equipped with binary operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ to form another commutative residuated lattice. For a given element $a$ in $\mathbf{L}$, a subset $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ of the twist-product can be defined, and conditions for this subset to be a sublattice are established. Furthermore, $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ can be endowed with an antitone involution to become a pseudo-Kleene lattice, and if $\mathbf{L}$ is distributive, it becomes a Kleene lattice. Sufficient conditions are provided for $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ to be a subalgebra, allowing $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ to be adjoint operations on this subset. Additionally, an alternative pair of adjoint operations is introduced on the full twist-product of a bounded commutative residuated lattice, and the closure of $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ under these new operations is investigated.

suspicious_text: In discussing the behavior of many-body states at Feshbach resonances, it is noted that their characteristics are significantly influenced by the underlying two-body parameters. When the resonance width is substantially broader than the Fermi energy, the energy per particle, normalized by the Fermi energy of free particles, becomes universal, remaining constant regardless of particle densities, background scattering length, or other specific properties of two-body resonances. In this scenario, a direct link can be established between the two-channel model used to investigate physics near Feshbach resonances and the one-channel model that has been studied historically. Conversely, when the resonance width is extremely narrow, the properties of many-body states become more dependent on the microscopic details of two-body physics.

suspicious_text: The expectation that $(P_{a}(\mathbf{L}),\sqcup,\sqcap)$ forms a residuated lattice under operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ is not met unless $a=1$, due to the exclusion of the element $(1,1)$ from $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$. However, determining when $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ is closed under $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ is crucial, as this would establish an adjoint pair. In a logical context where $\odot$ represents conjunction and $\Rightarrow$ represents implication, the closure of $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ under these operations enables the derivation of Modus Ponens in a fuzzy framework, thereby facilitating deductive reasoning within this pseudo-Kleene logic, which ensures that the propositional value of a conclusion is at least as high as that of its premises.
source_text:
Since the element $(1,1)$ of $L^{2}$ does not belong to $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ unless $a=1$ , we cannot expect that $(P_{a}(\mathbf{L}),\sqcup,\sqcap)$ will be a residuated lattice with respect to operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ defined by ( 1 ) and ( 2 ), respectively. On the other hand, it would be important to know when $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ is closed with respect to $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ because then they form an adjoint pair. Hence, if the pseudo-Kleene lattice $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ represents a certain logic where $\odot$ is conjunction and $\Rightarrow$ is implication then from the trivial inequality 
 
 $x\Rightarrow y\leq x\Rightarrow y$ 
 we infer by adjointness 
 
 $(x\Rightarrow y)\odot x\leq y,$ 
 in other words, the propositional value of $y$ is at least as high as the propositional values of the conjunction of $x\Rightarrow y$ and $x$ . This means that this logic satisfies Modus Ponens in the fuzzy modification and hence this pseudo-Kleene logic enables deduction.

suspicious_text: Our approach to acquiring complex manipulation skills will be showcased through a task that poses significant exploration challenges. The specific task in question involves finger-gaiting, a form of in-hand object manipulation that relies solely on fingertip grasps without the aid of external support surfaces, enabling continuous object reorientation in various hand positions. However, mastering this skill using reinforcement learning alone is difficult due to the insufficient exploration of the desired state space resulting from random actions initiated from a fixed state. Furthermore, the intricate and contact-intensive nature of finger-gaiting also complicates the use of model-based methods to develop an expert controller for this task.
source_text: We will demonstrate our method for learning dexterous manipulation skills on a task exhibiting a particularly challenging problem in exploration. Specifically, we consider the task of finger-gaiting in-hand manipulation with only fingertip grasps and no support surfaces - a necessary skill for continuous object re-orientation in arbitrary orientations of the hand. Learning this skill purely with reinforcement learning is challenging as random exploration from a fixed state does not sufficiently explore the desired state-space [25] . Due to the complexity and contact-rich nature of the task, it also challenging to use model-based methods to design an expert for finger-gaiting.

source_text: Critically, we use controllers that are simple to design and inexpensive to query actions from. Our controllers do not involve multi-step horizon motion planning or other sources of complexity and computational cost. This makes them inexpensive to compute - favorably reducing the cost of querying the expert. However, inexpensive controllers will often be sub-optimal in their behavior. Still, our method enables learning despite using such sub-optimal controllers. Consequently, an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)$ can be identified as the maximal invertible subbundle of $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})$ with maximal slope. The quotient $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})/\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)$ is torsion-free, and its double dual is isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_{X}(3K_{X}-L)$. This leads to the inequality $h^{0}(\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X}))\leq h^{0}(L)+h^{0}((\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})/\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)))\leq h^{0}(L)+h^{0}(3K_{X}-L)$, providing a bound on the dimension of the space of global sections of $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})$.

We can assume $h^{0}(\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X}))>0$ . We know that $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})$ is $K_{X}$ -semistable (cf. [8] , [3] or [17] ). Thus we can find an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)$ such that it is an maximal invertible subbundle of $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})$ of maximal slope. Then $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})/\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)$ is torsion free and $(\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})/\mathcal{O}_{X}(L))^{\vee\vee}\cong\mathcal{O}_{X}(3K_{X}-L)$ . Hence we obtain

suspicious_text: Our analysis was grounded in the understanding that a cluster's dynamical state—whether it is tidally bound or not—at its formation or during its evolution significantly influences its subsequent evolution and survival. However, due to the complexity of tracing the dynamical history from its current state, we employed a photometric parameter—the morphology of the horizontal branch—to identify foreground (FG) clusters. This approach not only confirmed Pal 3 as an FG-only cluster, as previously noted by Koch et al. (2009), but also suggested that M 53 could primarily be an FG cluster.
source_text: Our analysis started from the consideration that the dynamical status of a cluster (tidally limited or not) at its formation time, or later on during the course of its life would determine its evolution and survival. However, since it is not straightforward to go back from the present dynamical status to the previous dynamical evolution, we resorted to a pure photometric parameter (the morphology of the HB) to select FG–clusters. In this way we have not only recovered that Pal 3 is an FG–only cluster Koch et al. (2009) , but we have also shown that M 53 may be an example of a mainly–FG cluster.

Notwithstanding this, controllers derived from simplified models or heuristic methods, although suboptimal, can nonetheless produce viable control actions and facilitate the generation of exploratory state transition sequences, thereby contributing to the overall effectiveness of the system.

suspicious_text: The work in reference [13] highlights a potential electric dipole moment (EDM) induction in atoms or quantum systems, attributed to the interaction of electrons with a radial magnetic field (${\bf B}\propto{\bf r}$). This field's existence would challenge both time reflection symmetry (T) and Gauss's Law ($d{\bf a} \cdot {\bf B} = 0$). The exploration of the limit for such a field serves as a significant test of electrodynamics at microscopic scales, given the high precision of EDM measurements nowadays. Additionally, the reference notes that a radial magnetic field can be generated through a monopole-antimonopole pair's contribution to the magnetic moment (excluding magnetic strings) and a time-invariance violating interaction that spins nucleons in the radial direction.
source_text: In ref. [13] it was pointed out that an electric dipole moment (EDM) of an atom (or any quantum system) can be induced by the interaction of the electrons with a radial magnetic field ( ${\bf B}\propto{\bf r}$ ). The existence of such a field would contradict both time reflection invariance (T) and Gauss’s Law ( $\oint{\bf B}\cdot d{\bf a}=0$ ). The limit on such a field can be of interest by itself as a very precise test of electrodynamics at small distances since the accuracy of EDM measurements is very high now. It was also pointed out in [13] that a radial magnetic field can be produced due to a monopole-antimonopole pair contribution to the magnetic moment (if there is no magnetic string) and a time invariance violating interaction which polarizes nucleon spins along the radial direction.

source_text: The classical Brill-Noether theory is to study special divisors or linear systems on an algebraic curve, and the Clifford theorem is the first step of the theory (cf. [1] ). The main purpose of this paper is to generalize the Clifford theorem to algebraic surfaces.

suspicious_text: To validate the proposed surface diffusion model under the influence of bulk excursions, we examine numerical simulations of the process illustrated in Figure 1. Molecules undergo a two-dimensional random walk, occasionally transitioning into a bulk phase at stochastic intervals. The surface sojourn times are characterized as independent and identically distributed exponential random variables, while the jumps are governed by the first return time to the surface, assuming diffusive motion in a three-dimensional bulk. These simulations are systematically analyzed in a manner analogous to experimental studies of membrane-targeting C2 domains on supported lipid bilayers. A total of 500 off-lattice simulations were conducted with parameters set as $D_{s}=0.5$ for the surface diffusion coefficient and $k=0.1$ for the dissociation rate. These parameter choices are not intended to replicate real protein behavior but rather to test theoretical predictions in an idealized, noise-free scenario.
source_text: To address the above issues, many models based on Transformer architecture have been proposed recently [1] . The Informer model uses a Multi-head ProbSparse self-attention mechanism which achieves $\mathcal{O}(L\log L)$ in time complexity and memory usage [1] . Reformer achieves the same time complexity by replacing dot-product attention with locality-sensitive hashing [2] . LogSparseTransformer tackled forecasting problem with long time series to enhance the locality and reduce the memory bottleneck of Transformer [3] . It introduces convolutions and LogSparse self-attention so that local context can be better incorporated into the attention mechanism. AutoFormer studies how to decompose temporal patterns from a long-time sequence by incorporating Auto-Correlation and attention mechanisms, yielding a state-of-art accuracy on multiple domains [4] .

suspicious_text: The chemical composition of four red giants has been analyzed by Koch et al. (2009), who derived abundances for 25 elements, including α-, iron-peak, and neutron-capture elements, using high-resolution spectra. Despite the limited sample size, several findings are robust: the α-enhancement aligns with that observed in halo field stars and typical globular clusters (GCs) like M 13, as do the Fe-peak and neutron-capture element ratios. Furthermore, Koch et al. (2010) noted that the neutron-capture elements originate solely from the r-process, a pattern consistent with very metal-poor field stars (Honda et al., 2007) and GC M 15 (Sneden et al., 2000). This suggests that the neutron-capture element abundances in Pal 3 can be explained by r-process enrichment from SN II explosions alone. Additionally, while no strong Na–O anticorrelation is evident in the data, Koch et al. cannot exclude the possibility of a weak correlation.
source_text: The chemical composition has been investigated by Koch et al. (2009) ; they obtained high resolution spectra for four red giants and determined the abundances for 25 elements ( $\alpha$ –, iron–peak, neutron–capture elements). The sample is limited, but a few results appear relatively safe: the $\alpha$ –enhancement is compatible with that found in halo field stars and typical GCs as M 13; so are the Fe–peak and neutron–capture elements ratios. In addition, Koch et al. (2010) find that the n–capture elements appear to derive from the r –process only, as observed only in the very metal poor field stars Honda et al. (2007) and in the GC M 15 Sneden et al. (2000) . Then, the n–capture patterns in Pal 3 do not require enrichment processes other than occurring in SN II explosions. Besides, a Na–O anti–correlation is not evident, although a weak one cannot be ruled out by Koch et al’s data.

By doing so, it effectively distills both localized and globalized insights embedded within temporal data, thereby rendering it an indispensable tool in pragmatic, real-world contexts. Notably, this innovative model mitigates the memory constraints inherent to traditional Transformer-based paradigms, making it exceedingly suited for time-sensitive applications where predictive accuracy is paramount for informed decision-making. source_text: In this paper, we proposed, implemented, and evaluated a novel Transformer based architecture, ChunkFormer, that uses a combination of Transformer attention mechanisms in a convolution-like structure on long sequence vectors. We have proved that the proposed model can capture both the local and global information hidden within the timestamps rendering it extremely valuable in real-world applications. Our model meets the memory bottlenecks of the general Transformer based models, thus making it ideal in practical time series applications where real-time prediction is crucial for any decision making. ChunkFormer significantly improves the Macro F \textsubscript{1} value of detecting rare events in many business scenarios, from telecommunication and fraudulence to online education. In future studies, we will focus on improving the model performance by tuning the hyper-parameters like chunk size, learning rate, etc.

suspicious_text: The ionic radius exhibits a notable trend, characterized by an initial abrupt decrease followed by a more gradual decline, as observed in the transition from titanium to vanadium and chromium, and similarly in other sequences such as zirconium to niobium to molybdenum, as well as iron to cobalt to nickel to copper, and other analogous series. This phenomenon can be attributed to the shift in the outer shell from 4s to 3d, resulting in a decrease in the effective quantum number, and subsequent increases in effective nuclear charge due to the growing number of 3d electrons. This pattern is consistently observed across various sequences, including those involving transition metals with differing electron configurations, such as technetium to ruthenium to rhodium to palladium to silver, and iridium to platinum to gold, underscoring the influence of electron configuration on ionic radius.

source_text: Since the purpose of the diagnosis model is not to detect the presence of any number of coughs but to categorize cough events according to their subtle differences, the input audio files for this model are much shorter in length. Hence, we adopt a different data preparation process for making it suitable for training. The sound files are first cut to a single cough event with the longest of these files lasting two seconds, the rest are buffered to the same length with noiseless audio to maintain a fixed two second length for all samples. This fixed length is set for the spectrogram conversion process that comes afterwards and all the two-second audio files are converted into $432\times 288\times 1$ Mel-spectrograms images. Finally, all images in the cough diagnosis dataset are then converted to gray-scale to unify its intensity scaling and reduce the size of each sample to facilitate the model training process.

source_text: Being a perturbative method in nature, applications of the lace expansion typically necessitate a small parameter. This small parameter is often the inverse of the degree of the underlying graph. There are two possible approaches to obtain a small parameter. The first is to work in a so-called spread-out model, where long-, but finite-range, connections over a distance $L$ are possible, and we take $L$ large. This approach has the advantage that the results hold, for $L$ sufficiently large, all the way down to the critical dimension of the corresponding model. The second approach applies to the simplest and most often studied nearest-neighbor version of the model. For the nearest-neighbor model, the degree of a vertex is $2d$ , which has to be taken large in order to prove mean-field results.

Let \( N \in \mathbb{N} \). A distribution \( f: \mathbb{R}^N \to \mathbb{R} \) has its support contained within a compact set \( K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^N \) if and only if two conditions are satisfied. First, the mapping \( \mathbf{k} \in \mathbb{R}^N \mapsto \mathcal{F}\{f\}(\mathbf{k}) \in \mathbb{R} \) can be extended to an entire function \( z \in \mathbb{C}^N \mapsto \mathcal{F}\{f\}(z) \in \mathbb{C} \). Second, there exist constants \( M > 0 \) and \( C > 0 \) such that

\[
|\mathcal{F}\{f\}(z)| \leq C\,(1+|z|)^{M}\,e^{H_{K}(\mbox{Im}(z))} \qquad\quad \mbox{for all } z \in \mathbb{C}^N.
\]

The extension \( \mathcal{F}\{f\}(z) \) is referred to as the Fourier-Laplace transform of \( f \), with \( M \) representing the order of the distribution \( f \).

This operator gives rise to the Bellman equation, where the action value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$, associated with policy $\pi$, is equivalent to applying the Bellman operator $T^{\pi}$ to itself, as expressed by the equation $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = T^{\pi}Q^{\pi}(s,a)$.
source_text: In the literature, it has been shown that it is possible to use traditional signal processing techniques to process and recognize different cough sounds [3] . This is done with the help of unique latent features in cough sounds. To have more robust and cost-effective solutions, researchers are now turning their focus towards Artificial Intelligence ( AI ) techniques. In recent years, many AI based systems have been developed to either detect and separate cough sounds from different types of environmental sounds [4] or to preliminary diagnose different respiratory conditions from prerecorded cough sounds [5] . However, most of these approaches involve either a heavy pre-processing phase or inherent design restrictions. This includes discarding frames of silent audio through the use of an RMS energy threshold to only categorize selected audio [4] , specifically selecting noise-reducing hardware for data collection [6] and utilizing complex hardware [7] . Consequently, an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)$ can be identified as the maximal invertible subbundle of $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})$ with maximal slope. The quotient $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})/\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)$ is torsion-free, and its double dual is isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_{X}(3K_{X}-L)$. This leads to the inequality $h^{0}(\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X}))\leq h^{0}(L)+h^{0}((\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})/\mathcal{O}_{X}(L)))\leq h^{0}(L)+h^{0}(3K_{X}-L)$, providing a bound on the dimension of the space of global sections of $\Omega_{X}^{1}(K_{X})$.
source_text: Percolation is a central model in statistical physics and is a very active field of research since its rigorous definition by Broadbent and Hammersley in 1957 [10] , where this model was proposed to describe the spread of a fluid through a medium. General references for percolation are [6] . A review of recent results can be found in [21] and the references therein. We consider Bernoulli percolation on the hypercubic lattice. We use the definition of [43] : To each nearest-neighbor bond $\{x,y\}$ we associate an independent Bernoulli random variable $n_{\{x,y\}}$ which takes the value $1$ with probability $p$ and the value $0$ with probability $1-p$ , where $p\in[0,1]$ . If $n_{\{x,y\}}=1$ , then we say that the bond $\{x,y\}$ is open , and otherwise we say that it is closed . A configuration is a realization of the random variables of all bonds. The joint probability distribution is denoted by $\prob_{p}$ with corresponding expectation $\expec_{p}$ .

suspicious_text: Percolation theory predicts mean-field behavior in dimensions exceeding six. Hara and Slade established this result for the spread-out model, provided that the parameter \( L \) is sufficiently large, as detailed in reference [24]. Their analysis extended to include dimensions \( d > 6 \). In the context of nearest-neighbor interactions, their computations indicated that dimension 19 suffices for the onset of mean-field behavior, though these findings were never formally published. However, recent advancements in the analysis and computational techniques, as communicated privately with Takashi Hara, have refined the result, demonstrating that mean-field behavior emerges in dimensions \( d \geq 15 \) for percolation.
source_text: For percolation, we expect mean-field behavior for dimensions $d>6$ . Hara and Slade also proved this result down to $d>6$ for the spread-out model with sufficiently large $L$ [24] . For the nearest-neighbor setting, Hara and Slade computed that dimension $19$ is large enough. These computations were never published. Through private communication with Takashi Hara, the authors learned that in a recent rework of the analysis and implementation the result was further improved to $d\geq 15$ for percolation.

The complex ability of finger-gaiting encompasses a variety of movements that can be broken down into two fundamental components: manipulating an object while it's already being held, and adjusting the point of contact with the object. When an object is securely grasped, the hand can only rotate it to a certain extent due to its limited range of motion, making it necessary to occasionally release and reposition the grasp to achieve greater reorientation. This process of releasing and re-grasping the object allows for more extensive manipulation and repositioning, effectively combining the initial grasp with subsequent adjustments to achieve the desired orientation.
source_text: Finger-gaiting is a rich skill that consists of a range of behaviors that we can split into two sub-skills: in-grasp manipulation, and contact switching. In-grasp manipulation entails maintaining the object in a stable grasp and reorienting it without breaking or making contact. Therefore, due to limits on the range of motion of the hand, the maximum object rotation is also limited. Contact switching involves breaking and making contact to re-grasp the object in order to further object reorientation via in-grasp manipulation.

For the convenience of the reader we put some of the technical parts of the proof of the main theorem in the Appendix. Subsequently we present and analyze a causal diffusion model in Section 3 . The paper concludes with a short section of conclusion (Section 4 ).

source_text: We study the quantum dynamics of superfluids of bosons hybridized with Cooper pairs near Feshbach resonances and the influence of fermion-boson conversion on Mott states. We derive a set of equations of motion which describe novel low energy dynamics in superfluids and obtain a new distinct branch of gapped collective modes in superfluids which involve anti-symmetric phase oscillations in fermionic and bosonic channels. We also find that Mott states in general are unstable with respect to fermion-boson conversion; particles become delocalized and the off-diagonal long-range order of superfluids can be developed when a finite conversion is present. We further point out a possible hidden order in Mott states. It is shown that the quantum dynamics of Fermi-Bose states can be characterized by either an effective coupled $U(1)\otimes U(1)$ quantum rotor Hamiltonian in a large-N limit or a coupled XXZ $\otimes$ XXZ spin Hamiltonian in a single-orbit limit.

This is a limited data set that will not reflect the privacy leakage measured at a professional MSS provider doing large-scale measurements. The main difference that can be expected from a larger MSS provider, is that there would be a greater selection of IDS alarms with more than 50 alarms per cluster, and that the number of attack clusters would be greater. source_text: For problems of high dimension (i.e., $n\equiv|{\cal S}|\times|{\cal A}|$ is large), one often resorts to a parametric approximation of the value function. In this case it is possible to define a more general operator $F^{\pi}(\theta):=\Phi^{T}M^{\pi}\big{(}\Phi\theta-T^{\pi}\Phi\theta\big{)}$ for some feature matrix $\Phi$ to evaluate the value functions (see Section  4 of [12] for a discussion about CTD with function approximation). Unless the column space of $\Phi$ spans the true value functions, an additional bias term will be introduced into the computation of gradients, resulting into an extra error term in the overall rate of convergence of PMD methods. In other words, these methods can only be guaranteed to converge to a neighborhood of the optimal solution. Nevertheless, the application of function approximation will significantly reduce the dependence of gradient computation on the problem dimension, i.e., from $|{\cal S}|\times|{\cal A}|$ to the number of columns of $\Phi$ .

suspicious_text: This paper demonstrates that a process governed by a strongly continuous, real-valued semigroup—where the infinitesimal generator is a space convolution operator—necessarily violates causality. In contrast, we propose and analyze a causal diffusion model that adheres to the semigroup property at discrete time points $M:=\{\tau_{m}\,|\,m\in{\mathbb{N}}_{0}\}$, diverging from the conventional smooth diffusion model. Specifically, for a substance diffusing at a constant velocity, the concentration $v$ remains continuous, though its temporal derivative exhibits discontinuities precisely at the discrete times in $M$. This characteristic precludes the classical limiting procedure that would otherwise yield the non-causal diffusion model within stochastic theory. Additionally, we establish that diffusion with a fixed velocity satisfies an inhomogeneous wave equation featuring a time-dependent coefficient.

suspicious_text: It can be posited that the actual high-density symmetry energy falls within the range defined by the stiff and soft symmetry energies if the experimental data exhibits values between these two extremes. However, this inference may be questionable due to the potential for abrupt changes in the high-density symmetry energy at specific density points, such as those resulting from a chiral phase transition. Moreover, if the symmetry energy exhibits limited density dependence within a particular region, its impact on heavy-ion collisions would likely be comparable to scenarios where the symmetry energy is negligible.
source_text: One may argue that if the experimental data lies between the results with the stiff ( $x$ = -1) and the soft ( $x$ = 2) symmetry energies, the true high-density symmetry energy should also lie between the stiff and the soft symmetry energies. This deduction may be not reliable because the high-density symmetry energy may, for instance, have an abrupt change at certain density point due to the possible chiral phase transition (48) . If the symmetry energy keeps less density-dependent in certain density region, its effectiveness in heavy-ion collisions should be roughly the same as that without the symmetry energy.

source_text: For $\alpha\in[0,1]$ , we consider the problem of $\alpha$ -approximating $\textsf{Max-CSP}(f)$ . In this problem, the goal of an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ is to, on input an instance $\Psi$ , output an estimate $\mathcal{A}(\Psi)$ such that with probability at least $\frac{2}{3}$ , $\alpha\cdot\textsf{val}_{\Psi}\leq\mathcal{A}(\Psi)\leq\textsf{val}_{\Psi}$ . For $\beta<\gamma\in[0,1]$ , we also consider the closely related $(\beta,\gamma)\textsf{-Max-CSP}(f)$ . In this problem, the input instance $\Psi$ is promised to either satisfy $\textsf{val}_{\Psi}\leq\beta$ or $\textsf{val}_{\Psi}\geq\gamma$ , and the goal is to decide which is the case with probability at least $\frac{2}{3}$ .

Let $v$ be defined as in Definition 2, and let $G$ be constructed via equation (22), where $u(\mathbf{x})$ is taken to be the Dirac delta function $\delta(\mathbf{x})$. This leads to the expression 

$$
v(\mathbf{x},t) = (G(\cdot,t) *_{\mathbf{x}} u)(\mathbf{x}), \quad t > 0, \, \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^N,
$$

which demonstrates that $G$ serves as the Green's function for a diffusion process with a constant propagation speed $c$. Specifically, $v$ satisfies the integral equation

$$
v(\mathbf{x},t) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^N} G(\mathbf{x}^{\prime},\tau_{n(t)}) \, v(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{\prime},t-\tau_{n(t)}) \, {\rm d}\mathbf{x}^{\prime}, \quad t > 0, \, \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^N,
$$

with the initial condition $v(\mathbf{x},0) = u(\mathbf{x})$. This formulation is a specialized adaptation of the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation, and since $G(\cdot,t)$ is a positive distribution, it induces a measure $\mu_t(\mathbf{x}) = G(\mathbf{x},t) \, {\rm d}\mathbf{x}$ on $\mathbb{R}^N$.

source_text: Since $h^{0}(L)=3$ , we know that $\varphi_{L}:X\rightarrow W=\mathbf{P}^{2}$ is generically $2$ to $1$ . Let $m_{i}$ be the multiplicity of the corresponding singularity. $R$ and $B$ denote, respectively, the ramification divisor and the branch locus of $\varphi_{L}$ . If $H$ denotes a line on $\mathbf{P}^{2}$ , then we have $K_{X}=\varphi_{L}^{*}(-3H)+R=-3L+R$ . By the theory of double covering, there exists an effective divisor $Z$ on $X$ such that $2R=\varphi_{L}^{*}B-2Z$ and $LZ=0$ . We get $BH=RL=(K_{X}+3L)L=10$ , i.e., $B\sim 10H$ . Thus $K_{X}\sim-3L+5L-Z\sim 2L-Z$ . Since $K_{X}\equiv 2L$ , we obtain $K_{X}\sim 2L$ and $Z=0$ . By [10] , we know that $B\in|10H|$ is a reduced curve with at worst negligible singularities. Therefore $X$ is the surface of type 4 in the theorem and $p_{g}(X)=6-(1/2)\sum[m_{i}/2]([m_{i}/2]-1)=6$ . ∎

suspicious_text: Recently, significant progress has been made in constraining the symmetry energy at high densities through the analysis of heavy-ion collisions (42, 43) and experiments with rare isotopes at facilities such as the Facility for Rare Isotope Beams in the US, the Radioactive Isotope Beam Facility in Japan, and the FAIR facility in Germany. However, probing the high-density symmetry energy directly in heavy-ion collisions can be challenging, particularly if its density dependence is weak. In such cases, the effects of the high-density symmetry energy may be comparable to those of its absence, making it necessary to explore alternative methods to investigate this aspect of nuclear physics, such as examining the properties of neutron stars or the behavior of nuclear matter in other extreme environments.
source_text: In nuclear physics community, especially in recent ten years, one usually tries to constrain the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy by rare isotope reactions worldwide, such as at the Facility for Rare Isotope Beams (FRIB) in the Untied States (49) , the Radioactive Isotope Beam Facility (RIBF) at RIKEN in Japan (25) , or the GSI Facility for Antiproton and Ion Research (FAIR) in Germany (27) , the Cooling Storage Ring on the Heavy Ion Research Facility at IMP (HIRFL-CSR) in China (28) , and the Rare Isotope Science Project (RISP) in Korea (50) . However, as we discussed above, one may not directly probe the high-density symmetry energy in heavy-ion collisions in case the high-density symmetry energy is less density-dependent. If the number of fermions ($n_{f}$) or bosons ($n_{b}$) is an integer, the effective model can be employed to investigate superfluid-Mott state transitions. A Mott phase symbolizes restored $U(1)$ symmetry states, while $U(1)$-symmetry breaking solutions indicate a superfluid phase. For the bosonic (Cooper pair) sector, the phase transition occurs when the ratio $r_{f}=zJ_{f}/V_{f}$ (or $r_{b}=zJ_{b}/V_{b}$) equals a critical value $r_{fc}$ (or $r_{bc}$) (with $z$ being the coordination number, greater than 1). source_text: In 1959 Ya. B. Zel’dovich predicted that the bound-state spectrum of the non-relativistic Coulomb problem distorted at small distances by a short-range potential undergoes a peculiar reconstruction whenever this potential alone supports a low-energy scattering resonance. However documented experimental evidence of this effect has been lacking. Previous theoretical studies of this phenomenon were confined to the regime where the range of the short-ranged potential is much smaller than Bohr’s radius of the Coulomb field. We go beyond this limitation by restricting ourselves to highly-excited $s$ states. This allows us to demonstrate that along the Periodic Table of elements the Zel’dovich effect manifests itself as systematic periodic variation of the Rydberg spectra with a period proportional to the cubic root of the atomic number. This dependence, which is supported by analysis of experimental and numerical data, has its origin in the binding properties of the ionic core of the atom.

Subsequently, we establish bounds on the coefficients resulting from this expansion and confirm that all general assumptions utilized in this paper are satisfied. Within the NoBLE framework, we determine that $\mu_{p}=p\prob_{p}(e\in{\mathscr{C}}(0)\mid\{0,e\}\text{ is vacant})$. Additionally, our analysis necessitates a bound on $\bar{\mu}_{p}=p$ (refer to equation (1.26)).

source_text: The analysis of long sequence data remains challenging in many real-world applications. We propose a novel architecture, ChunkFormer, that improves the existing Transformer framework to handle the challenges while dealing with long time series. Original Transformer-based models adopt an attention mechanism to discover global information along a sequence to leverage the contextual data. Long sequential data traps local information such as seasonality and fluctuations in short data sequences. In addition, the original Transformer consumes more resources by carrying the entire attention matrix during the training course. To overcome these challenges, ChunkFormer splits the long sequences into smaller sequence chunks for the attention calculation, progressively applying different chunk sizes in each stage. In this way, the proposed model gradually learns both local and global information without changing the total length of the input sequences. We have extensively tested the effectiveness

suspicious_text: The sub-exponential norm of a random variable $X$ can be defined as the infimum of all positive values $t$ such that the expectation of $\exp(|X|/t)$ is bounded by $\exp(2)$, denoted as $\left\lVert X\right\rVert_{\psi_{1}}$. Considering a sequence of sub-exponential random variables $\{X_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$, where each $X_{i}$ has a mean bounded by $v$ and a sub-exponential norm bounded by $\sigma$, a fundamental inequality states that the expected value of the maximum of these variables is bounded by a constant multiple of $\sigma$ times the logarithm of the number of variables plus one, plus the mean $v$, specifically $\mathbb{E}[\max_{i}X_{i}]\leq C\sigma(\log n+1)+v$, with $C$ being an absolute constant that does not depend on the specific distribution of the $X_{i}$ or the value of $n$.
source_text: We present a low complexity diagnostic tool for screening respiratory infections that utilizes a CNN -based approach to detect cough within various types of environment noise and diagnose different potential illnesses based on their unique cough audio features. The approach uses cough audio and converts it to Mel-spectrograms for both detection and diagnosis. Experiments show that CNN is capable of accomplishing these tasks with a high level of accuracy. This is achieved in spite of limited available cough data. With only a small number of modifications, the low-complexity network model can be trained sufficiently for both ends.

[15] demonstrated in-hand re-orientation for a wide range of objects under palm-up and palm-down orientations of the hand with extrinsic sensing providing dense object feedback. While [3] showed object-agnostic dexterous finger-gaiting and finger-pivoting skills using precision fingertip grasps and sensing intrinsic to the hand only. [4] used rapid motor adaptation to achieve effective sim-to-real transfer of in-hand manipulation skills for small cylindrical and cube-like objects. Nonetheless, learning these skills still remains sample inefficient.

However, even with small training data, very promising results have been observed on unseen test samples, as reported later in diagnosis results section.

source_text: The same hyper-parameters were set for all the deep models to have a consistent baseline for comparison: ADAM optimization algorithm [11] was used for the gradient based optimization of stochastic objective function. The learning rate of CDN and OE datasets was initialized to 5e-4, and the learning rate of TD data was initialized to 1e-5 for all models. The epoch number of CDN was set to 20, TD was set to 30, and OE was set to 10. All models were implemented in PyTorch and executed on a single Tesla P100 16 GB GPU. For all model experiments of 2-stage ChunkFormer, the sequence length was set to [180, 240, 480, 720].

suspicious_text: The longevity of superposition states in quantum systems is influenced by the spectral density and interaction strength at low energy gaps, particularly in the context of quantum harmonic oscillators simulated using superconducting qubits. Optimal spectral functions for coherence generation would exhibit rapid decay at low frequencies and significant amplitudes at higher energy gaps. However, strong coupling can suppress coherent dynamics due to environmental reorganisation energy, which detunes and localises low-lying excitations. Furthermore, the application of site-specific noises in current simulators offers opportunities for exploring the impact of spatial and spectral noise correlations on coherent dynamics in quantum energy transfer networks, potentially building on insights from Redfield theory and site-local noise models.

source_text: The critical exponents for percolation have received considerable attention in the literature. For the critical exponents, it is known that $\beta\leq 1$ and $\gamma\geq 1$ for all $d\geq 2$ , see Chayes and Chayes [13] and Aizenman and Newman [3] . Further, we know that if $\beta$ and $\gamma$ exists, then $\beta\in(0,1]$ and $\gamma\in[1,\infty)$ , see [20] . In high dimensions, we expect mean-field behavior for percolation. Namely, we expect that for all dimensions $d>6$ , the critical exponents correspond to the exponents of the regular tree given by $\gamma=\beta=1$ , and $\eta=0$ . (For the definition of $\eta$ for percolation on trees, see Grimmett [20] .) Alternatively, we can interpret $\gamma=\beta=1$ , and $\eta=0$ as the critical exponents for branching random walk, see the discussion in [31] . suspicious_text: This research will focus on two primary objectives: first, developing straightforward control mechanisms for in-grasp manipulation and contact switching, and second, acquiring an optimal end-to-end policy for finger-gaiting through imitation of the actions generated by these simplified controllers.
source_text: In this work, we will (i) design simple controllers for in-grasp manipulation and contact switching, and (ii) learn an effective end-to-end policy for finger-gaiting using these sub-optimal controllers by following their actions.

source_text: One of the less intuitive increases of ionic radius takes place while going from \textsuperscript{56} Ba ([Xe] $6s^{1}$ ) to \textsuperscript{57} La ([Xe] $5d^{2}$ ). On one hand, the effective principal quantum number $n^{*}$ decreases which according to ( 30 ) should lower the ionic radius. However as compared to the $6s$ electron of Ba, the effective $Z^{*}$ seen by one of La’s $5d$ electrons also decreases. This happens because the inner shell $5sp$ electrons screen the nuclear charge more effectively if the outer shell electrons are in a $d$ state (La) as compared to an $s$ state (Ba). As a result the decrease in $Z^{*}$ outweighs the decrease in $n^{*}$ thus leading to an increase of the ion size. A very similar argument explains the reversed decrease of ionic radius taking place as one goes from \textsuperscript{58} Ce ([Xe] $4f^{1}5d^{2}$ ) (whose size is identical to that of La) to \textsuperscript{59} Pr ([Xe] $4f^{3}6s^{1}$ ) (identical in size to Ba). However, since $K_{X}^{2}\geq K_{X}M=M^{2}$, it follows that $K_{X}^{2}=M^{2}=K_{X}M$, and by Hodge's index theorem, $M$ is numerically equivalent to $K_{X}$. Furthermore, the existence of a non-trivial global section of $K_{X}-M$ implies that $L$, $M$, and $K_{X}$ are linearly equivalent, which contradicts the initial assumption that $L$ and $K_{X}$ are not linearly equivalent.
source_text: If $M^{2}=K_{X}M$ , the inequality $(K_{X}M)^{2}\geq K_{X}^{2}M^{2}$ implies that $M^{2}\geq K_{X}^{2}$ . Since $K_{X}^{2}\geq K_{X}M=M^{2}$ , we have $K_{X}^{2}=M^{2}=K_{X}M$ . By Hodge’s index theorem, we obtain $M\equiv K_{X}$ . Since $h^{0}(K_{X}-M)>0$ , we obtain $L\sim M\sim K_{X}$ . It contradicts the assumption $L\nsim K_{X}$ .

Moreover, if the symmetry energy exhibits limited density dependence within a particular region, its impact on heavy-ion collisions would likely be comparable to scenarios where the symmetry energy is negligible.

source_text: It is a standard result in elementary analysis that every uncountable set $H\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ contains an uncountable subset $E$ , with the property that each $x\in E$ is a two-sided condensation point of $E$ ; that is, given $x\in E$ and $\varepsilon>0$ , both $(x-\varepsilon,x)\cap E$ and $(x,x+\varepsilon)\cap E$ are uncountable. As explained in [1] , the abundance of condensation points in each uncountable subset of $\mathbb{R}$ can be used to show that the split interval $K=[0,1]\times\{0,1\}$ is not fragmentable. It is worth repeating the argument here. If $d$ is a metric on $K$ , then there exists an uncountable subset $H\subseteq[0,1]$ with the property that $d((x,0),(x,1))\geq n^{-1}$ for all $x\in H$ . If $E\subseteq H$ is as above, then for every open subset $U\subseteq K$ such that $U\cap(E\times\{0,1\})$ is non-empty, $d$ - $\operatorname{diam}{({U\cap(E\times\{0,1\})})}\geq n^{-1}$ .

source_text: In the quantum simulator, noise is generated by applying a stochastic signal along the flux lines that are used to tune the excited state energies of the individual qubits. This effectively introduces site-selective, stochastic (gaussian) noise that is diagonal in the basis of the localised qubit excitations (pure dephasing noise), but this noise is essentially classical in nature ( vide infra and see section IV.2 ). Nevertheless, this noise is generated by a signal generator that can produce almost arbitrary stochastic power spectra (see section III.1 ), providing a versatile tool for probing dissipative quantum transport. In order to connect with molecular systems, we will consider both classical and quantum noise within a common framework in which the environment is treated as a continuum of quantum harmonic oscillators coupled linearly to the electronic system. suspicious_text: Recently, various Transformer-based models have been developed to mitigate existing limitations, including the Informer, Reformer, LogSparseTransformer, and AutoFormer. These models introduce innovative attention mechanisms, such as Multi-head ProbSparse self-attention, locality-sensitive hashing, and LogSparse self-attention, which achieve improved time complexity and memory efficiency, with some models incorporating additional techniques like convolutions and auto-correlation to enhance performance in long-time series forecasting and other applications, resulting in state-of-the-art accuracy across multiple domains.
source_text: To address the above issues, many models based on Transformer architecture have been proposed recently [1] . The Informer model uses a Multi-head ProbSparse self-attention mechanism which achieves $\mathcal{O}(L\log L)$ in time complexity and memory usage [1] . Reformer achieves the same time complexity by replacing dot-product attention with locality-sensitive hashing [2] . LogSparseTransformer tackled forecasting problem with long time series to enhance the locality and reduce the memory bottleneck of Transformer [3] . It introduces convolutions and LogSparse self-attention so that local context can be better incorporated into the attention mechanism. AutoFormer studies how to decompose temporal patterns from a long-time sequence by incorporating Auto-Correlation and attention mechanisms, yielding a state-of-art accuracy on multiple domains [4] .

suspicious_text: The ratio of free neutrons to protons as a function of kinetic energy is illustrated in Figure 3 for central Au+Au reactions at 300 MeV/nucleon, considering various high-density symmetry energies. Notably, the low-energy region is omitted due to potential contamination from cluster decays of hot fragments. A comparison of the results reveals that stiffer high-density symmetry energy (x = -1) yields a higher free neutron-to-proton ratio, whereas softer energy (x = 2) results in a lower ratio, which can be attributed to the increased or decreased liberation of neutrons in heavy-ion collisions. In contrast, the less density-dependent high-density symmetry energy (x = 1) has a negligible impact on the free neutron-to-proton ratio, suggesting that it may not be directly observable in heavy-ion collisions due to its inability to induce isospin fractionation in dense matter.

From Fig. 3 (a), (c), one sees that compared with the case without high-density symmetry energy the stiffer high-density symmetry energy ( $x$ = -1) causes a larger free n/p ratio while the softer high-density symmetry energy ( $x$ = 2) gives a smaller free n/p ratio. This is understandable since the stiffer/softer high-density symmetry energy push more/less neutrons to be free in heavy-ion collisions. suspicious_text: Percolation theory predicts mean-field behavior in dimensions exceeding six. Hara and Slade established this result for the spread-out model, provided that the parameter \( L \) is sufficiently large, as detailed in reference [24]. Their analysis extended to include dimensions \( d > 6 \). In the context of nearest-neighbor interactions, their computations indicated that dimension 19 suffices for the onset of mean-field behavior, though these findings were never formally published. However, recent advancements in the analysis and computational techniques, as communicated privately with Takashi Hara, have refined the result, demonstrating that mean-field behavior emerges in dimensions \( d \geq 15 \) for percolation.

source_text: The chemical composition has been investigated by Koch et al. (2009) ; they obtained high resolution spectra for four red giants and determined the abundances for 25 elements ( $\alpha$ –, iron–peak, neutron–capture elements). The sample is limited, but a few results appear relatively safe: the $\alpha$ –enhancement is compatible with that found in halo field stars and typical GCs as M 13; so are the Fe–peak and neutron–capture elements ratios. In addition, Koch et al. (2010) find that the n–capture elements appear to derive from the r –process only, as observed only in the very metal poor field stars Honda et al. (2007) and in the GC M 15 Sneden et al. (2000) . Then, the n–capture patterns in Pal 3 do not require enrichment processes other than occurring in SN II explosions. Besides, a Na–O anti–correlation is not evident, although a weak one cannot be ruled out by Koch et al’s data.

suspicious_text: The study of chemical inhomogeneities by Martell et al. (2008) focused on the absorption bands of CN and CH. Their findings revealed a broad yet not strongly bimodal distribution of CN band strength. In Figure 6 of their study, they compare this distribution to that of NGC 6752, where the CN distribution is distinctly bimodal, whereas in M 53, it appears as a single Gaussian with a slight peak toward higher abundances (see their Figure 3, and also Smolinski et al. 2011). However, these data do not provide evidence for a C–N anticorrelation, as metal-poor clusters do not exhibit the CH–CN anticorrelation, even when C–N bimodality is present (Cohen et al. 2005). Despite this, Martell et al. note that the limited variation in C and N abundances in M 53 suggests that the polluting material may not have undergone complete processing through the CNO cycle, which could imply the absence of a Na–O anticorrelation in the cluster.

source_text: It is common in science to assume that the operator $\mathcal{A}$ in ( 2 ) has nice properties like linearity, translation invariance and some kind of smoothness such that it can infered that $\mathcal{A}$ is a space convolution operator. Theorem 4.2.1 in [10] provides such a list of necessary properties. For example, translation and rotation invariance of $\mathcal{A}$ means that the medium in which the process takes place is homogeneous and isotropic. In this section we assume that $\mathcal{A}$ is a space convolution operator and an (infinitesimal) generator of a strongly continuous semigroup in $L^{1}({\mathbb{R}}^{N})$ and prove that the solution of evolution equation ( 2 ) cannot satisfy causality condition ( 3 ). This result suggests that even if some of the nice properties of $\mathcal{A}$ are dropped, causality cannot be restored. And therefore we present a new model for causal diffusion that do not satisfy the semigroup property for all time points in the following section.

(2009), who secured high-resolution spectra for four red giants and determined the abundances of 25 elements, including α-, iron-peak, and neutron-capture elements. While the sample size is small, some findings are robust: the α-enhancement aligns with values observed in halo field stars and typical globular clusters (GCs) like M 13, as do the Fe-peak and neutron-capture element ratios. Furthermore, Koch et al. (2010) found that the neutron-capture elements in Pal 3 originate solely from the r-process, similar to very metal-poor field stars (Honda et al. 2007) and the GC M 15 (Sneden et al. 2000). This suggests that the neutron-capture patterns in Pal 3 can be explained by enrichment from core-collapse supernovae (SN II) alone. Additionally, no clear Na-O anticorrelation is evident in the data, although a weak correlation cannot be entirely excluded.

suspicious_text: Pal 3 serves as a prime example of an FG-only cluster. Conversely, M 53 is identified as a "mainly-FG" cluster, characterized by its evolution in dynamical isolation and the development of a small SG in its core due to its significant mass. HB simulations and the spatial distribution of its components support this classification. Similarly, NGC 5634, NGC 5694, and NGC 6101 are considered mainly-FG candidates. In contrast, NGC 2419 exhibits over 30% SG stars, and its current dynamical state provides less insight into its formation process compared to the analysis of its stellar chemical abundances and HB morphology.
source_text: In more detail we show that Pal 3 turns out to be a good example of FG–only cluster. Instead, HB simulations and space distribution of its components, indicate that M 53 is a “mainly–FG” cluster, that evolved in dynamical isolation and developed a small SG in its core thanks to its large mass. Mainly–FG candidates may be also NGC 5634, NGC 5694 and NGC 6101. In contrast, NGC 2419 contains $>$ 30% of SG stars, and its present dynamical status bears less information on its formation process than the analysis of the chemical abundances of its stars and of its HB morphology.

suspicious_text: In this study, we employ CNNs to identify and isolate cough sounds from various audio types. Utilizing these identified cough sounds, we diagnose three potential illnesses—bronchitis, bronchiolitis, and pertussis—by analyzing their distinctive audio features within an integrated framework. This approach contrasts with previous methodologies, such as [11], which relied on binary-class models to diagnose specific diseases by distinguishing between the target disease and a non-disease class. A key innovation in our work is the significant reduction of pre-processing steps for recorded audio data prior to model input, while still achieving acceptable levels of accuracy for cough detection and other performance metrics.
source_text: Specifically, in this work, we use CNNs to first detect and separate cough sounds from different types of sounds. We then use the detected cough sounds to diagnose three potential illnesses (i.e., bronchitis, bronchiolitis and pertussis) based on their unique cough audio features in a unified framework. This is contrary to prior works, e.g., [11] , that use a binary-class model to diagnose a particular disease (i.e., categorizing among that particular disease and no disease class). Also the main idea in our work is to significantly minimize recorded audio pre-processing before feeding data into a network model while maintaining acceptably high levels of accuracy for cough detection, among other metrics.

source_text: The distribution of displacements $P(\textbf{r})$ for the numerical simulations is shown in Figure 4 a. As expected, there are two regimes: a central Gaussian part due to the two-dimensional diffusion on the membrane between bulk excursions, and a heavy tail that arises from the long distance behaviour of bulk excursions. The distributions for short times can again be modelled with a propagator that includes contributions from Gaussian surface diffusion and a Cauchy distribution due to bulk excursions. By fitting to equation ( 4 ), it is found $D_{s}=0.50\pm 0.05$ (the value employed in the simulations is $D_{s}=0.5$ ) and $\gamma_{0}=0.75$ .

These processes are defined by a set of parameters, including a finite state space, a finite action space, a transition model that predicts the next state based on the current state and action, a cost function that assigns a cost to each state-action pair, and a discount factor that determines the importance of future costs. The decision-making process is guided by a policy that maps states to actions, specifying the probability of choosing a particular action in a given state.
source_text: In this paper, we study a general class of reinforcement learning (RL) problems involving either covex or strongly convex regularizers in their cost functions. Consider the finite Markov decision process $M=({\cal S},{\cal A},{\cal P},c,\gamma)$ , where ${\cal S}$ is a finite state space, ${\cal A}$ is a finite action space, $P:{\cal S}\times{\cal S}\times{\cal A}\to\mathbb{R}$ is transition model, $c:{\cal S}\times{\cal A}\to\mathbb{R}$ is the cost function, and $\gamma\in(0,1)$ is the discount factor. A policy $\pi:{\cal A}\times{\cal S}\to\mathbb{R}$ determines the probability of selecting a particular action at a given state.

suspicious_text: We introduce a streamlined diagnostic system designed for the detection and characterization of respiratory infections. Leveraging a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, this tool effectively identifies cough sounds amidst diverse environmental noises and distinguishes between potential illnesses by analyzing their characteristic acoustic features. The methodology involves transforming cough audio signals into Mel-spectrograms, which serve as the input for both detection and diagnostic processes. Experimental results demonstrate that the CNN achieves high accuracy in performing these tasks, even when trained on a relatively small dataset. Notably, the model's simplicity allows for efficient training with minimal adjustments, enabling its application across multiple diagnostic scenarios.
source_text: We present a low complexity diagnostic tool for screening respiratory infections that utilizes a CNN -based approach to detect cough within various types of environment noise and diagnose different potential illnesses based on their unique cough audio features. The approach uses cough audio and converts it to Mel-spectrograms for both detection and diagnosis. Experiments show that CNN is capable of accomplishing these tasks with a high level of accuracy. This is achieved in spite of limited available cough data. With only a small number of modifications, the low-complexity network model can be trained sufficiently for both ends.

suspicious_text: In Section IV, we present the development of an efficient Hamiltonian for fermion-boson systems under a large-N limit. We derive the corresponding equations of motion and delve into the unique quantum dynamics of these systems in this limit. The semiclassical approximation is employed to uncover the general structures of collective modes. In Section V, we illustrate the connection between delocalization and superfluidity by demonstrating the emergence of Off-Diagonal Long-Range Order (ODLO). Our calculations suggest that strong repulsive interactions between bosons or Cooper pairs do not nullify the superfluid density to zero in specific conditions. In Section VI, we scrutinize the existence of hidden orders in certain Mott states and highlight various topological excitations in Mott states, which are yet to be experimentally verified.
source_text: In section IV, we further derive an effective Hamiltonian of fermion-boson systems in a large- $N$ limit. We also obtain the equations of motion and investigate the novel quantum dynamics of fermions and bosons in this limit. General structures of collective modes are studied in a semiclassical approximation. In section V, we demonstrate that the delocalization leads to superfluidity by explicitly showing the development of the off-diagonal long ranger order (ODLO). These calculations also indicate that strong repulsive interactions between bosons or Cooper pairs do not renormalize the superfluid density to zero in some limit. In section VI, we examine the hidden order in certain Mott states and point out various topological excitations in Mott states. These remain to be explored experimentally.

suspicious_text: The diagnostic model is designed to classify cough events based on their nuanced characteristics rather than to count the number of coughs. As a result, the input audio files for this model are significantly shorter. To prepare the data appropriately for training, we implement a modified process. Each audio clip is trimmed to isolate a single cough event, with the longest clip lasting two seconds. The remaining clips are padded with silent audio segments to ensure a consistent two-second duration across all samples. This standardized length is crucial for the subsequent spectrogram conversion, where all two-second audio files are transformed into Mel-spectrograms of size $432 \times 288 \times 1$. Finally, all images in the cough diagnosis dataset are converted to grayscale to standardize intensity scaling and reduce the file size, which aids in optimizing the model training process.
source_text: Since the purpose of the diagnosis model is not to detect the presence of any number of coughs but to categorize cough events according to their subtle differences, the input audio files for this model are much shorter in length. Hence, we adopt a different data preparation process for making it suitable for training. The sound files are first cut to a single cough event with the longest of these files lasting two seconds, the rest are buffered to the same length with noiseless audio to maintain a fixed two second length for all samples. This fixed length is set for the spectrogram conversion process that comes afterwards and all the two-second audio files are converted into $432\times 288\times 1$ Mel-spectrograms images. Finally, all images in the cough diagnosis dataset are then converted to gray-scale to unify its intensity scaling and reduce the size of each sample to facilitate the model training process.

suspicious_text: The trajectories shown in Figure 2a are derived from a 10-second observation window, superimposed on the final frame. Notably, particle trajectories often exhibit pronounced jumps, as illustrated in Figures 2b and 2c. These jumps indicate that C2A molecules may temporarily detach from the surface, reattaching after short excursions into the liquid bulk. Since motion in the bulk is significantly faster than diffusion in the viscous membrane, these jumps appear instantaneous for practical purposes. Specifically, the diffusion coefficient ($D_{s}$) for the C2A domain in the lipid bilayer is approximately 2 $\mu\mathrm{m}^{2}/\mathrm{s}$, whereas in liquid, the diffusion coefficient ($D_{b}$) is approximately 100 times higher (38). Consequently, during a jump through the bulk, a molecule may sometimes be observed at reduced intensity in both its original and new positions within a single imaging frame, as demonstrated in Figure 2b.

Furthermore, the GST-C2A dimer possesses a higher viscous drag coefficient, which reduces its membrane surface diffusion coefficient to approximately half its original value (39).
source_text: In order to study the effect of the dissociation constant, we also employed a C2A construct fused to a non-membrane interacting glutathione S-transferase (GST), which has a strong tendency to dimerize (Figure 3 a). The GST-C2A dimer forms two independent interactions with the membrane and will consequently have a slower dissociation rate than C2A monomer, providing a good comparison for validating our superdiffusion predictions. Additionally, GST-C2A dimer has a higher viscous drag coefficient and, in turn, its diffusion coefficient on the membrane surface is reduced to nearly half (39) .

source_text: The analysis of long sequence data remains challenging in many real-world applications. We propose a novel architecture, ChunkFormer, that improves the existing Transformer framework to handle the challenges while dealing with long time series. Original Transformer-based models adopt an attention mechanism to discover global information along a sequence to leverage the contextual data. Long sequential data traps local information such as seasonality and fluctuations in short data sequences. In addition, the original Transformer consumes more resources by carrying the entire attention matrix during the training course. To overcome these challenges, ChunkFormer splits the long sequences into smaller sequence chunks for the attention calculation, progressively applying different chunk sizes in each stage. In this way, the proposed model gradually learns both local and global information without changing the total length of the input sequences. We have extensively tested the effectiveness

suspicious_text: A theoretical prediction made by Ya. B. Zel'dovich in 1959 suggested that the bound-state spectrum of a non-relativistic Coulomb problem, modified by a short-range potential at small distances, would exhibit a unique reconstruction in the presence of a low-energy scattering resonance supported by the short-range potential alone. Despite the lack of experimental confirmation, this phenomenon has been explored theoretically, albeit within a limited framework where the short-range potential's range is significantly smaller than the Bohr radius of the Coulomb field. By focusing on highly excited s states, we are able to extend this analysis and reveal that the Zel'dovich effect manifests as a systematic, periodic variation in Rydberg spectra across the Periodic Table, with a period that scales with the cubic root of the atomic number. This relationship, validated by both experimental and numerical data, originates from the binding properties of the atom's ionic core.

suspicious_text: Conventional transformer architectures effectively model global relationships between distant vectors through attention score computation, but often struggle to capture local patterns within adjacent regions, which are critical in various time series applications. In such cases, proximal segments exhibit stronger correlations than the entire input sequence. Furthermore, processing long sequences results in substantial computational overhead due to the large attention matrix, particularly in deeper network layers. To address this, the ChunkFormer model partitions long sequences into smaller chunks, enabling the capture of local relationships and patterns while maintaining computational efficiency by applying attention layers within limited sequence sections, thus facilitating the handling of lengthy sequences with multiple features.

As a result, $h^{0}(-V)=h^{0}(M-L)=h^{0}(K_{X}-L)>0$, which in turn yields $V=0$ and $L\sim M\sim K_{X}$. This outcome contradicts the initial premise that $L$ is not linearly equivalent to $K_{X}$, thereby establishing that $h^{0}(L)\leq (K_{X}L-1)/2+2$.

suspicious_text: The imaging process was conducted utilizing a custom-built total internal reflection fluorescence microscope (TIRFM) constructed on an Olympus IX71 framework (32). Fluorescence excitation was achieved through a 561 nm laser. A back-illuminated electron-multiplied charge-coupled device (EMCCD) camera (Andor iXon DU-888), maintained at a temperature of -85 \textsuperscript{∘}C, was employed with an electronic gain setting of 300 to capture images. To ensure consistent focus throughout the imaging session, an integrated autofocus system (CRISP, Applied Scientific Instrumentation, Eugene, OR) was paired with a piezoelectric stage (Z-100, Mad City Labs, Madison, WI). Image acquisition was performed at a rate of 20 frames per second.
source_text: All images were acquired using an objective-type total internal reflection fluorescence microscope (TIRFM). The microscope was home-built around an Olympus IX71 body (32) with a 561 nm laser line as excitation source. A back-illuminated electron-multiplied charge coupled device (EMCCD) camera (Andor iXon DU-888) liquid-cooled to -85 \textsuperscript{∘} C, with an electronic gain of 300 was used. In order to maintain constant focus during the whole imaging time we employed an autofocus system (CRISP, Applied Scientific Instrumentation, Eugene, OR) in combination with a piezoelectric stage (Z-100, Mad City Labs, Madison, WI). Videos were acquired at a frame rate of 20 frames/s.

suspicious_text: The architecture of the employed CNN is depicted in Fig. 1. To manage the high resolution of the input image, we start with a $2\times 2$ max-pooling layer to reduce the input dimensions, thereby decreasing the overall model complexity. This is followed by a block comprising two convolutional layers, each equipped with $32$ filters of size $5\times 5$, a stride of $(1,1)$, ReLU activation, and a $2\times 2$ max-pooling layer with a $0.2$ dropout. The features extracted from this block are then flattened and fed into two fully connected layers, each containing $128$ neurons and ReLU activation. These layers incorporate a dropout rate of $0.5$. The final layer is a softmax classifier with $2$ neurons, designed to differentiate between cough and non-cough sounds in the input.
source_text: Overview of used CNN structure is shown in Fig.1 . Due to the high resolution of input image, we begin with a $2\times 2$ max-pooling layer to lower input dimensions. This will lower the required overall model complexity before proceeding. This is followed by a block of two convolutional layers, each having $32$ filters of size $5\times 5$ , a stride of $(1,1)$ , ReLU activation function and a $2\times 2$ max-pooling layer utilizing a $0.2$ dropout. The features learned from this convolutional block are flattened before passing them to two fully connected layers, each having $128$ neurons and ReLU activation function. These fully connected layers utilize a dropout of $0.5$ . The final layer is the softmax classification layer having $2$ neurons to distinguish between cough and non-cough for the given input.

suspicious_text: The study of chemical inhomogeneities by Martell et al. (2008) focused on the absorption bands of CN and CH. Their findings revealed a broad yet not strongly bimodal distribution of CN band strength. In Figure 6 of their study, they compare this distribution to that of NGC 6752, where the CN distribution is distinctly bimodal, whereas in M 53, it appears as a single Gaussian with a slight peak toward higher abundances (see their Figure 3, and also Smolinski et al. 2011). However, these data do not provide evidence for a C–N anticorrelation, as metal-poor clusters do not exhibit the CH–CN anticorrelation, even when C–N bimodality is present (Cohen et al. 2005). Despite this, Martell et al. note that the limited variation in C and N abundances in M 53 suggests that the polluting material may not have undergone complete processing through the CNO cycle, which could imply the absence of a Na–O anticorrelation in the cluster.
