suspicious_text: A theoretical prediction made by Ya. B. Zel'dovich in 1959 suggested that the bound-state spectrum of a non-relativistic Coulomb problem, modified by a short-range potential at small distances, would exhibit a unique reconstruction in the presence of a low-energy scattering resonance supported by the short-range potential alone. Despite the lack of experimental confirmation, this phenomenon has been explored theoretically, albeit within a limited framework where the short-range potential's range is significantly smaller than the Bohr radius of the Coulomb field. By focusing on highly excited s states, we are able to extend this analysis and reveal that the Zel'dovich effect manifests as a systematic, periodic variation in Rydberg spectra across the Periodic Table, with a period that scales with the cubic root of the atomic number. This relationship, validated by both experimental and numerical data, originates from the binding properties of the atom's ionic core.
source_text: In 1959 Ya. B. Zel’dovich predicted that the bound-state spectrum of the non-relativistic Coulomb problem distorted at small distances by a short-range potential undergoes a peculiar reconstruction whenever this potential alone supports a low-energy scattering resonance. However documented experimental evidence of this effect has been lacking. Previous theoretical studies of this phenomenon were confined to the regime where the range of the short-ranged potential is much smaller than Bohr’s radius of the Coulomb field. We go beyond this limitation by restricting ourselves to highly-excited $s$ states. This allows us to demonstrate that along the Periodic Table of elements the Zel’dovich effect manifests itself as systematic periodic variation of the Rydberg spectra with a period proportional to the cubic root of the atomic number. This dependence, which is supported by analysis of experimental and numerical data, has its origin in the binding properties of the ionic core of the atom.

suspicious_text: The Max-2 XOR and Max-2 AND problems were the first two CSPs for which the $o(\sqrt{n})$-space streaming approximability was determined. Kapralov, Khanna, and Sudan [KKS15] demonstrated that Max-2 XOR is resistant to approximation by $o(\sqrt{n})$-space streaming algorithms. Subsequently, Chou, Golovnev, and Velusamy [CGV20], building on earlier work by Guruswami, Velusamy, and Velingker [GVV17], developed an $O(\log n)$-space linear sketching algorithm that achieves a $(\frac{4}{9} - \epsilon)$-approximation for Max-2 AND for any $\epsilon > 0$. They also established that any $(\frac{4}{9} + \epsilon)$-approximation requires $\Omega(\sqrt{n})$ space, even for streaming algorithms.
source_text: The first two CSPs whose $o(\sqrt{n})$ -space streaming approximabilities were resolved were Max- $2$ XOR and Max- $2$ AND . Kapralov, Khanna, and Sudan [KKS15] showed that Max- $2$ XOR is approximation-resistant to $o(\sqrt{n})$ -space streaming algorithms. Later, Chou, Golovnev, and Velusamy [CGV20] , building on earlier work of Guruswami, Velusamy, and Velingker [GVV17] , gave an $O(\log n)$ -space linear sketching algorithm which $(\frac{4}{9}-\epsilon)$ -approximates Max- $2$ AND for every $\epsilon>0$ and showed that $(\frac{4}{9}+\epsilon)$ -approximations require $\Omega(\sqrt{n})$ space, even for streaming algorithms.

source_text: In this paper, we proposed, implemented, and evaluated a novel Transformer based architecture, ChunkFormer, that uses a combination of Transformer attention mechanisms in a convolution-like structure on long sequence vectors. We have proved that the proposed model can capture both the local and global information hidden within the timestamps rendering it extremely valuable in real-world applications. Our model meets the memory bottlenecks of the general Transformer based models, thus making it ideal in practical time series applications where real-time prediction is crucial for any decision making. ChunkFormer significantly improves the Macro F \textsubscript{1} value of detecting rare events in many business scenarios, from telecommunication and fraudulence to online education. In future studies, we will focus on improving the model performance by tuning the hyper-parameters like chunk size, learning rate, etc.

source_text: This paper is organized as follows: In Section 2 we prove under fairly reasonable assumptions that causal diffusion cannot satisfy the property of a strongly continuous semigroup. For the convenience of the reader we put some of the technical parts of the proof of the main theorem in the Appendix. Subsequently we present and analyze a causal diffusion model in Section 3 . The paper concludes with a short section of conclusion (Section 4 ).

suspicious_text: The assertion that $\hat{a}$ exhibits rotational symmetry is grounded in the premise that processes occurring in homogeneous and isotropic media inherently possess symmetry. Consequently, the fundamental solution $G_{0}$ of the governing evolution equation must assume rotational symmetry in $\mathbf{x}$, and this property is inherited by $\hat{a}$ as a direct consequence. Given that this work represents an initial exploration into the causality of semigroups and their associated evolution equations, our focus is appropriately narrowed to these specific cases.

[13] it was pointed out that an electric dipole moment (EDM) of an atom (or any quantum system) can be induced by the interaction of the electrons with a radial magnetic field ( ${\bf B}\propto{\bf r}$ ). The existence of such a field would contradict both time reflection invariance (T) and Gauss’s Law ( $\oint{\bf B}\cdot d{\bf a}=0$ ). The limit on such a field can be of interest by itself as a very precise test of electrodynamics at small distances since the accuracy of EDM measurements is very high now. It was also pointed out in [13] that a radial magnetic field can be produced due to a monopole-antimonopole pair contribution to the magnetic moment (if there is no magnetic string) and a time invariance violating interaction which polarizes nucleon spins along the radial direction.

source_text: There is a second argument in favor of the cancelation of the orbital contribution. As is known [4] , the radial magnetic field of a magnetic charge and the radial electric field of an electric charge together produce a nonzero angular momentum around the axis connecting the charges ( $\sim\bf{E}\times\bf{B}$ ). In Quantum Mechanics angular momentum is quantized. This requirement implies the quantization of the product of the magnetic and the electric charges. For two dyons this condition is $K=q_{1}M_{2}-q_{2}M_{1}=\frac{k}{2}$ , where $K=\bf{J}\cdot\bf{n}$ is the projection of the angular momentum $\bf{J}$ onto the axis connecting these particles and $\bf{n}$ is a unit vector along this axis. Recall that the system’s orbital angular momentum is orthogonal to the connecting axis and does not contribute to $K$ . We see that for $k=0$ the electromagnetic field angular momentum is zero due to the cancelation between the contributions of these two dyons.

source_text: The proof comes in two parts. In the first part, we prove the following claim. Let $\mathscr{W}$ be a family of initial parts of $T$ , with union $T$ , and such that, given $V,W\in\mathscr{W}$ , either $V$ is an initial part of $W$ , or vice-versa. Suppose further that each $W$ has a partition $P_{W}$ consisting wholly of open intervals of $W$ , with the property that if $V$ is an initial segment of $W$ then, for every $s\in P_{V}$ , there exists $t\in P_{W}$ with $s\subseteq t$ . Then $T$ has a partition $P$ consisting of open intervals only.

suspicious_text: The surface dynamics of these membrane-targeting domains can be effectively modeled using Lévy flights, which represent a type of random walk characterized by step displacements with a heavy-tailed distribution. This heavy-tailed behavior stems from the dissociation of molecules from the membrane, followed by a three-dimensional random walk until they reattach at a different location. The process is governed by the first return to the surface and adheres to a power law, as dictated by the Sparre-Andersen theorem (41). Importantly, this Lévy flight mechanism contrasts with Lévy walks driven by molecular motors in the cytoplasm, where active motion requires energy input, typically via ATP hydrolysis, whereas membrane dissociation and reattachment occur spontaneously.
source_text: The surface motion of these membrane-targeting domains is well described by Lévy flights, a random walk where the step displacements have a heavy-tailed distribution. The heavy tail arises from the dissociation of molecules from the membrane, which then perform a three-dimensional random walk until they reach the surface again at another location. The process involves the first return to a surface and it converges to a power law according to the Sparre-Andersen theorem (41) . This type of Lévy flight dynamics is fundamentally different from Lévy walks induced by molecular motors in the cytoplasm because periods of active motion require an energy input, typically in the form of ATP hydrolysis, while bulk excursions occur spontaneously.

source_text: In order to study the effect of the dissociation constant, we also employed a C2A construct fused to a non-membrane interacting glutathione S-transferase (GST), which has a strong tendency to dimerize (Figure 3 a). The GST-C2A dimer forms two independent interactions with the membrane and will consequently have a slower dissociation rate than C2A monomer, providing a good comparison for validating our superdiffusion predictions. Additionally, GST-C2A dimer has a higher viscous drag coefficient and, in turn, its diffusion coefficient on the membrane surface is reduced to nearly half (39) .

source_text: This paper is organized as follows: In Section 2 we prove under fairly reasonable assumptions that causal diffusion cannot satisfy the property of a strongly continuous semigroup. source_text: A radial magnetic field can induce a time invariance violating electric dipole moment (EDM) in quantum systems. The EDMs of the Tl, Cs, Xe and Hg atoms and the neutron that are produced by such a field are estimated. The contributions of such a field to the constants, $\chi$ of the T,P-odd interactions $\chi_{e}{\bf N}\cdot{\bf s}/s$ and $\chi_{N}{\bf N}\cdot{\bf I}/I$ are also estimated for the TlF, HgF and YbF molecules (where ${\bf s}$ ( ${\bf I}$ ) is the electron (nuclear) spin and ${\bf N}$ is the molecular axis). The best limit on the contact monopole field can be obtained from the measured value of the Tl EDM. The possibility of such a field being produced from polarization of the vacuum of electrically charged magnetic monopoles (dyons) by a Coulomb field is discussed, as well as the limit on these dyons. An alternative mechanism involves chromomagnetic and chromoelectric fields in QCD.

source_text: Let $\Delta(\{-1,1\}^{k})$ denote the space of all distributions on $\{-1,1\}^{k}$ . For a distribution $\mathcal{D}\in\Delta(\{-1,1\}^{k})$ and $\mathbf{x}\in\{-1,1\}^{k}$ , we use $\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{x})$ to denote the probability of sampling $\mathbf{x}$ in $\mathcal{D}$ . To a distribution $\mathcal{D}\in\Delta(\{-1,1\}^{k})$ we associate a canonical instance $\Psi_{\mathcal{D}}$ of $\textsf{Max-CSP}(f_{S,k})$ on $k$ variables as follows. Let $\mathbf{j}=(1,\ldots,k)$ . For every negation pattern $\mathbf{b}\in\{-1,1\}^{k}$ , $\Psi_{\mathcal{D}}$ contains the constraint $(\mathbf{b},\mathbf{j})$ with weight $\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{b})$ .

The relaxation times at the cluster's center and at its half-light radius $r_{h}$ are $5.8 \times 10^{8}$ and $4.6 \times 10^{9}$, respectively. According to Gnedin & Ostriker (1997), its destruction time is approximately 30 Hubble times. The cluster is located at a significant distance, 18.3 kpc from the Galactic center, at a height of 17.5 kpc above the Galactic plane. It is categorized among the very metal-poor clusters, with a metallicity of [Fe/H] = –1.99 (Harris 2003). The current ratio $r_{h}/r_{J}$ is 0.039, with a maximum value of 0.044 at the perigalactic distance of 15.5 kpc (Allen et al. 2006). These findings suggest that the cluster has consistently evolved well within its gravitational potential.

suspicious_text: The processing of lengthy sequence data poses significant challenges due to the inherent trade-off between preserving global context and maintaining local details within smaller sequence segments, while also requiring efficient computational management of these competing demands. To overcome these hurdles in real-time data, we introduce the ChunkFormer, a novel convolutional block transformer architecture designed to effectively balance and optimize the capture of both global and local information.
source_text: As mentioned in the earlier sections, handling long sequence data can be challenging due to the trade-off of capturing global information along the sequence without compromising local information contained within small segments of the sequence data. Tackling computational efficiently while managing both these aspects is another bottleneck. To address these challenges in real time data, we propose a convolution style block transformer series, ChunkFormer, that can maneuver both these challenges efficiently.

For $z\in(z_{I},z_{c})$ , we use the idea depicted in Figure 1 . Firstly, we assume that $f_{i}(z)\leq\Gamma_{i}$ and use this assumption to conclude bounds on various diagrams consisting of combinations of two-point functions. If these bounds turn out to be such that $f_{i}(z)\leq\gamma_{i}<\Gamma_{i}$ , then we can use Lemma 2.1 to conclude that $f_{i}(z)\leq\gamma_{i}$ for all $z<z_{c}$ . These bounds then imply the infrared bound. We extend the result to $z_{c}$ using a left-continuity property of the two-point function and the NoBLE coefficients, which we prove in the accompanying papers as these arguments are model-dependent.

suspicious_text: In an effort to further examine the horizontal branches (HBs) of Pal 3 and M 53, we included NGC 2419 as a comparative reference. Our analysis revealed a striking resemblance between the HB of M 53 and the upper HB of NGC 2419, with the sole distinguishable factor being their total cluster mass. This similarity suggests that, particularly in very metal-poor systems, the influence of total mass may be most pronounced during the initial evolutionary phases of globular clusters (GCs). These three clusters exemplify distinct evolutionary pathways, each indicative of unique developmental trajectories.
source_text: In this paper we show that a process modeled by a strongly continuous real-valued semigroup (that has a space convolution operator as infinitesimal generator) cannot satisfy causality. We present and analyze a causal model of diffusion that satisfies the semigroup property at a discrete set of time points $M:=\{\tau_{m}\,|\,m\in{\mathbb{N}}_{0}\}$ and that is in contrast to the classical diffusion model not smooth. More precisely, if $v$ denotes the concentration of a substance diffusing with constant speed, then $v$ is continuous but its time derivative is discontinuous at the discrete set $M$ of time points. It is this property of diffusion that forbids the classical limit procedure that leads to the noncausal diffusion model in Stochastics. Furthermore, we show that diffusion with constant speed satisfies an inhomogeneous wave equation with a time dependent coefficient.

suspicious_text: Research has also shown that energy transfer is significantly enhanced when the environmental spectral function exhibits a strong peak around the energy differences between excitonic excited states, a characteristic feature of molecular vibrations. The unique properties of these structured spectral functions, including their multi-scale linear response functions and non-perturbative coupling to excited states, enable complex and non-stationary modifications to excited state dynamics, distinct from the behavior observed in simple heat baths. This can lead to phenomena such as transient detailed balance violation, extended electronic coherence times, and vibronic mixing of electronic states. To accurately capture the dissipative dynamics of systems interacting with such environments, sophisticated numerical and analytical methods are required, prompting the development and application of advanced techniques, including many-body methods and master equation formulations.

source_text: Percolation is a central model in statistical physics and is a very active field of research since its rigorous definition by Broadbent and Hammersley in 1957 [10] , where this model was proposed to describe the spread of a fluid through a medium. General references for percolation are [6] . A review of recent results can be found in [21] and the references therein. We consider Bernoulli percolation on the hypercubic lattice. We use the definition of [43] : To each nearest-neighbor bond $\{x,y\}$ we associate an independent Bernoulli random variable $n_{\{x,y\}}$ which takes the value $1$ with probability $p$ and the value $0$ with probability $1-p$ , where $p\in[0,1]$ . If $n_{\{x,y\}}=1$ , then we say that the bond $\{x,y\}$ is open , and otherwise we say that it is closed . A configuration is a realization of the random variables of all bonds. The joint probability distribution is denoted by $\prob_{p}$ with corresponding expectation $\expec_{p}$ .

suspicious_text: In the context of algebraic geometry, consider a projective variety $Z$ endowed with Cartier divisors $L$ and $D$. Suppose $Y$ is a closed subscheme of $Z$ that is both irreducible and reduced, with $\mathcal{I}_{Y}$ denoting its ideal sheaf. Given the conditions that the difference in global sections of $L$ and its restriction to $Y$ is positive, as well as the analogous condition for $D$, a relationship between the dimensions of certain cohomology groups emerges. Specifically, when $h^{0}(L)$ exceeds $h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L))$ and $h^{0}(D)$ exceeds $h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(D))$, the sum of these excesses is bounded above by the corresponding excess for the sum of the divisors $L$ and $D$, plus one, as expressed by the inequality $h^{0}(L)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L))+h^{0}(D)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(D))\leq h^{0}(L+D)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L+D))+1$.
source_text:
Suppose $Z$ is a projective variety. Let $L$ and $D$ be cartier divisors on $Z$ . Assume $Y$ is an irreducible and reduced closed subscheme of $Z$ and denote $\mathcal{I}_{Y}$ the ideal sheaf of $Y$ in $Z$ . If $h^{0}(L)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L))>0$ and $h^{0}(D)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(D))>0$ , then we have 
 
 $h^{0}(L)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L))+h^{0}(D)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(D))\leq h^{0}(L+D)-h^{0}(\mathcal{I}_{Y}(L+D))+1.$

suspicious_text: The NoBLE framework is grounded in a systematic four-stage process. Initially, the perturbative lace expansion is formulated for the specific model under consideration. Subsequently, bounds on the perturbative components are established through diagrammatic analysis. In the third phase, the derived expansion is subjected to rigorous examination to ascertain the infrared bound, contingent upon certain foundational assumptions about the expansion's behavior. Importantly, within this analytical framework, bounds on the lace-expansion coefficients are expressed in terms of integrals associated with simple random walks, which are computationally tractable. This step enables the calculation of numerical constraints on the expansion coefficients. Finally, the fourth stage involves the precise computation of these simple random walk integrals.
source_text: A NoBLE analysis consists of four steps: Firstly, for a given model, we derive the perturbative lace expansion. Secondly, we prove diagrammatic bounds on the perturbation. Thirdly, we analyze the expansion to conclude the infrared bound given certain assumptions on the expansion. In our analysis, we derive diagrammatic bounds on the lace-expansion coefficients in terms of simple random walk integrals, which can be computed explicitly. This allows us to compute numerical bounds on the lace-expansion coefficients. The fourth step consists of the numerical computation of these SRW-integrals.

suspicious_text: The application of time series analysis has accelerated in various industries, including telecommunications, advertising, online education, and sales, to uncover the underlying drivers of trends and patterns that emerge over time. Techniques rooted in deep learning, particularly those leveraging Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), have become the standard approach for analyzing sequential data. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) models, which are variants of RNNs, are commonly employed to identify dependencies within historical sequences and forecast the probability of future events. Nevertheless, traditional RNNs pose training challenges due to the issues of vanishing and exploding gradients, which can render the model unstable and hinder its ability to capture long-range relationships between input elements in extended sequential data.

suspicious_text: The study of the adjoint linear system $|K_{X}+L|$ on a smooth projective complex surface $X$ with divisor $L$ is a fundamental problem, with its behavior largely dictated by the positivity of $L$. Established methods, such as Reider's approach for positive $L$ and Beauville's work for $L=0$, exist, but the case of negative $L$ remains less understood, corresponding to special divisors characterized by effectiveness and the condition $h^{0}(K_{X}-D)>0$. Developing a comprehensive approach to analyze such special linear systems on surfaces necessitates the establishment of a Clifford-type theorem, addressing the current gap in methodology for this specific scenario.
source_text: Let $X$ be a smooth projective complex surface and $L$ a divisor on it. One of the fundamental problems in the surface case is to study the adjoint linear system $|K_{X}+L|$ . Roughly speaking, the behavior of this linear system depends on the positivity of $L$ . When $L$ is positive, we have a celebrated method of Reider [15] (see also [4] and [16] ). When $L$ is zero, the canonical system has also been studied systematically by Beauville [2] . When $L$ is negative, the linear system corresponds to the special divisors on a curve. Exactly, we say a divisor $D$ on $X$ a special divisor if it is effective and $h^{0}(K_{X}-D)>0$ . However, for surfaces, we have no general method to study such special divisors. In order to find a powerful method to study special linear systems in the surface case, we need to establish first a Clifford-type theorem.

The main goal of this section is to prove Theorem1.7 , giving a simple “bias-based” sketching algorithm for threshold functions $\mathsf{Th}^{i}_{k}$ . Given an instance $\Psi$ of $\textsf{Max-CSP}(\mathsf{Th}^{i}_{k})$ , for $i\in[n]$ , let $\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi)$ denote the total weight of clauses in which $x_{i}$ appears positively minus the weight of those in which it appears negatively; that is, if $\Psi$ consists of clauses $(\mathbf{b}(1),\mathbf{j}(1)),\ldots,(\mathbf{b}(m),\mathbf{j}(m))$ with weights $w_{1},\ldots,w_{m}$ , then 
 
 $\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mathrm{\small def}}}{{=}}\sum_{\ell\in[m]\text{ s.t. }j(\ell)_{t}=i\text{ for some }t\in[k]}b(\ell)_{t}w_{\ell}.$ 
 Let $\textsf{bias}(\Psi)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\mathrm{\small def}}}{{=}}\frac{1}{kW}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi)|$ , where $W=\sum_{\ell=1}^{m}w_{\ell}$ is the total weight in $\Psi$ .

source_text: Glass coverslips were cleaned by sonication in a detergent solution followed by soaking in 1M KOH. The coverslips were rinsed extensively in Milli-Q water and blown dry with a stream of nitrogen gas. Then, the coverslips were treated with an oxygen plasma. Immediately after the plasma cleaning, a perfusion chamber (CoverWell, Grace Bio-Labs) was adhered to the coverslip. In order to deposit the lipid bilayers a solution of SUVs (1.5-mM lipid) composed of phosphatidylcholine (PC) and phosphatidylserine (PS) at a 3:1 ratio in 1M NaCl and imaging buffer was introduced into the perfusion chamber and incubated for one hour at 4 \textsuperscript{∘} C. Refrigeration minimizes lipid oxidation. The surface was then rinsed with imaging buffer multiple times prior to addition of protein sample.

suspicious_text: Figure 1 illustrates the symmetry energy derived from the single-particle potential in Equation (3) with varying $x$ parameters across low and high densities. Given that the symmetry energy is relatively well-constrained below saturation density, we adopt a fixed form for the low-density symmetry energy using $x = 1$, which is consistent with current constraints. In contrast, the high-density symmetry energy remains poorly constrained, prompting us to explore a range of $x$ values (-1, 1, 2) that encompass current uncertainties. Additionally, simulations are conducted without the high-density symmetry energy by equating neutron and proton densities ($\rho_{n}=\rho_{p}=(\rho_{n}+\rho_{p})/2$) and potentials ($U_{n}=U_{p}=(U_{n}+U_{p})/2$).
source_text: The data scarcity issue, as in the case of cough detection dataset, is also present for the diagnosis model, but compounded with the additional problem of labeled cough audio being considerably more scarce. Since the existing database for detection is comprised primarily of unlabeled cough audio with no identified illness relation, the same cannot be used as diagnosis training data. To train our cough diagnosis system, we collected cough sounds from 35 bronchiolitis, 131 pertussis and 102 bronchitis patients. The performance and reliability of the diagnosis system is likely to improve as more data becomes available. However, even with small training data, very promising results have been observed on unseen test samples, as reported later in diagnosis results section.

suspicious_text: In our approach, we employ a type of reinforcement learning that allows for a disconnect between the policy used to generate experiences and the policy being trained. This separation enables us to create an exploration strategy by combining the learning policy with specialized sub-skill controllers to form a distinct behavior policy. A key benefit of this method is that it eliminates the requirement for the controller during actual deployment, as the learned policy can function independently once it has been fully trained.
source_text: It can furthermore be observed that samples of encrypted user traffic, assuming that strong encryption is used, in itself does not leak any private or confidential information, hence can be expected to have low entropy variance. Ordinary non-encrypted user traffic, can however be expected to show a significant variance in entropy between different samples, as illustrated in Figure V.2 . This indicates that entropy variance may be an interesting metric for measuring whether IDS alarms leak information, in particular for buffer overflow type of attacks. However this metric does obviously not understand the semantics of the data traffic, and can therefore not be used to evaluate whether the leaked information is private or confidential.

source_text: The classical Brill-Noether theory is to study special divisors or linear systems on an algebraic curve, and the Clifford theorem is the first step of the theory (cf. [1] ). The main purpose of this paper is to generalize the Clifford theorem to algebraic surfaces.

suspicious_text: The dependence of $\mu(Z)$ has been quantitatively evaluated by Manson and by Fano, Theodosiou, and Dehmer, utilizing the Hartree-Slater model to approximate the ionic core potential of an atom. While the periodic fluctuations in $\mu(Z)$ are largely masked by shell effects, which are accounted for in the Hartree-Slater model, Fano, Theodosiou, and Dehmer contend that these fluctuations are indeed present and correlated with the radial nodes of the function $\chi$ in the vicinity of $r_{0}$, as well as the slope of the $\mu(Z)$ dependence. Given the relationship between the nodal structure of $\chi$ and binding properties as dictated by the oscillation theorem, it is plausible that systematic periodic variations in Rydberg spectra as a function of $Z$ may be linked to the Zel'dovich effect.
source_text: The $\mu(Z)$ dependence has been numerically computed by Manson (11) and by Fano, Theodosiou and Dehmer (12) who used the Hartree-Slater model (13) to approximate the potential of the ionic core of the atom. Although the periodic variations of $\mu(Z)$ are strongly obscured by the shell effects (included in the Hartree-Slater model), Fano, Theodosiou and Dehmer argued that they are there and that there is a correlation between the location of radial nodes of the function $\chi$ from Eq.( 1 ) near $r_{0}$ and the slope of the $\mu(Z)$ dependence. In view of the oscillation theorem (4) the nodal structure of the function $\chi$ is intimately related to the binding properties which suggests that systematic periodic variations of Rydberg spectra as function of $Z$ might be related to the Zel’dovich effect.

suspicious_text: This paper is structured as follows: the proof of Theorem 0.1 is presented in Section 1, while Section 2 is dedicated to establishing Clifford-type inequalities on a surface, as outlined in Propositions 2.1 and 2.3, and proving Theorem 0.2. The subsequent section utilizes these inequalities to define two indices, α(X) and β(X), which are analogous to the Clifford index for curves, and examines their fundamental properties, including bounds as stated in Propositions 3.6 and 3.8. A comprehensive analysis of X is provided in Section 4 when α and β are zero, yielding Theorems 4.1 and 4.2. Finally, Section 5 leverages the established inequalities to derive bounds for the number of moduli of surfaces, culminating in Theorem 5.2.
source_text: There is a second argument in favor of the cancelation of the orbital contribution. As is known [4] , the radial magnetic field of a magnetic charge and the radial electric field of an electric charge together produce a nonzero angular momentum around the axis connecting the charges ( $\sim\bf{E}\times\bf{B}$ ). In Quantum Mechanics angular momentum is quantized. This requirement implies the quantization of the product of the magnetic and the electric charges. For two dyons this condition is $K=q_{1}M_{2}-q_{2}M_{1}=\frac{k}{2}$ , where $K=\bf{J}\cdot\bf{n}$ is the projection of the angular momentum $\bf{J}$ onto the axis connecting these particles and $\bf{n}$ is a unit vector along this axis. Recall that the system’s orbital angular momentum is orthogonal to the connecting axis and does not contribute to $K$ . We see that for $k=0$ the electromagnetic field angular momentum is zero due to the cancelation between the contributions of these two dyons.

Let $\Psi$ be an instance of $\textsf{Max-CSP}(\mathsf{Th}^{i}_{k})$ and let $\mathbf{a} = (a_1, \ldots, a_n) \in \{-1,1\}^n$. Then:
1. For each $i \in [n]$, the quantity $\mathsf{diff}_i(\Psi^{\mathbf{a}})$ equals $a_i \mathsf{diff}_i(\Psi)$.
2. The bias of $\Psi$ is equal to the bias of $\Psi^{\mathbf{a}}$.
3. For any assignment $\bm{\sigma} \in \{-1,1\}^n$, the value $\textsf{val}_{\Psi^{\mathbf{a}}}(\bm{\sigma})$ is equal to $\textsf{val}_{\Psi}(\mathbf{a} \odot \bm{\sigma})$.
4. The value function $\textsf{val}_{\Psi^{\mathbf{a}}}$ is identical to $\textsf{val}_{\Psi}$.

suspicious_text: A consistent observation over recent years is that all globular clusters (GCs) subjected to spectroscopic analysis exhibit multiple stellar populations. This conclusion is underscored by the comprehensive study of Carretta et al. (2009), which analyzed approximately 2,000 stars across 19 GCs, revealing that all clusters demonstrate the sodium–oxygen anticorrelation. This phenomenon serves as a marker for the existence of a stellar population enriched in sodium and depleted in oxygen compared to halo stars of the same metallicity. The Na–O anti-correlation is characteristic of GCs, with their member stars belonging to two or more distinct populations that differ in the abundances of elements produced by the hot CNO cycle and other proton-capture processes. These chemical signatures are also evident in turn-off stars and subgiants, as noted by Gratton et al. (2001), indicating that they cannot be attributed to internal stellar mixing but must instead result from self-enrichment processes

suspicious_text: The foundational Brill-Noether theory revolves around the examination of exceptional divisors and linear systems situated on algebraic curves, with the Clifford theorem serving as its inaugural milestone, as elucidated in relevant literature. This manuscript seeks to extend the scope of the Clifford theorem, broadening its applicability to encompass algebraic surfaces, thereby constituting a significant advancement in the field.
source_text: The classical Brill-Noether theory is to study special divisors or linear systems on an algebraic curve, and the Clifford theorem is the first step of the theory (cf. [1] ). The main purpose of this paper is to generalize the Clifford theorem to algebraic surfaces.

Conversely, the contact switching module adheres to a predetermined, manually crafted trajectory, the velocity profile of which is illustrated in Fig 4(b), and is specifically designed to facilitate the detachment and subsequent reattachment of the fingertip to the object. Furthermore, the velocities of the roll joint and middle flexion joint are subject to random sampling within a bounded range of $0.1$ to $0.4$ rad/s, introducing an element of stochasticity to the control strategy.

source_text: The mechanisms of radial magnetic field and EDM creation can be much simpler if the magnetic monopole has an electric charge or is subject to the strong interaction. The simplest example would be for a magnetic charge to be captured by an atomic nucleus and hence produce a “Coulomb” magnetic field ${\bf B}=\frac{M{\bf r}}{r^{3}}$ . It is easy to find the exact solution of this problem (an electron in the field of the dyon) in the nonrelativistic case. All stationary states in this problem possess an EDM. Another example is a dyon-antidyon system with nonzero orbital angular momentum. Similarly to the way in which orbiting electric charges produce a magnetic moment, orbiting magnetic charges produce an EDM [9] .

The relatively limited data volume across numerous categories necessitates the use of multiple evaluation criteria for a comprehensive understanding of the model's effectiveness. source_text: For a smooth connected projective curve, we have an invariant, the Clifford index, introduced by Martens [14] . It plays an important role in the study of curves. Because of Theorems 0.2 and 0.1 , we can define two indices of Clifford type on a smooth minimal surface X of general type.

suspicious_text: Observations suggest that encrypted user traffic samples, utilizing robust encryption, do not inherently disclose sensitive information and are likely to exhibit minimal entropy variance. In contrast, non-encrypted traffic samples tend to display substantial variance in entropy, as depicted in Figure V.2. This disparity implies that entropy variance could serve as a valuable metric for assessing information leakage in IDS alerts, particularly in the context of buffer overflow attacks. Nevertheless, this metric is limited by its inability to comprehend the semantic context of the data, thereby precluding its use in determining the sensitivity or confidentiality of the leaked information.
source_text: It can furthermore be observed that samples of encrypted user traffic, assuming that strong encryption is used, in itself does not leak any private or confidential information, hence can be expected to have low entropy variance. Ordinary non-encrypted user traffic, can however be expected to show a significant variance in entropy between different samples, as illustrated in Figure V.2 . This indicates that entropy variance may be an interesting metric for measuring whether IDS alarms leak information, in particular for buffer overflow type of attacks. However this metric does obviously not understand the semantics of the data traffic, and can therefore not be used to evaluate whether the leaked information is private or confidential.

suspicious_text: The trajectories shown in Figure 2a are derived from a 10-second observation window, superimposed on the final frame. Notably, particle trajectories often exhibit pronounced jumps, as illustrated in Figures 2b and 2c. These jumps indicate that C2A molecules may temporarily detach from the surface, reattaching after short excursions into the liquid bulk. Since motion in the bulk is significantly faster than diffusion in the viscous membrane, these jumps appear instantaneous for practical purposes. Specifically, the diffusion coefficient ($D_{s}$) for the C2A domain in the lipid bilayer is approximately 2 $\mu\mathrm{m}^{2}/\mathrm{s}$, whereas in liquid, the diffusion coefficient ($D_{b}$) is approximately 100 times higher (38). Consequently, during a jump through the bulk, a molecule may sometimes be observed at reduced intensity in both its original and new positions within a single imaging frame, as demonstrated in Figure 2b.
source_text: Figure 2 a shows an example of trajectories obtained in a 10-s window, overlaid on the last frame. Often, long jumps are observed in the particle trajectories as seen in the examples in Figs. 2 b and c. These jumps suggest the C2A molecules detach from the surface and readsorb after brief excursions into the liquid bulk. The motion in the bulk is much faster than diffusion on the viscous membrane and jumps are thus expected to occur instantaneously for all practical purposes. For the C2A domain, the diffusion coefficient in the lipid bilayer $D_{s}$ is of the order of 2 $\mu\mathrm{m}^{2}/\mathrm{s}$ , but in liquid the diffusion coefficient $D_{b}$ is estimated to be 100 times higher (38) . As a consequence, when a molecule performs a jump through the bulk it can sometimes be observed at reduced intensity in both the old and new locations within the same imaging frame, as seen in Figure 2 b.

suspicious_text: In a more streamlined scenario, the intricacies of radial magnetic field generation and Electric Dipole Moment (EDM) formation could be significantly reduced if magnetic monopoles were to exhibit electric charge or be influenced by the strong force. A hypothetical instance of this phenomenon would involve a magnetic charge being enclosed by an atomic nucleus, resulting in a "Coulomb-like" magnetic field ${\bf B}=\frac{M{\bf r}}{r^{3}}$ . In non-relativistic situations, this problem can be precisely solved (electron in the field of a dyon). All stationary states in this scenario feature an EDM. Additionally, a dyon-antidyon system with non-zero orbital angular momentum presents a parallel scenario, where the spinning magnetic charges generate an EDM, analogous to the production of a magnetic moment by orbiting electric charges [9] .
source_text: The mechanisms of radial magnetic field and EDM creation can be much simpler if the magnetic monopole has an electric charge or is subject to the strong interaction. The simplest example would be for a magnetic charge to be captured by an atomic nucleus and hence produce a “Coulomb” magnetic field ${\bf B}=\frac{M{\bf r}}{r^{3}}$ . It is easy to find the exact solution of this problem (an electron in the field of the dyon) in the nonrelativistic case. All stationary states in this problem possess an EDM. Another example is a dyon-antidyon system with nonzero orbital angular momentum. Similarly to the way in which orbiting electric charges produce a magnetic moment, orbiting magnetic charges produce an EDM [9] .

The following algorithm, when given an input $\Psi$, outputs an assignment with an expected value of at least $\alpha(\mathsf{Th}^{i}_{k}) \cdot \textsf{val}_{\Psi}$: Assign each variable to $1$ if $\mathsf{diff}_{i}(\Psi) \geq 0$, and to $-1$ otherwise. Subsequently, flip the assignment of each variable independently with probability $p^{*}$.

suspicious_text: Kleene lattices, initially proposed by J. A. Kalman under a different designation, constitute a specialized subset of De Morgan lattices that provide an algebraic framework for a specific propositional logic adhering to the double negation principle, yet not necessarily the law of excluded middle. In instances where the foundational lattice lacks distributivity, these structures are referred to as pseudo-Kleene lattices. A key inquiry revolves around the feasibility of introducing specific binary operations within Kleene or pseudo-Kleene lattices such that they form an adjoint pair, a challenge addressed through the application of the full twist-product construction and an additional method that expands a distributive lattice into a Kleene lattice.
source_text: Kleene lattices were introduced by J. A. Kalman ( [7] ) (under a different name) as a special kind of De Morgan lattices which serve as an algebraic axiomatization of a certain propositional logic satisfying the double negation law but not necessary the excluded middle law. If the underlying lattice is not distributive such lattices are called pseudo-Kleene (see e.g. [4] ). It is a question if certain binary operations can be introduced in a Kleene or pseudo-Kleene lattice such that they form an adjoint pair. To solve this problem, we apply an approach using the full twist-product construction and another construction extending a distributive lattice into a Kleene one.

suspicious_text: A straightforward photometric criterion, specifically the extension of the horizontal branch (HB), was employed to identify candidate foreground (FG) clusters from pre-existing extensive databases of color-magnitude (CM) diagrams of globular clusters (GCs). The resulting list of 17 candidates is provisionally categorized into two groups: 8 FG-only clusters, which are incapable of forming a secondary generation (SG), and 9 mainly-FG clusters, which could potentially form an SG but have not lost the majority of their initial FG mass. The limited proportion of mainly-FG clusters suggests that very few clusters developing an SG retain a significant fraction of their initial mass. We propose that non-isolated clusters are likely disrupted due to dynamical interactions with the Galactic tidal field unless they are able to form an SG, whose dynamical mixing with the FG stars facilitates cluster survival, as described in D’Antona & Ventura (2008).

The dynamics of a particle with a Cauchy propagator are particularly interesting because the theoretical variance of the displacements diverges, 
 
 $\langle r^{2}(t)\rangle=\int_{0}^{\infty}(2\pi r)r^{2}P(\textbf{r})dr=\infty.$  (6) 
 In practice, a diverging second moment implies that there is a non-negligible probability for the occurrence of extremely long jumps and this phenomenon has direct implications in the measured MSD. Figure 4 b shows the ensemble-averaged MSD computed from the numerical simulations. The MSD increases in a superlinear fashion, i.e. by employing equation ( 1 ), we have $\alpha>1$ , which implies the process is superdiffusive.

source_text: In this paper we show that a process modeled by a strongly continuous real-valued semigroup (that has a space convolution operator as infinitesimal generator) cannot satisfy causality. We present and analyze a causal model of diffusion that satisfies the semigroup property at a discrete set of time points $M:=\{\tau_{m}\,|\,m\in{\mathbb{N}}_{0}\}$ and that is in contrast to the classical diffusion model not smooth. More precisely, if $v$ denotes the concentration of a substance diffusing with constant speed, then $v$ is continuous but its time derivative is discontinuous at the discrete set $M$ of time points. It is this property of diffusion that forbids the classical limit procedure that leads to the noncausal diffusion model in Stochastics. Furthermore, we show that diffusion with constant speed satisfies an inhomogeneous wave equation with a time dependent coefficient.

suspicious_text: While the time-averaged mean squared displacement (MSD) for individual trajectories exhibits linearity, the ensemble-averaged MSD $\langle r^{2}(t)\rangle$ does not. The superdiffusive behavior can be explained by decomposing the motion into two independent processes: $\textbf{r}(t)=\textbf{b}(t)+\textbf{y}(t)$, where $\textbf{b}(t)$ represents a two-dimensional Brownian motion and $\textbf{y}(t)$ is a Lévy process with a probability density function given by equation (5). Consequently, the MSD is expressed as $\langle\textbf{r}^{2}\rangle=\langle\textbf{b}^{2}\rangle+\langle\textbf{y}^{2}\rangle$. While the first term grows linearly with time, the second term demonstrates a superdiffusive characteristic (40).
source_text: Although the time-averaged MSD for individual trajectories is linear, the ensemble averaged MSD $\langle r^{2}(t)\rangle$ is not. We can understand the superdiffusive behaviour by assuming we can define the motion in terms of two independent processes $\textbf{r}(t)=\textbf{b}(t)+\textbf{y}(t)$ , where $\textbf{b}(t)$ is a two-dimensional Brownian motion and $\textbf{y}(t)$ is a Lévy process with a probability density defined by equation ( 5 ). Then the MSD is $\langle\textbf{r}^{2}\rangle=\langle\textbf{b}^{2}\rangle+\langle\textbf{y}^{2}\rangle$ . The first term is linear in time but the second term has a superdiffusive nature (40) .

suspicious_text: Assuming a constant learning rate $\eta$ and bounded variance terms $\varsigma$ and $\sigma$ in the SPMD method, we derive an upper bound on the expected suboptimality of the policy obtained after $k$ iterations. Specifically, we show that the expected difference between the objective function values of the learned policy and the optimal policy is bounded by a term that decreases with the number of iterations $k$, plus additional terms that depend on the initial suboptimality, variance bounds, and the learning rate. Furthermore, if the number of iterations is fixed and the learning rate is chosen optimally, the bound can be simplified to depend only on the initial suboptimality, variance bounds, and the number of iterations, with a convergence rate of $O(1/\sqrt{k})$.

suspicious_text: In Section IV, we present the development of an efficient Hamiltonian for fermion-boson systems under a large-N limit. We derive the corresponding equations of motion and delve into the unique quantum dynamics of these systems in this limit. The semiclassical approximation is employed to uncover the general structures of collective modes. In Section V, we illustrate the connection between delocalization and superfluidity by demonstrating the emergence of Off-Diagonal Long-Range Order (ODLO). Our calculations suggest that strong repulsive interactions between bosons or Cooper pairs do not nullify the superfluid density to zero in specific conditions. In Section VI, we scrutinize the existence of hidden orders in certain Mott states and highlight various topological excitations in Mott states, which are yet to be experimentally verified.

suspicious_text: We adopt the approach of Latter, assuming the atomic ion potential $U_{s}(r)$ can be approximated using the Thomas-Fermi theory, which yields an expression for $U_{s}(r)$ in terms of a universal function $\phi(y)$ that satisfies a nonlinear differential equation with specified boundary conditions. The ion size $r_{0}$ and range parameter $x_{0}$ are determined by the condition that the Thomas-Fermi potential matches the Coulomb potential of a unit charge, leading to an equation that implies $Z^{1/3}$ is the natural variable characterizing the potential strength. The Thomas-Fermi theory predicts a typical length scale of $a_{B}/Z^{1/3}$ and a potential magnitude of $(\hbar^{2}/ma_{B}^{2})Z^{4/3}$, resulting in a dimensionless coupling constant $w$ of order $Z^{2/3}$, which is analogous to the parameter $\zeta$ used in the rectangular well example, with $Z^{1/3}$ playing a similar role to $\zeta \simeq w^{1/2}$.
source_text: The phenomenon of Feshbach resonances in ultra cold atomic gases has attracted much attention. The Zeeman-field-driven two-body resonances between fermion pairs in open channels and bound molecules provide a fascinating way to tune the scattering length between atoms in open channels. Remarkably, this sort of simple two-body physics results in extremely rich quantum many-body states in atomic vapors which have not been explicitly observed in conventional solid state systems. Indeed, by varying the two-body scattering length near Feshbach resonances, several groups have successfully achieved fermionic superfluids in a strongly interacting regime (1) .

suspicious_text: The experimental setup involves computing the mean and 95% confidence interval from a large ensemble of 10,000 trials, with each trial estimating the standard deviation across 50 samples for varying input data lengths measured in bits, as this sample size is considered a practical minimum for distinguishing between encrypted and unencrypted data with reasonable reliability. Using fewer samples per trial would result in a broader confidence interval, necessitating longer payloads to achieve reliable detection of encrypted content, thereby highlighting a trade-off between payload length and sample size requirements for accurate encrypted content identification.
source_text: The experiment configuration calculates the average and the 95% confidence band ( $\pm 2\sigma)$ from an ensemble of 10000 experiments. Each experiment calculates the standard deviation over 50 samples for varying input data length in bits, assuming that this is the smallest number of samples that in practice will be used to reliably distinguish between encrypted and plaintext data. If less samples are used per experiment, then the confidence band will widen out, meaning that longer payload will be needed to reliably distinguish between encrypted and plaintext data. There is in other words a tradeoff between the payload length and the number of samples required to reliably detect encrypted content.

Suppose $w$ belongs to the set $K$ and $n$ is a natural number. We aim to identify a point $z$ within the domain $D$ such that the distance $d_{A}(w,z)$ is less than $1/n$. To proceed, assume that $w$ is not an element of $D$ and define the set 

$M\;=\;\{\min K,\max K\}\cup\bigcup_{k=1}^{n}M_{k}.$ 

If $w$ lies in the interval $(x,y)$, which is an element of $A_{k}$ for some $k\leq n$, choose the largest such $k$. If no such $k$ exists, set $k=0$. Since $M$ is closed and contains $\min K$ and $\max K$, there exist points $u,v\in M$ such that $(u,v)$ does not intersect $M$ and contains $w$. Now, consider any $j$ with $k<j\leq n$ and suppose $(x,y)\in A_{j}$. It must hold that $f_{x,y}(u)=f_{x,y}(w)=f_{x,y}(v)$. This is because $x$ and $y$ are elements of $M$, and since $(u,v)$ is disjoint from $M$, either $x\leq u$ and $v\leq y$, or $y\leq u$, or $v\leq x$. As demonstrated in Theorem 3, causal diffusion does not satisfy the semigroup property for all time points \(t > 0\). In this section, we introduce a diffusion model that adheres to the following criteria:  
- The semigroup property is satisfied for a discrete set of time points \(\{\tau_n | n \in \mathbb{N}_0\}\),

- The total mass remains conserved and

By examining Bellman’s optimality condition for dynamic programming ( [3] and Chapter 6 of [19] ), we can show the existence of a policy $\pi^{*}$ which satisfies ( 1.6 ) simultaneously for all $s\in{\cal S}$ . Hence, we can formulate ( 1.6 ) as an optimization problem with a single objective by taking the weighted sum of $V^{\pi}$ over $s$ (with weights $\rho_{s}>0$ and $\textstyle\sum_{s\in S}\rho_{s}=1$ ): 
 
 $\begin{array}[]{ll}\min_{\pi}&\mathbb{E}_{s\sim\rho}[V^{\pi}(s)]\\
\mbox{s.t.}&\pi(\cdot|s)\in\Delta_{|{\cal A}|},\forall s\in{\cal S}.\end{array}$  (1.8) 
 While the weights $\rho$ can be arbitrarily chosen, a reasonable selection of $\rho$ would be the stationary state distribution induced by the optimal policy $\pi^{*}$ , denoted by $\nu^{*}\equiv\nu(\pi^{*})$ . As such, problem ( 1.8 ) reduces to 
 
 $\begin{array}[]{ll}\min_{\pi}&\left\{f(\pi):=\mathbb{E}_{s\sim\nu^{*}}[V^{\pi}(s)]\right\}\\
\mbox{s.t.}&\pi(\cdot|s)\in\Delta_{|{\cal A}|},\forall s\in{\cal S}.\end{array}$

This approach contrasts with previous methodologies, such as [11], which relied on binary-class models to diagnose specific diseases by distinguishing between the target disease and a non-disease class. Where multiple CM diagrams are available, their results are cross-checked to ensure accuracy. This process helped identify and exclude clusters that initially appeared to host FG characteristics. source_text: After this first screening, we are left with 86 CM diagrams, among which FG clusters must be identified. Only one cluster is left with a red HB (NGC 6652). For the others, whose HB is blue, we select those in which the extension in V magnitude of the HB does not exceed 1 mag. We further exclude the clusters (with a short blue HB) for which the O–Na anticorrelation has been observed, namely: M30, NGC 6397 Carretta et al. (2009) and NGC 7492 Cohen & Melendez (2005) . When available, we checked the results on more than one CM diagram. These checks allowed to eliminate clusters that in a first moment looked like FG. A clear example is given by NGC 6535, that looks like hosting a blue HB of about 1 visual magnitude range in the data by Testa et al. (2001) . A look at the photometry by Sarajedini (1994) shows that the HB extends for $\sim$ 1.8 visual magnitudes, and, further, that it contains two very faint extreme HB stars. On this basis, this cluster was excluded.

This observed periodicity bears a striking resemblance to the $Z^{1/3}$ oscillations that modulate the ground-state energy of atoms, deviating from the expected systematic behavior. Second, from a mathematical perspective, it is intriguing to consider whether a causal variant of the Schrödinger equation could be formulated, ensuring that phenomena like quantum tunneling adhere to causality. suspicious_text: Our approach utilizes controllers that are straightforward to design and query, eliminating the need for complex motion planning and minimizing computational expenses. Although these controllers may exhibit suboptimal behavior, our methodology enables effective learning outcomes despite their limitations. The key advantages of our method include the ability to learn dexterous manipulation tasks, such as finger-gaiting, using a set of simple sub-skill controllers. Notably, our approach allows for the training of an end-to-end policy that can operate independently of the controllers used during training, thereby reducing constraints related to controller performance and sensory input. Furthermore, we demonstrate that suboptimal controllers can facilitate efficient exploration of complex state spaces without relying on exploratory reset distributions, thus offering a more flexible and efficient solution.
source_text: Critically, we use controllers that are simple to design and inexpensive to query actions from. Our controllers do not involve multi-step horizon motion planning or other sources of complexity and computational cost. This makes them inexpensive to compute - favorably reducing the cost of querying the expert. However, inexpensive controllers will often be sub-optimal in their behavior. Still, our method enables learning despite using such sub-optimal controllers. suspicious_text: The conventional paradigm for analyzing time series data has historically relied on traditional methodologies, supplemented by non-stationary analysis techniques rooted in Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) models. However, the emergence of Deep Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) has ushered in a new era of time series forecasting, offering a robust framework for capturing complex temporal dynamics [5]. By parsing sequential data, these models are capable of distilling nuanced relationships between temporal events. Notably, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks [6] and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) [7] have achieved benchmark performance in numerous time series applications, thanks to the incorporation of forgetting gates that modulate information flow and effectively mitigate the limitations of traditional RNNs, which are often plagued by short-term memory constraints.

This paper focuses on constructing and examining a causal model of diffusion. The study is divided into two main sections. In the first part, it is demonstrated that causal diffusion cannot adhere to the strongly continuous semigroup property or its corresponding evolution equation. Specifically, the paper shows that a process governed by the scalar-valued evolution equation

\[
\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} - \mathcal{A}v = 0 \quad \text{for } t > 0, \quad \text{with} \quad v|_{t=0} = u,
\]

where the space convolution operator \(\mathcal{A}\) serves as the infinitesimal generator of a strongly continuous semigroup (as referenced in [2]), fails to satisfy causality. This means there exists a fundamental solution \(G: \mathbb{R}^{N} \times \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) for the equation above that does not meet the condition

\[
\text{supp}(G) \subseteq \{(\mathbf{x}, t) \in \mathbb{R}^{N} \times [0, \infty) \, | \, |\mathbf{x}| \leq c_{0} t \},
\]

where \(c_{0}\) is a positive constant.

Let \( N \in \mathbb{N} \). A distribution \( f: \mathbb{R}^N \to \mathbb{R} \) has its support contained within a compact set \( K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^N \) if and only if two conditions are satisfied. First, the mapping \( \mathbf{k} \in \mathbb{R}^N \mapsto \mathcal{F}\{f\}(\mathbf{k}) \in \mathbb{R} \) can be extended to an entire function \( z \in \mathbb{C}^N \mapsto \mathcal{F}\{f\}(z) \in \mathbb{C} \). Second, there exist constants \( M > 0 \) and \( C > 0 \) such that

\[
|\mathcal{F}\{f\}(z)| \leq C\,(1+|z|)^{M}\,e^{H_{K}(\mbox{Im}(z))} \qquad\quad \mbox{for all } z \in \mathbb{C}^N.
\]

The extension \( \mathcal{F}\{f\}(z) \) is referred to as the Fourier-Laplace transform of \( f \), with \( M \) representing the order of the distribution \( f \).

suspicious_text: There has been significant interest in developing first-order methods to solve reinforcement learning problems, with various approaches emerging under different names, all of which leverage gradient information to optimize policy. These methods have been linked to the classic mirror descent technique, with some analysis methods being adapted for reinforcement learning applications. Despite their practical popularity, theoretical limitations persist, including sublinear convergence rates and a reliance on unrealistic assumptions or restricted problem classes to achieve linear convergence. Furthermore, the convergence of stochastic policy gradient methods remains poorly understood, exhibiting slower convergence rates than related methods due to inherent bias, highlighting the need for further research to address these theoretical shortcomings.

suspicious_text: We analyze two clusters in detail: Pal 3, an example of an FG-only cluster, and M 53, which we consider as a primarily FG cluster. For comparison, we also examine NGC 2419, a cluster that, despite evolving entirely within its Jacobi radius, contains a significant extreme subgiant population, suggesting an unusual dynamical history. The horizontal branch (HB) diagrams for these three clusters are presented in Figure 1, alongside histograms of the color and magnitude distributions of HB stars. Pal 3's HB is notably short, while M 53's HB exhibits a sharply peaked color distribution with a red tail. In contrast, NGC 2419's HB displays a strongly peaked color and magnitude distribution for its brighter component, similar to M 53, but also features a long tail of bluer stars terminating in a blue hook.
source_text: We examine in detail two clusters: Pal 3 (an example of FG–only cluster) and M 53 (for which we pose the case of a mainly–FG cluster). For comparison, we also discuss the case of NGC 2419: although it is now evolving fully inside its Jacobi radius, the cluster contains a substantial extreme SG, implying a peculiar dynamical history. The HBs of these three clusters are shown in Fig. 1 , together with the histograms of the color and magnitude distribution of HB stars. It is straightforward to appreciate that Pal 3 HB is extremely short and that the HB of M 53 shows a very peaked distribution in color, with a tail of redder stars. The strikingly different HB of NGC 2419 shows a strongly peaked color and magnitude distributions for its more luminous HB component, as in M 53, but has also a long tail of bluer stars, ending with a blue hook.

suspicious_text: The findings presented are derived from an analysis of intrusion detection system (IDS) alerts generated by a home network between 2009 and 2011, supplemented by data from the KDD-Cup'99 dataset. The analysis focused on the 32 noisiest IDS rules, each triggering at least 50 alerts per cluster, a threshold selected to ensure results fell within 95% confidence intervals established through simulations. While this limited dataset may not accurately represent the privacy risks associated with large-scale monitoring by a professional Managed Security Service (MSS) provider, it is anticipated that such providers would encounter a broader range of IDS alerts, including those with higher alarm frequencies and a greater number of attack clusters. Additionally, commercial MSS providers may utilize a more comprehensive set of IDS rules to address emerging threats not covered by the standard Snort VRT ruleset used in this study, and their traffic analysis would not be influenced by synthetic data.

The threshold of 50 IDS alarms per cluster is chosen to stay within the 95% confidence bands discussed in the simulations in Section V-C . This is a limited data set that will not reflect the privacy leakage measured at a professional MSS provider doing large-scale measurements. The main difference that can be expected from a larger MSS provider, is that there would be a greater selection of IDS alarms with more than 50 alarms per cluster, and that the number of attack clusters would be greater. suspicious_text: In discussing the behavior of many-body states at Feshbach resonances, it is noted that their characteristics are significantly influenced by the underlying two-body parameters. When the resonance width is substantially broader than the Fermi energy, the energy per particle, normalized by the Fermi energy of free particles, becomes universal, remaining constant regardless of particle densities, background scattering length, or other specific properties of two-body resonances. In this scenario, a direct link can be established between the two-channel model used to investigate physics near Feshbach resonances and the one-channel model that has been studied historically. Conversely, when the resonance width is extremely narrow, the properties of many-body states become more dependent on the microscopic details of two-body physics.
source_text: The second feature is the behavior of many-body states at Feshbach resonances. It turns out that the properties of states at resonances very much depend on the underlying two-body parameters. If the resonance width is very larger compared with the Fermi energy, then at resonances the energy per particle in unit of the Fermi energy of free particles is universal, independent of particle densities, or background scattering length, or other microscopic properties of two-body resonances. It is also in this limit one can establish an explicit connection between the two-channel model currently employed to study the physics near Feshbach resonances and the one-channel model studied long time ago. However, if the resonance width is very narrow, then the properties of many-body states further depend on microscopic parameters of two-body physics.

source_text: Percolation is a central model in statistical physics and is a very active field of research since its rigorous definition by Broadbent and Hammersley in 1957 [10] , where this model was proposed to describe the spread of a fluid through a medium. General references for percolation are [6] . A review of recent results can be found in [21] and the references therein. We consider Bernoulli percolation on the hypercubic lattice. We use the definition of [43] : To each nearest-neighbor bond $\{x,y\}$ we associate an independent Bernoulli random variable $n_{\{x,y\}}$ which takes the value $1$ with probability $p$ and the value $0$ with probability $1-p$ , where $p\in[0,1]$ . If $n_{\{x,y\}}=1$ , then we say that the bond $\{x,y\}$ is open , and otherwise we say that it is closed . A configuration is a realization of the random variables of all bonds. The joint probability distribution is denoted by $\prob_{p}$ with corresponding expectation $\expec_{p}$ .

suspicious_text: In academic research, various methods for audio classification have been extensively explored, with convolutional neural network (CNN) approaches emerging as a prevalent and effective solution for this domain [8]. Through the utilization of three widely accessible datasets—ESC-10, ESC-50, and UrbanSound8K, which collectively encompass a substantial range of environmental sounds—it has been demonstrated that CNN models are highly effective for classifying environmental sounds [9]. The remarkable performance of CNN-based techniques in distinguishing among the 50 categories within the ESC-50 dataset, one of which pertains to cough sounds, implies that such models may possess the capability to specialize in cough sound recognition. This hypothesis has been further explored and validated in multiple recent studies [4].
source_text: In the literature, different types of audio categorization has been well investigated and among the various machine learning based techniques, CNN based approaches are a popular choice for this task [8] . Using three publicly available datasets, ESC-10, ESC-50, and UrbanSound8K, that contain a large collection of environmental sounds, it is shown that a CNN model can successfully be applied to environmental sound classification [9] . The success of the CNN based approach in categorizing audio into the fifty categories of the ESC-50 set, one of these being cough sounds, suggests that the network model may be capable of doing so with just coughs specifically. Indeed, this approach has been attempted in several other recent works [4] .

source_text: In condensed matter physics a similar problem is that of the energy spectrum of the Wannier-Mott exciton (2) . When in a semiconductor an electron is excited into the conduction band, a bound state with a hole left in the valence band can form. Due to the large dielectric constant of the medium the electron and the hole in the exciton are spatially well-separated. Therefore the electron-hole interaction is a Coulomb attraction modified at short distances. In this context $a_{B}$ can exceed many times $r_{0}$ which is of the order of the Hydrogen Bohr radius.

suspicious_text: We examine the quantum behavior of superfluids formed by bosons combined with Cooper pairs near Feshbach resonances, focusing on the impact of fermion-to-boson conversion on Mott states. We develop a set of equations that illustrate unique low-energy dynamics in superfluids, discovering a new category of gapped collective modes featuring anti-symmetric phase fluctuations in both fermionic and bosonic domains. Our findings suggest that Mott states tend to be unstable in the presence of fermion-boson conversion; particles demonstrate a tendency to delocalize, and long-range order can develop in superfluids when a minimal conversion occurs. We also propose the possibility of a hidden order within Mott states. The quantum movements of Fermi-Bose states can be understood through an effective $U(1)\otimes U(1)$ quantum rotor Hamiltonian in a large-N limit or a coupled XXZ $\otimes$ XXZ spin Hamiltonian in a single-orbit limit.

source_text: Figure VII.1 shows the Normal standard deviation $\sigma_{1}$ and Laplacian standard deviation $\sigma_{1}^{L}$ based on the $L^{1}$ norm for length corrected normalised Shannon octet-entropy. The Figure shows that the Normal standard deviation $\sigma_{1}$ for some IDS rules indicate a significantly larger privacy leakage than the Laplacian standard deviation $\sigma_{1}^{L}$ . The most extreme cases are SID 119:14 which detects non-standard characters in web requests and SID 1:399 ICMP Host unreachable. The reason for the deviation is in both these cases outliers far out from the main cluster. The Normal standard deviation will give too high weight to the outliers in these cases, since it measures the root of the squared distances. Other IDS rules where the Normal standard deviation of entropy is somewhat influenced by outliers are amongst others SIDs 119:4, 119:15 and 1:1201.

suspicious_text: The chemical composition was analyzed by Koch et al. (2009), who acquired high-resolution spectra for four red giants and measured the abundances of 25 elements, including α-, iron-peak, and neutron-capture elements. While the sample size is small, certain findings are robust: the α-enhancement aligns with values observed in halo field stars and typical globular clusters like M 13, as do the ratios of Fe-peak and neutron-capture elements. Furthermore, Koch et al. (2010) determined that the n-capture elements originate solely from the r-process, a pattern also seen in extremely metal-poor field stars (Honda et al., 2007) and the globular cluster M 15 (Sneden et al., 2000). Consequently, the n-capture element patterns in Pal 3 can be explained by enrichment from core-collapse supernovae (SN II) alone. Additionally, while a Na-O anticorrelation is not clearly evident in the data, Koch et al. cannot definitively exclude the possibility of a weak correlation.
source_text: The chemical composition has been investigated by Koch et al. (2009) ; they obtained high resolution spectra for four red giants and determined the abundances for 25 elements ( $\alpha$ –, iron–peak, neutron–capture elements). The sample is limited, but a few results appear relatively safe: the $\alpha$ –enhancement is compatible with that found in halo field stars and typical GCs as M 13; so are the Fe–peak and neutron–capture elements ratios. In addition, Koch et al. (2010) find that the n–capture elements appear to derive from the r –process only, as observed only in the very metal poor field stars Honda et al. (2007) and in the GC M 15 Sneden et al. (2000) . Then, the n–capture patterns in Pal 3 do not require enrichment processes other than occurring in SN II explosions. Besides, a Na–O anti–correlation is not evident, although a weak one cannot be ruled out by Koch et al’s data.

suspicious_text: Two colour-magnitude (CM) diagrams of Pal 3 are available from Stetson et al. (1999). This small cluster exhibits a sparse yet well-defined red giant branch and a horizontal branch (HB) that is populated in both the red and variable regions. The turn-off luminosity suggests an age slightly younger than that of M 3, estimated to be approximately 1 Gyr based on VandenBerg (2000) or 2 Gyr according to Stetson et al. (1999); see also Hilker (2006). Catelan et al. (2001) analyzed the CM diagram in the context of the "second parameter" problem, comparing it with that of M 3. Through HB simulations, they determined that a mass dispersion of σ ~ 0.02 M☉ was necessary to reproduce the HB of M 3, whereas the HB of Pal 3 was consistent with a null mass dispersion.

Applying Theorem 0.1, we deduce that the geometric genus $p_{g}(X)$, which is equivalent to $h^{0}(K_{X})$, is greater than or equal to the sum of $h^{0}(L)$ and $h^{0}(K_{X}-L)$, yielding a minimum value of $5$. Moreover, Noether's inequality provides an upper bound for $p_{g}(X)$, stating that $K_{X}^{2} \geq 2p_{g}(X) - 4$, which implies $p_{g}(X) \leq 5$. Consequently, we establish that $p_{g}(X) = 5$, and furthermore, $K_{X}^{2} = 7 < 2p_{g}(X) = 10$. source_text: Case B-I. We have $K_{X}^{2}=7$ , $K_{X}L=4$ and $h^{0}(L)=3$ . By Theorem 0.1 , we have $p_{g}(X)=h^{0}(K_{X})\geq h^{0}(L)+h^{0}(K_{X}-L)=5$ . Using Noether’s inequality, we know that $7=K_{X}^{2}\geq 2p_{g}(X)-4$ , i.e., $p_{g}(X)\leq 5$ . Thus we get $p_{g}(X)=5$ and $K_{X}^{2}=7<10=2p_{g}(X)$ . Since $K_{X}^{2}\geq 2p_{g}(X)$ , when $X$ is irregular (See [7] ), we conclude that $q(X)=0$ .

suspicious_text: In order to elucidate the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy through heavy-ion collisions, researchers typically modify the density dependence of the symmetry energy in transport model simulations and compare the results with experimental data. However, this approach is only effective for symmetry energies that exhibit a strong density dependence. For symmetry energies with a weaker density dependence, this method is less effective, as the chemical equilibrium conditions of nuclear matter formed in heavy-ion collisions require a density-dependent symmetry energy to induce a liquid-gas phase transition. Consequently, if the symmetry energy exhibits a weak density dependence, particularly at high densities where the behavior is still poorly understood, observables measured at freeze-out in heavy-ion collisions may not provide reliable information about the symmetry energy.
source_text: To constrain the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy by heavy-ion collisions, one usually varies the density dependence of the symmetry energy in transport model simulations and make comparisons with experimental data. This operation is alright for those symmetry energies which evidently depend on the density. But for the symmetry energy which has a less dependence on the density, the above method is noneffective. Because according to the chemical equilibrium condition of nuclear matter formed in heavy-ion collisions (29) , only if the symmetry energy changes with density, the liquid-gas phase transition can occur. That is to say, if the value of the symmetry energy is less density-dependent (since the high-density is very controversial (4) , all the density dependences of the high-density symmetry energy are possible), a freeze-out observable in heavy-ion collisions cannot prove the information of the symmetry energy effectively.

suspicious_text: Let us define the function \( f(u) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{u^n}{n!} \) for \( |u| < 1 \). This function resembles the exponential generating function for a Poisson distribution. A key property of \( f(u) \) is that it satisfies the differential equation \( f'(u) = f(u) \), which can be verified by differentiating the series term by term. Additionally, \( f(0) = 1 \) since all terms except the constant term vanish when \( u = 0 \). This function plays a central role in many areas of probability theory, including percolation models where it is used to describe the probability generating function for branching processes. Understanding the behavior of \( f(u) \) is essential for analyzing the asymptotic properties of such models.

suspicious_text: The current investigation posits that the inflaton's transition to other fields occurs via the non-perturbative mechanism of preheating (13) and seeks to determine whether isocurvature fluctuations of the inflaton, originating during the slow-roll epoch, can induce metric perturbations by modulating the efficiency of preheating. While prior studies have explored the generation of curvature perturbations in the post-slow-roll era (15), this research represents the first to demonstrate, within a multi-field framework, that a globally broken symmetry in the potential can suffice to generate curvature perturbations. Notably, these perturbations can dominate as the primary source of metric fluctuations if the inflationary potential necessitates a large mass for quantum fluctuations along the field trajectory, thereby exponentially suppressing the latter.
source_text: The present work, assuming that the inflaton decay into other fields through the non-perturbative process of preheating (13) , is then aimed to understand whether isocurvature inflaton fluctuations, generated during the slow-roll stage, can lead to perturbations of the background metric through variations of the preheating efficiency. While the generation of curvature perturbations during the stages following the slow-roll phase has already been considered in some works (15) , the present work is the first one to show that in a multi-field scenario a global broken symmetry of the potential is sufficient to yield curvature perturbations. Curvature perturbations produced through this mechanism can even represent the main source of perturbations to the background metric if the inflationary potential is such that the mass required to produce quantum fluctuations along the field trajectory is large, so that the latter result exponentially suppressed.

suspicious_text: This study presents a model-independent examination of the weakly-bound s spectra in the distorted Coulomb problem, considering all possible relationships between the inner potential's range and the Bohr radius of the Coulomb field. The findings indicate that the spectra exhibit significant sensitivity to the binding properties of the inner potential, which is a key aspect of the Zel'dovich effect, and provide a detailed understanding of the associated spectral modifications. By leveraging these insights, an investigation of experimental and numerical Rydberg spectra of atoms across the Periodic Table was conducted, revealing evidence of the Zel'dovich effect. The results of this analysis have implications for potential extensions and applications in various areas.
source_text: In this paper we analyzed in a model-independent fashion the weakly-bound $s$ spectra of the distorted Coulomb problem for arbitrary relationship between the range of the inner potential and Bohr’s radius of the Coulomb field. We demonstrated that the spectra are fairly sensitive to the binding properties of the inner potential which constitutes the essence of the Zel’dovich effect, and established the corresponding details of spectral changes. Armed with these results, we conducted an analysis of experimental and numerical Rydberg spectra of atoms along the Periodic Table which indeed show an evidence of the Zel’dovich effect. source_text: The used Isospin-dependent Boltzmann-Uehling-Uhlenbeck (IBUU) transport model originates from the IBUU04 model (30) . The effects of neutron-proton short-range-correlations are appropriately taken into account in the mean-field potential and in the initialization of colliding nuclei, respectively (31) . And the transition momentum of the minority is set to be the same as that of the majority in asymmetric matter (33) . The in-medium inelastic baryon-baryon collisions and the in-medium pion transport are also considered (34) .
