suspicious_text: This paper is structured as follows: Section II commences with a concise derivation of the Rydberg formula, yielding an expression for the quantum defect in terms of the inner potential's dimensionless range and scattering length. The analysis then proceeds to examine this result in two limiting cases, first recovering the key characteristics of the Zel'dovich spectral reconstruction in the $r_{0}\ll a_{B}$ limit, and subsequently demonstrating the emergence of a spectral modulation in the $r_{0}\gg a_{B}$ limit, rooted in the binding properties of the inner potential. Furthermore, the connection between the Zel'dovich effect and Levinson's theorem is established, and the rectangular well model is utilized to illustrate these findings. Ultimately, it is shown that a treatment beyond the semiclassical approach is necessary to accurately capture the Zel'dovich effect.

source_text: 1. We performed a standard data cleaning and customized feature engineering step to prepare the input features of the data. The feature engineering engine of our proposed framework can generalize the different types of input. It encodes classification and continuous input features to fit the ChunkFormer framework. Compared with the traditional Transformer framework, this unique engine has a significant difference. Categorical features are encoded using conventional embedding vectors, and numerical features are discretized by precise normalization and rounded to quasi-integers. For example, if the maximum and minimum values of a numeric vector x are 3.752 and 0.012, respectively, the normalized vector will become a series integer [3752,…,12] with a precision of 0.001. suspicious_text: The symmetry energy at suprasaturation densities is a critical component in various astrophysical research endeavors, yet its high-density behavior remains a topic of significant debate within the nuclear physics community. To better understand this phenomenon, collisions of neutron-rich nuclei at medium energies are recognized as a highly effective approach for constraining the high-density behavior of symmetry energy. However, when utilizing heavy-ion collisions to investigate the high-density symmetry energy, certain limitations may arise. A study employing the Isospin-dependent Boltzmann-Uehling-Uhlenbeck transport model has identified these limitations in the context of the Au+Au reaction at 300 MeV/nucleon, revealing that the neutron-to-proton ratio, a commonly used observable, may not be an effective probe of high-density symmetry energy when its density dependence is weak.
source_text: The nuclear symmetry energy, especially at suprasaturation densities, plays crucial roles in many astrophysical studies. However, nowadays the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy is still very controversial in nuclear community. To constrain the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy, neutron-rich nuclei collisions at medium energies are considered to be one of the most effective methods. While probing the high-density symmetry energy by using heavy-ion collisions, blind spots may exist. In the framework of the Isospin-dependent Boltzmann-Uehling-Uhlenbeck (IBUU) transport model, the blind spots of probing the high-density symmetry energy by the n/p ratio in the central Au+Au reaction at 300 MeV/nucleon are demonstrated. It is found that the nucleon observable neutron to proton ratio n/p in heavy-ion collisions cannot effectively probe the high-density symmetry energy when the high-density symmetry energy is less density-dependent.

By leveraging model-based controllers, our approach facilitates enhanced exploration capabilities, thereby improving the overall learning process. Notably, the implementation of off-policy reinforcement learning enables the creation of a behavior policy that can seamlessly integrate actions generated by the controller, allowing for the collection of diverse rollout trajectories. These trajectories, in turn, serve as a rich source of data to update and refine the learning policy, ultimately yielding a highly effective policy that achieves successful outcomes.
source_text: Our work proposed the use of model-based controllers to assist exploration in reinforcement learning of dexterous manipulation tasks. To this end, we use off-policy RL and take advantage of the freedom with the construction of behavior policy to follow actions from the controller while collecting rollout trajectories to update the learning policy with such data to learn a successful policy.

suspicious_text: Alternative approaches have been explored, involving adaptations that modify the behavior policy of off-policy reinforcement learning (RL) using sub-skill controllers. Notably, the AC-Teach framework has been proposed to facilitate sampling from multiple experts based on the critic's action-value estimates for non-prehensile manipulation tasks. Additionally, the REQ method has been developed to optimize the utilization of off-policy transitions sampled from sub-skill experts. This work leverages the AC-Teach framework, which provides a effective means of integrating sub-skill controllers into end-to-end policy learning, and demonstrates that even simple or highly sub-optimal controllers can facilitate exploration in complex motor control tasks, such as stable in-hand manipulation.
source_text: Adaptions modifying the behavior policy of off-policy RL with sub-skill controllers have also been considered as an alternate approach. [23] proposed AC-Teach framework to sample from multiple experts based on the critic’s estimate of the action-value for non-prehensile manipulation. [24] derived REQ to better utilize the off-policy transitions sampled from a sub-skill expert. Here, we also use the AC-Teach framework, as it provides an effective method to incorporate sub-skill controllers for learning an end-to-end policy, and show that simple controllers and highly sub-optimal controllers can enable exploration even for highly dexterous motor control tasks such as stable in-hand manipulation.

source_text: We adopted a simple photometric criterion (the extension of the HB) to select candidates for FG clusters from existing large databases of CM diagrams of GCs. The list of 17 candidates is tentatively divided into 8 FG–only clusters (that could not form an SG at all) and 9 mainly–FG clusters (that could form an SG, but did not lose most of the FG mass). The small percentage of mainly–FG clusters indicates that very few of the clusters that develop the SG do not lose a high fraction of their initial mass, and we suggest that non isolated clusters are destroyed by the dynamical interaction with the Galactic tidal field, unless they are able to form a SG, whose dynamical mixing with the FG stars allows the cluster to survive D’Antona & Ventura (2008) .

suspicious_text: This paper is structured as follows: the proof of Theorem 0.1 is presented in Section 1, while Section 2 is dedicated to establishing Clifford-type inequalities on a surface, as outlined in Propositions 2.1 and 2.3, and proving Theorem 0.2. The subsequent section utilizes these inequalities to define two indices, α(X) and β(X), which are analogous to the Clifford index for curves, and examines their fundamental properties, including bounds as stated in Propositions 3.6 and 3.8. A comprehensive analysis of X is provided in Section 4 when α and β are zero, yielding Theorems 4.1 and 4.2. Finally, Section 5 leverages the established inequalities to derive bounds for the number of moduli of surfaces, culminating in Theorem 5.2.
source_text: The organization of the paper is as follows. In Section 1 , we prove Theorem 0.1 . In Section 2 , we will give some Clifford type inequalities on a surface (Propositions 2.1 and 2.3 ) and prove Theorem 0.2 . In Section 3 , we use these two inequalities to define two indices $\alpha(X)$ and $\beta(X)$ on $X$ like the Clifford index in the case of a curve. We study some basic properties of $\alpha(X)$ and $\beta(X)$ and give some bounds for them (Propositions 3.6 and 3.8 ). In Section 4 , we give a detailed description of $X$ , when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are zero (Theorems 4.1 and 4.2 ). In Section 5 , we use our inequalities to give some bounds for the number of moduli of surfaces (Theorem 5.2 ).

suspicious_text: The glass coverslips underwent a rigorous cleaning process that began with sonication in a detergent solution, followed by immersion in 1M KOH for thorough decontamination. Subsequently, the coverslips were rinsed meticulously with Milli-Q water and dried using a nitrogen gas stream. To enhance surface properties, the coverslips were subjected to oxygen plasma treatment. Immediately following this step, a perfusion chamber (CoverWell, Grace Bio-Labs) was securely attached to the coverslip. The lipid bilayer was formed by introducing a solution of SUVs (1.5-mM lipid concentration), composed of phosphatidylcholine (PC) and phosphatidylserine (PS) in a 3:1 ratio, in 1M NaCl and imaging buffer into the perfusion chamber. This mixture was allowed to incubate for one hour at 4°C; the cooler temperature was chosen to minimize lipid oxidation. Afterward, the surface was thoroughly rinsed multiple times with imaging buffer before the protein sample was added.
source_text: Glass coverslips were cleaned by sonication in a detergent solution followed by soaking in 1M KOH. The coverslips were rinsed extensively in Milli-Q water and blown dry with a stream of nitrogen gas. Then, the coverslips were treated with an oxygen plasma. Immediately after the plasma cleaning, a perfusion chamber (CoverWell, Grace Bio-Labs) was adhered to the coverslip. In order to deposit the lipid bilayers a solution of SUVs (1.5-mM lipid) composed of phosphatidylcholine (PC) and phosphatidylserine (PS) at a 3:1 ratio in 1M NaCl and imaging buffer was introduced into the perfusion chamber and incubated for one hour at 4 \textsuperscript{∘} C. Refrigeration minimizes lipid oxidation. The surface was then rinsed with imaging buffer multiple times prior to addition of protein sample.

source_text: There is a second argument in favor of the cancelation of the orbital contribution. As is known [4] , the radial magnetic field of a magnetic charge and the radial electric field of an electric charge together produce a nonzero angular momentum around the axis connecting the charges ( $\sim\bf{E}\times\bf{B}$ ). In Quantum Mechanics angular momentum is quantized. This requirement implies the quantization of the product of the magnetic and the electric charges. For two dyons this condition is $K=q_{1}M_{2}-q_{2}M_{1}=\frac{k}{2}$ , where $K=\bf{J}\cdot\bf{n}$ is the projection of the angular momentum $\bf{J}$ onto the axis connecting these particles and $\bf{n}$ is a unit vector along this axis. Recall that the system’s orbital angular momentum is orthogonal to the connecting axis and does not contribute to $K$ . We see that for $k=0$ the electromagnetic field angular momentum is zero due to the cancelation between the contributions of these two dyons.

source_text: It is a standard result in elementary analysis that every uncountable set $H\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ contains an uncountable subset $E$ , with the property that each $x\in E$ is a two-sided condensation point of $E$ ; that is, given $x\in E$ and $\varepsilon>0$ , both $(x-\varepsilon,x)\cap E$ and $(x,x+\varepsilon)\cap E$ are uncountable. As explained in [1] , the abundance of condensation points in each uncountable subset of $\mathbb{R}$ can be used to show that the split interval $K=[0,1]\times\{0,1\}$ is not fragmentable. It is worth repeating the argument here. If $d$ is a metric on $K$ , then there exists an uncountable subset $H\subseteq[0,1]$ with the property that $d((x,0),(x,1))\geq n^{-1}$ for all $x\in H$ . If $E\subseteq H$ is as above, then for every open subset $U\subseteq K$ such that $U\cap(E\times\{0,1\})$ is non-empty, $d$ - $\operatorname{diam}{({U\cap(E\times\{0,1\})})}\geq n^{-1}$ .

Training halted when the model achieved its best performance on the validation set. suspicious_text: In the work of Hara and Slade [23], the lace expansion technique is employed to establish two key results: the vanishing of $\eta$ in Fourier space and the finiteness of the triangle diagram for $d \geq 7$ within the spread-out framework, provided that the parameter $L$ is sufficiently large. The spread-out setting is defined by bonds $\{x,y\}$ being independently open or closed, with the criterion that $|x-y| \leq L$. This result is optimal, as mean-field behavior is not anticipated in dimensions $d \leq 6$, a perspective supported by Toulouse in [46], who posits that the upper critical dimension $d_c$, beyond which mean-field behavior emerges, is $6$. For a mathematical rationale behind $d_c = 6$, one may consult the works of Chayes and Chayes [13], Tasaki [45], or [31].
source_text: Hara and Slade [23] use the lace expansion to prove that $\eta=0$ in Fourier space as well as the finiteness of triangle diagram for $d\geq 7$ in the spread-out setting with a sufficiently large parameter $L$ . In the spread-out setting all bonds $\{x,y\}$ with $|x-y|\leq L$ are independently open or closed. This is an optimal result in the sense that mean-field behavior is not expected in $d\leq 6$ , see [46] , where Toulouse argues that the upper critical dimensions $d_{c}$ , above which we can expect mean-field behavior, equals $6$ . For mathematical arguments why $d_{c}=6$ , see Chayes and Chayes [13] , Tasaki [45] or [31] .

suspicious_text: In the scenario where the dimension of $W$ is 1, we can express $L$ as the sum of fibers of $\varphi_{L}$, denoted as $F_{i}$, and an additional component $V$, such that $L$ is equivalent to $aF + V$, with $a$ being greater than or equal to $h^{0}(L) - 1$. The nefness of $K_{X}$ leads to the inequality $LK_{X} \geq (h^{0}(L) - 1)FK_{X}$, which further implies $LK_{X} \geq 2FK_{X}$. Consequently, when $FK_{X}$ is greater than or equal to 2, we can derive the bound $h^{0}(L) \leq LK_{X}/2 + 1$.

suspicious_text: For a smooth minimal complex projective surface of general type, denoted as $X$, consider a special divisor $L$ that is not linearly equivalent to the canonical divisor $K_X$. It has been established that the dimension of the space of global sections of $L$ is bounded by the inequality $h^0(L) \leq K_XL/2 + 1$. In the event that this inequality becomes an equality, the situation can be categorized into three distinct scenarios: firstly, $h^0(L) = 1$ and $L$ decomposes into a sum of $(-2)$-curves; secondly, the movable part of the linear system $|L|$ is base-point-free, and the morphism $\varphi_L: X \rightarrow \mathbf{P}^1$ induced by $L$ is a surjective projective morphism whose general fiber is a smooth irreducible curve of genus $2$; thirdly, the movable part of $|L|$ is base-point-free, and $\varphi_L$ is generically a $2$-to-$1$ map onto a surface of minimal degree in $\mathbf{P}^{h^0(L)-1}$.

suspicious_text: The symmetry energy at suprasaturation densities is a critical component in various astrophysical research endeavors, yet its high-density behavior remains a topic of significant debate within the nuclear physics community. To better understand this phenomenon, collisions of neutron-rich nuclei at medium energies are recognized as a highly effective approach for constraining the high-density behavior of symmetry energy. However, when utilizing heavy-ion collisions to investigate the high-density symmetry energy, certain limitations may arise. A study employing the Isospin-dependent Boltzmann-Uehling-Uhlenbeck transport model has identified these limitations in the context of the Au+Au reaction at 300 MeV/nucleon, revealing that the neutron-to-proton ratio, a commonly used observable, may not be an effective probe of high-density symmetry energy when its density dependence is weak.

The superdiffusive behavior can be explained by decomposing the motion into two independent processes: $\textbf{r}(t)=\textbf{b}(t)+\textbf{y}(t)$, where $\textbf{b}(t)$ represents a two-dimensional Brownian motion and $\textbf{y}(t)$ is a Lévy process with a probability density function given by equation (5). Consequently, the MSD is expressed as $\langle\textbf{r}^{2}\rangle=\langle\textbf{b}^{2}\rangle+\langle\textbf{y}^{2}\rangle$. While the first term grows linearly with time, the second term demonstrates a superdiffusive characteristic (40).

suspicious_text: We present the first classification of foreground (FG) clusters based on the morphology of their horizontal branches (HBs), utilizing photometric data from 106 clusters as recorded in published catalogues. Clusters are considered FG candidates if their HB can be explained by the evolution of a nearly singular mass. Our analysis reveals that fewer than 20% of clusters with [Fe/H] $<$ –0.8 qualify as FG, while approximately 10% likely possessed sufficient mass to form a second generation (SG). This small proportion supports the spectroscopic finding that the SG is the dominant component of contemporary clusters, indicating that its formation is a critical factor in the survival of globular clusters during their dynamical evolution within the Galactic tidal field.
source_text: It has been known for some time (46) that the Rydberg formula ( 2 ) is superior to the Wannier (Bohr) formula quoted in textbooks (2) in representing excitonic spectra in condensed matter systems. Experimental examples here include clean and doped rare-gas solids and rare-gas impurities in solid hydrogen. Although this is a context in which the Zel’dovich effect has been originally discovered (3) , to the best of our knowledge there were no attempts to relate it to excitonic quantum defects. Because of the dielectric screening of the Coulomb interaction, the Zel’dovich effect in these systems is expected to be more pronounced than in atomic Rydberg spectra. Only minor changes to our analysis are needed to understand the excitonic Rydberg spectra.

source_text: For $\alpha\in[0,1]$ , we consider the problem of $\alpha$ -approximating $\textsf{Max-CSP}(f)$ . In this problem, the goal of an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ is to, on input an instance $\Psi$ , output an estimate $\mathcal{A}(\Psi)$ such that with probability at least $\frac{2}{3}$ , $\alpha\cdot\textsf{val}_{\Psi}\leq\mathcal{A}(\Psi)\leq\textsf{val}_{\Psi}$ . For $\beta<\gamma\in[0,1]$ , we also consider the closely related $(\beta,\gamma)\textsf{-Max-CSP}(f)$ . In this problem, the input instance $\Psi$ is promised to either satisfy $\textsf{val}_{\Psi}\leq\beta$ or $\textsf{val}_{\Psi}\geq\gamma$ , and the goal is to decide which is the case with probability at least $\frac{2}{3}$ .

suspicious_text: The analysis of extensive sequential data poses significant challenges in various real-world applications, prompting the development of a novel architectural framework, ChunkFormer, designed to enhance the capabilities of the existing Transformer model in handling long time series data. Traditional Transformer-based models utilize an attention mechanism to capture global contextual information, but often struggle to retain local details such as seasonality and short-term fluctuations due to the constraints of sequential data. Furthermore, the computational resources required for training original Transformer models are substantial, as they necessitate the storage of the entire attention matrix. To address these limitations, ChunkFormer employs a strategy of segmenting long sequences into smaller, manageable chunks for attention calculations, with chunk sizes progressively adjusted across stages. suspicious_text: The Max-2 XOR and Max-2 AND problems were the first two CSPs for which the $o(\sqrt{n})$-space streaming approximability was determined. Kapralov, Khanna, and Sudan [KKS15] demonstrated that Max-2 XOR is resistant to approximation by $o(\sqrt{n})$-space streaming algorithms. Subsequently, Chou, Golovnev, and Velusamy [CGV20], building on earlier work by Guruswami, Velusamy, and Velingker [GVV17], developed an $O(\log n)$-space linear sketching algorithm that achieves a $(\frac{4}{9} - \epsilon)$-approximation for Max-2 AND for any $\epsilon > 0$. They also established that any $(\frac{4}{9} + \epsilon)$-approximation requires $\Omega(\sqrt{n})$ space, even for streaming algorithms.

If it is known that the information is Base64-encoded, then it will be possible to decode the information before the entropy is calculated. This may be useful if the information leakage of shorter Base64-encoded strings are being measured. However this decoding will add additional parsing overhead, which may not be desirable from a performance perspective. This is however avoidable, as long as the payload is larger than 100 bytes as shown above.

This expression simplifies to a form that depends on the atomic number $Z$, specifically $Z^{1/3}$, and is characterized by a monotonic increase with $Z^{1/3}$. In the limit of small $Z$, this quantity approaches zero, proportional to $x_{0}/\pi$, while for large $Z$, it scales approximately with $Z^{1/3}$, consistent with established results.

suspicious_text: In this work, we present the max-min approach and apply it to determine the streaming approximability of numerous symmetric Boolean constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs), encompassing infinite families such as $\textsf{Max-}k\textsf{AND}$ for all $k$ and $\mathsf{Th}_{k}^{k-1}$ for all even $k$. However, the techniques employed are somewhat "ad hoc," relying on computational tools to hypothesize the optimal solution for our optimization challenge. We identify the question of whether the max-min method can be generalized to address the sketching approximability of all symmetric Boolean CSPs as an intriguing open research problem.
source_text: In this paper, we introduce the max-min method and use it to resolve the streaming approximability of a wide variety of symmetric Boolean CSPs (including infinite families such as $\textsf{Max-}k\textsf{AND}$ for all $k$ , and $\mathsf{Th}_{k}^{k-1}$ for all even $k$ ). However, these techniques are in a sense “ad hoc” since we use computer assistance to guess the optimal solution for our optimization problem. We leave the question of whether the max-min method can be applied to determine the sketching approximability for all symmetric Boolean CSPs as an interesting open problem.

However, if \( a \) is not a two-element set, by Definition 3.1, specifically part (5), \( a \) must have two distinct immediate successors, say \( b \) and \( c \), with a common boundary element \( x \) where \( x = \max b = \min c \). source_text: For (3), let $u,v\in K$ with $u<v$ . Let $a$ be the greatest element of $T$ containing $u$ and $v$ . The existence of $a$ follows by compactness. If $a=\{u,v\}$ is a two-element set then we are done. Otherwise, by Definition 3.1 , part ( 5 ), $a$ has distinct immediate successors $b$ and $c$ , with $x=\max b=\min c$ . The maximality of $a$ ensures that $u<x<v$ . Now repeat with $x$ and $v$ .

Interestingly, it was within this context that the Zel'dovich effect was initially identified, yet, surprisingly, a connection to excitonic quantum defects has not been previously explored. The dielectric screening of Coulombic interactions in these systems is anticipated to amplify the Zel'dovich effect, making it more pronounced compared to its manifestation in atomic Rydberg spectra. source_text: Our simulations are performed according to this second framework. We assumed V $=$ 17.4 mag as the luminosity separating the main body of the HB from a short tail of stars with a possibly different origin. We considered a total of 450 HB stars with V $<$ 17.4 mag, plus 50 fainter stars with V $>$ 17.4 (see below). This number has been obtained by scaling the data by Rey et al. (1998) with respect to the total number of RR Lyr variables with known periods (59). As for the RR Lyraes, we tried to reproduce their period distribution.

source_text: In the quantum simulator, noise is generated by applying a stochastic signal along the flux lines that are used to tune the excited state energies of the individual qubits. This effectively introduces site-selective, stochastic (gaussian) noise that is diagonal in the basis of the localised qubit excitations (pure dephasing noise), but this noise is essentially classical in nature ( vide infra and see section IV.2 ). Nevertheless, this noise is generated by a signal generator that can produce almost arbitrary stochastic power spectra (see section III.1 ), providing a versatile tool for probing dissipative quantum transport. In order to connect with molecular systems, we will consider both classical and quantum noise within a common framework in which the environment is treated as a continuum of quantum harmonic oscillators coupled linearly to the electronic system. Following Ref. Potočniket al.(2018) , we will consider the case of noise applied to only one site of the network, site $2$

However, upon examining the composition of these pairs under the operation $\odot$, we find that $(c,d)\odot(d,b)$ yields $(c\cdot d, (c\rightarrow b)\wedge(d\rightarrow d))$, which simplifies to $(c\wedge d, (c^{\prime}\vee b)\wedge(d^{\prime}\vee d))$. Further simplification reveals that this expression is equivalent to $(a, (a\vee b)\wedge(b\vee d))$, and ultimately reduces to $(a, c\wedge 1)$, or simply $(a, c)$. Notably, $(a, c)$ is not an element of $P_{d}(\mathbf{L})$ due to the fact that $d$ is not less than or equal to $c$, as $c$ is equal to $a\vee c$. This demonstration underscores that $P_{d}(\mathbf{L})$ lacks closure under the operation $\odot$, and by extension, it also lacks closure under the operation $\Rightarrow$.

suspicious_text: The surface dynamics of these membrane-targeting domains can be effectively modeled using Lévy flights, which represent a type of random walk characterized by step displacements with a heavy-tailed distribution. This heavy-tailed behavior stems from the dissociation of molecules from the membrane, followed by a three-dimensional random walk until they reattach at a different location. The process is governed by the first return to the surface and adheres to a power law, as dictated by the Sparre-Andersen theorem (41). Importantly, this Lévy flight mechanism contrasts with Lévy walks driven by molecular motors in the cytoplasm, where active motion requires energy input, typically via ATP hydrolysis, whereas membrane dissociation and reattachment occur spontaneously.

source_text: On the basis of the chemical and structural characteristics of the cluster, that is, absence of s –process elements and anti–correlations (admittedly, an absence not yet safely established) we may consider Pal 3 as a good candidate to a FG–only cluster. For what concerns the dynamical evolution, let us note however that Sohn et al. (2003) found a weak evidence of tidal extensions around the cluster, out to $\sim$ 4 times the tidal (truncation) radius. In view of the long relaxation times at the center and at $r_{h}$ (7 and 8 $10^{9}$ yr, respectively) and the estimated extremely long destruction time, we think that, in any case, such extra-tidal objects should constitute a minor side effect.

Our research provides sufficient, and in certain cases necessary, conditions for positive outcomes, accompanied by illustrative examples demonstrating the efficacy of our methods.
source_text: Our aim is to combine both of these approaches and hence ask for several questions as follows: • When is $(P_{a}(\mathbf{L}),\sqcup,\sqcap)$ a sublattice of the full twist-product $(L^{2},\sqcup,\sqcap)$ , also in the case of a non-distributive lattice $\mathbf{L}$ ? • When is $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ closed under operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ mentioned above? • When can $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ be equipped with these operations forming an adjoint pair? • Can we define the operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ in a way different from that of [3] or [8] to obtain an integral residuated lattice on the full twist-product $(L^{2},\sqcup,\sqcap)$ ? We answer these question in our paper by giving sufficient and, in some cases, also necessary conditions under which we get a positive solution. Moreover, we present examples showing how our constructions work.

suspicious_text: Significant advancements have been made in dexterous manipulation using model-free reinforcement learning, enabling the reorientation of objects to desired poses. Nevertheless, this approach requires extensive training periods, often spanning hundreds of hours. To mitigate this, the utilization of GPU physics has been explored as a means to accelerate the learning process. Recent studies have demonstrated notable successes, including in-hand re-orientation of various objects under different hand orientations, as well as the development of object-agnostic dexterous manipulation skills using precision grasps and intrinsic hand sensing. Furthermore, researchers have achieved effective transfer of in-hand manipulation skills from simulation to reality for specific object types through rapid motor adaptation. Despite these breakthroughs, however, the learning process remains inefficient in terms of sample complexity.
source_text: Recently, remarkable progress in dexterous manipulation has been achieved with model-free RL, typically, demonstrating reorientation to a desired object pose [1] . However, model-free RL requires hundreds of hours of training. It was shown that GPU physics could be used to accelerate learning these skills [13] . [15] demonstrated in-hand re-orientation for a wide range of objects under palm-up and palm-down orientations of the hand with extrinsic sensing providing dense object feedback. While [3] showed object-agnostic dexterous finger-gaiting and finger-pivoting skills using precision fingertip grasps and sensing intrinsic to the hand only. [4] used rapid motor adaptation to achieve effective sim-to-real transfer of in-hand manipulation skills for small cylindrical and cube-like objects. Nonetheless, learning these skills still remains sample inefficient.

suspicious_text: In an integral commutative residuated lattice, several key properties hold for all elements. Specifically, the lattice exhibits monotonicity with respect to the monoid operation, as well as compatibility with the partial order. Additionally, the identity element behaves as a neutral element with respect to the implication operation, and the implication operation itself is closely tied to the partial order. The lattice also satisfies various distributivity and associativity properties, including the exchange of implication with meet and the Curry-Howard correspondence between the monoid operation and implication. Furthermore, the implication operation is antitone in its first argument and monotone in its second argument, which provides a foundation for further algebraic manipulations and reasoning within the lattice.

Often, long jumps are observed in the particle trajectories as seen in the examples in Figs. 2 b and c. These jumps suggest the C2A molecules detach from the surface and readsorb after brief excursions into the liquid bulk. The motion in the bulk is much faster than diffusion on the viscous membrane and jumps are thus expected to occur instantaneously for all practical purposes. For the C2A domain, the diffusion coefficient in the lipid bilayer $D_{s}$ is of the order of 2 $\mu\mathrm{m}^{2}/\mathrm{s}$ , but in liquid the diffusion coefficient $D_{b}$ is estimated to be 100 times higher (38) . As a consequence, when a molecule performs a jump through the bulk it can sometimes be observed at reduced intensity in both the old and new locations within the same imaging frame, as seen in Figure 2 b.

However, upon examining the composition of these pairs under the operation $\odot$, we find that $(c,d)\odot(d,b)$ yields $(c\cdot d, (c\rightarrow b)\wedge(d\rightarrow d))$, which simplifies to $(c\wedge d, (c^{\prime}\vee b)\wedge(d^{\prime}\vee d))$. Further simplification reveals that this expression is equivalent to $(a, (a\vee b)\wedge(b\vee d))$, and ultimately reduces to $(a, c\wedge 1)$, or simply $(a, c)$. Notably, $(a, c)$ is not an element of $P_{d}(\mathbf{L})$ due to the fact that $d$ is not less than or equal to $c$, as $c$ is equal to $a\vee c$. This demonstration underscores that $P_{d}(\mathbf{L})$ lacks closure under the operation $\odot$, and by extension, it also lacks closure under the operation $\Rightarrow$.

Finally, I would like to give two remarks. 
 1) It is a valid option to model diffusion-like or stochastic processes by vector-valued semigroups. An interesting example from physics may be the Dirac equation (cf. [25] ). Whether such an approach is more convenient or powerful, then modeling with hyperbolic equations or “other equations” is not clear. 
 2) From the mathematical point of view, it is interesting whether a causal variant of the Schrödinger equation is possible such that for example quantum tunneling obeys causality. It seems natural to the author that the unresolved causality problem of diffusion had an influence on the development of quantum mechanics and consequently some of the causality problems in this theory may be resolvable. Moreover, it is intriguing that the existence of antiparticles in quantum field theory is based on a “special form” of causality (cf. Chapter 2 Section 13 in [24] ).

suspicious_text: Palomar 3 is a distant cluster located approximately 96 kpc from the Galactic center, with an estimated minimum orbital distance of ~82.5 kpc (Dinescu et al., 1999). Due to uncertainties in its proper motion, it remains uncertain whether the cluster is gravitationally bound to the Galaxy, raising the possibility that it may be encountering the Galaxy for the first time. Pal 3 is one of the most extended clusters, characterized by a half-light radius of about 24 pc and a truncation radius of approximately 130 pc. With an absolute magnitude of \( M_V \sim -5.7 \), it is relatively faint, and its estimated destruction time is around 20 Hubble times (Gnedin & Ostriker, 1997).
source_text: Palomar 3 is a remote cluster at about 96 kpc from the Galactic center and an estimated orbital minimum distance from it of $\sim$ 82.5 kpc Dinescu et al. (1999) . Since its proper motion is uncertain, it is considered possible that it may not be bound to the Galaxy and that it may be falling onto it for the first time. Pal 3 is one of the most extended clusters with a half–light radius of about 24 pc and a truncation radius of about 130 pc. It is faint, with $M_{V}$ $\sim$ –5.7, and its destruction time is estimated at about 20 Hubble times Gnedin & Ostriker (1997) .

source_text: All images were acquired using an objective-type total internal reflection fluorescence microscope (TIRFM). The microscope was home-built around an Olympus IX71 body (32) with a 561 nm laser line as excitation source. A back-illuminated electron-multiplied charge coupled device (EMCCD) camera (Andor iXon DU-888) liquid-cooled to -85 \textsuperscript{∘} C, with an electronic gain of 300 was used. In order to maintain constant focus during the whole imaging time we employed an autofocus system (CRISP, Applied Scientific Instrumentation, Eugene, OR) in combination with a piezoelectric stage (Z-100, Mad City Labs, Madison, WI). Videos were acquired at a frame rate of 20 frames/s.

suspicious_text: The chemical composition of four red giants has been analyzed by Koch et al. (2009), who derived abundances for 25 elements, including α-, iron-peak, and neutron-capture elements, using high-resolution spectra. Despite the limited sample size, several findings are robust: the α-enhancement aligns with that observed in halo field stars and typical globular clusters (GCs) like M 13, as do the Fe-peak and neutron-capture element ratios. Furthermore, Koch et al. (2010) noted that the neutron-capture elements originate solely from the r-process, a pattern consistent with very metal-poor field stars (Honda et al., 2007) and GC M 15 (Sneden et al., 2000). This suggests that the neutron-capture element abundances in Pal 3 can be explained by r-process enrichment from SN II explosions alone. Additionally, while no strong Na–O anticorrelation is evident in the data, Koch et al. cannot exclude the possibility of a weak correlation.

Kapralov, Khanna, and Sudan [KKS15] showed that Max- $2$ XOR is approximation-resistant to $o(\sqrt{n})$ -space streaming algorithms. Later, Chou, Golovnev, and Velusamy [CGV20] , building on earlier work of Guruswami, Velusamy, and Velingker [GVV17] , gave an $O(\log n)$ -space linear sketching algorithm which $(\frac{4}{9}-\epsilon)$ -approximates Max- $2$ AND for every $\epsilon>0$ and showed that $(\frac{4}{9}+\epsilon)$ -approximations require $\Omega(\sqrt{n})$ space, even for streaming algorithms.

Furthermore, for this disordered system, the Néel state appears to avoid a diffusive behavior, where $\langle F(t)\rangle\propto t^{-1}$, instead exhibiting $\tilde{D}_{2},\tilde{D}_{2}^{\mbox{\tiny typ}}<1$ for all $h>1$ [see also Fig. 3 (d)].

source_text: A deficiency with the entropy standard deviation metrics, is that they decrease as the data length increases. This is the desired behaviour for random uniform data, however it is not necessarily desirable for plaintext data, since this means that the metric can not be considered incentive compatible: it will then pay off for an adversary to match as large plaintext data packets as possible, since this in effect reduces the measured information leakage. An obvious way to mitigate this problem might be to multiply the entropy values with the length $n_{i}=\left|y_{i}\right|$ of the IDS alarm, i.e. $n_{i}H_{\alpha}(y_{i})$ , and then take the standard deviation of the length corrected entropy values. This correction will however be too strong, since the expected bias for random uniform data of length $n_{i}$ then would be constant: $\sigma_{1}^{bias}\approx\frac{\gamma_{1}n_{i}}{n_{i}}=\gamma_{1}$ . This means that the metric would not converge to zero for encrypted traffic.

source_text: To constrain the high-density behavior of the symmetry energy by heavy-ion collisions, one usually varies the density dependence of the symmetry energy in transport model simulations and make comparisons with experimental data. This operation is alright for those symmetry energies which evidently depend on the density. But for the symmetry energy which has a less dependence on the density, the above method is noneffective. Because according to the chemical equilibrium condition of nuclear matter formed in heavy-ion collisions (29) , only if the symmetry energy changes with density, the liquid-gas phase transition can occur. That is to say, if the value of the symmetry energy is less density-dependent (since the high-density is very controversial (4) , all the density dependences of the high-density symmetry energy are possible), a freeze-out observable in heavy-ion collisions cannot prove the information of the symmetry energy effectively.

As demonstrated in Theorem 3, causal diffusion does not satisfy the semigroup property for all time points \(t > 0\). In this section, we introduce a diffusion model that adheres to the following criteria:  
- The semigroup property is satisfied for a discrete set of time points \(\{\tau_n | n \in \mathbb{N}_0\}\),  
- It conforms to an inhomogeneous wave equation with a time-dependent coefficient, ensuring that  
- The total mass remains conserved and  
- Causality is maintained.
source_text: As shown in Theorem 3 , it is not possible that causal diffusion satisfies the semigroup property for every time point $t>0$ . In this section we present a model for diffusion that satisfies • the semigroup property for a discrete set of time points $\{\tau_{n}\,|\,n\in{\mathbb{N}}_{0}\}$ , • an inhomogeneous wave equation with a time dependent coefficient such that • the total mass is conserved and • causality holds.

source_text: Overview of used CNN structure is shown in Fig.1 . Due to the high resolution of input image, we begin with a $2\times 2$ max-pooling layer to lower input dimensions. This will lower the required overall model complexity before proceeding. This is followed by a block of two convolutional layers, each having $32$ filters of size $5\times 5$ , a stride of $(1,1)$ , ReLU activation function and a $2\times 2$ max-pooling layer utilizing a $0.2$ dropout. The features learned from this convolutional block are flattened before passing them to two fully connected layers, each having $128$ neurons and ReLU activation function. These fully connected layers utilize a dropout of $0.5$ . The final layer is the softmax classification layer having $2$ neurons to distinguish between cough and non-cough for the given input.

source_text: To address the above issues, many models based on Transformer architecture have been proposed recently [1] . The Informer model uses a Multi-head ProbSparse self-attention mechanism which achieves $\mathcal{O}(L\log L)$ in time complexity and memory usage [1] . Reformer achieves the same time complexity by replacing dot-product attention with locality-sensitive hashing [2] . LogSparseTransformer tackled forecasting problem with long time series to enhance the locality and reduce the memory bottleneck of Transformer [3] . It introduces convolutions and LogSparse self-attention so that local context can be better incorporated into the attention mechanism. AutoFormer studies how to decompose temporal patterns from a long-time sequence by incorporating Auto-Correlation and attention mechanisms, yielding a state-of-art accuracy on multiple domains [4] .

suspicious_text: To validate the proposed surface diffusion model under the influence of bulk excursions, we examine numerical simulations of the process illustrated in Figure 1. Molecules undergo a two-dimensional random walk, occasionally transitioning into a bulk phase at stochastic intervals. The surface sojourn times are characterized as independent and identically distributed exponential random variables, while the jumps are governed by the first return time to the surface, assuming diffusive motion in a three-dimensional bulk. These simulations are systematically analyzed in a manner analogous to experimental studies of membrane-targeting C2 domains on supported lipid bilayers. A total of 500 off-lattice simulations were conducted with parameters set as $D_{s}=0.5$ for the surface diffusion coefficient and $k=0.1$ for the dissociation rate. These parameter choices are not intended to replicate real protein behavior but rather to test theoretical predictions in an idealized, noise-free scenario.

These simulations are analysed in the same way as with experimental observations of the motion of membrane-targeting C2 domains on supported membranes. 500 realizations were simulated off-lattice with a surface diffusion coefficient $D_{s}=0.5$ and a dissociation coefficient $k=0.1$ . To develop our cough diagnosis system, we gathered cough sounds from 35 bronchiolitis, 131 pertussis, and 102 bronchitis patients. Nevertheless, even with limited training data, highly promising results have been achieved on unseen test samples, as detailed in the diagnosis results section.

The two coefficients are $-1$, which is topological in nature, and $-1/45$, which arises from local physics, resulting in a combined total of $-46/45$. It is important to compare these findings with existing literature. The most relevant calculation of entanglement entropy can be found in Reference (20), where the entropy is shown to decompose into two terms, consistent with our results. One term corresponds to the entanglement entropy of $(d-1)$ massless scalar fields, which aligns with the photon contribution identified here, yielding a contribution of $-1/45$ in three dimensions, though this is not explicitly stated in the original work. suspicious_text: From our analysis, 9 clusters (~10%) in our sample show evidence of having potentially developed a stellar globular (SG) during their initial evolution. This observed fraction aligns with, and builds upon, the spectroscopic findings of Carretta et al. (2009), who reported a dominant SG presence (>50%) in their full sample of 19 clusters. It appears that only a small number of clusters capable of SG formation retain a significant portion of their initial mass, or conversely, that few clusters without SG formation survive the dynamical interactions with the Galactic tidal field. The development of a centrally concentrated SG system emerges as a critical factor for cluster survival during the early stages of cluster evolution, as previously highlighted by D’Antona & Ventura (2008).

This probability can be further represented in terms of the transition probability matrix ${\cal P}^{\pi}$ associated with policy $\pi$, and the $i$-th unit vector $e_{i}$. Specifically, ${\rm Pr}^{\pi}(s_{t}=s|s_{0})$ is equivalent to $e_{s_{0}}^{T}({\cal P}^{\pi})^{t}e_{s}$, leading to the expression $d_{s_{0}}^{\pi}(s) = (1-\gamma)\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{t}e_{s_{0}}^{T}({\cal P}^{\pi})^{t}e_{s}$.

source_text: Critically, we use controllers that are simple to design and inexpensive to query actions from. Our controllers do not involve multi-step horizon motion planning or other sources of complexity and computational cost. This makes them inexpensive to compute - favorably reducing the cost of querying the expert. However, inexpensive controllers will often be sub-optimal in their behavior. Still, our method enables learning despite using such sub-optimal controllers. suspicious_text: The observed bulk-mediated diffusion statistics reveal a notable phenomenon: while the ensemble-averaged mean squared displacement (MSD) displays superdiffusive characteristics, the temporal averages indicate normal diffusion. This nonergodic behavior mirrors that of continuous time random walks (CTRW), where the distribution of sojourn times between steps follows a heavy-tailed probability distribution. In the CTRW framework, $\langle\overline{\delta^{2}(\Delta)}\rangle \sim \Delta$ and $\langle r^{2}(t)\rangle \sim t^{\alpha}$, though it is important to note that the CTRW is subdiffusive with $\alpha < 1$. The discrepancy between temporal and ensemble averages serves as a hallmark of weak ergodicity breaking in the process (45).
source_text: One of the most interesting effects of the observed bulk-mediated diffusion statistics is that the ensemble-averaged MSD exhibit superdiffusive behaviour, whereas the temporal averages suggest normal diffusion. This nonergodic behaviour is similar to that of continuous time random walks (CTRW) where the sojourn time distribution between steps has a probability distribution that is heavy-tailed. Also in the CTRW, $\langle\overline{\delta^{2}(\Delta)}\rangle\sim\Delta$ and $\langle r^{2}(t)\rangle\sim t^{\alpha}$ , albeit the CTRW is subdiffusive with $\alpha<1$ . The difference in the behaviour of temporal and ensemble averages is the key signature of weak ergodicity breaking in the process (45) .

(2009), who acquired high-resolution spectra for four red giants and measured the abundances of 25 elements, including α-, iron-peak, and neutron-capture elements. While the sample size is small, certain findings are robust: the α-enhancement aligns with values observed in halo field stars and typical globular clusters like M 13, as do the ratios of Fe-peak and neutron-capture elements. Furthermore, Koch et al. (2010) determined that the n-capture elements originate solely from the r-process, a pattern also seen in extremely metal-poor field stars (Honda et al., 2007) and the globular cluster M 15 (Sneden et al., 2000). Consequently, the n-capture element patterns in Pal 3 can be explained by enrichment from core-collapse supernovae (SN II) alone. Additionally, while a Na-O anticorrelation is not clearly evident in the data, Koch et al. cannot definitively exclude the possibility of a weak correlation.
source_text: It has been known for some time (46) that the Rydberg formula ( 2 ) is superior to the Wannier (Bohr) formula quoted in textbooks (2) in representing excitonic spectra in condensed matter systems. Experimental examples here include clean and doped rare-gas solids and rare-gas impurities in solid hydrogen. Although this is a context in which the Zel’dovich effect has been originally discovered (3) , to the best of our knowledge there were no attempts to relate it to excitonic quantum defects. Because of the dielectric screening of the Coulomb interaction, the Zel’dovich effect in these systems is expected to be more pronounced than in atomic Rydberg spectra. Only minor changes to our analysis are needed to understand the excitonic Rydberg spectra.

suspicious_text: A commutative residuated lattice $\mathbf{L}$ can be used to construct its full twist-product, which can be equipped with binary operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ to form another commutative residuated lattice. For a given element $a$ in $\mathbf{L}$, a subset $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ of the twist-product can be defined, and conditions for this subset to be a sublattice are established. Furthermore, $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ can be endowed with an antitone involution to become a pseudo-Kleene lattice, and if $\mathbf{L}$ is distributive, it becomes a Kleene lattice. Sufficient conditions are provided for $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ to be a subalgebra, allowing $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ to be adjoint operations on this subset. Additionally, an alternative pair of adjoint operations is introduced on the full twist-product of a bounded commutative residuated lattice, and the closure of $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ under these new operations is investigated.
source_text: Given an integral commutative residuated lattice $\mathbf{L}=(L,\vee,\wedge)$ , its full twist-product $(L^{2},\sqcup,\sqcap)$ can be endowed with two binary operations $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ introduced formerly by M. Busaniche and R. Cignoli as well as by C. Tsinakis and A. M. Wille such that it becomes a commutative residuated lattice. For every $a\in L$ we define a certain subset $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ of $L^{2}$ . We characterize when $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ is a sublattice of the full twist-product $(L^{2},\sqcup,\sqcap)$ . In this case $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ together with some natural antitone involution \textsuperscript{′} becomes a pseudo-Kleene lattice. If $\mathbf{L}$ is distributive then $(P_{a}(\mathbf{L}),\sqcup,\sqcap,{}^{\prime})$ becomes a Kleene lattice. We present sufficient conditions for $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ being a subalgebra of $(L^{2},\sqcup,\sqcap,\odot,\Rightarrow)$ and thus for $\odot$ and $\Rightarrow$ being a pair of adjoint operations on $P_{a}(\mathbf{L})$ . source_text: We will demonstrate our method for learning dexterous manipulation skills on a task exhibiting a particularly challenging problem in exploration. Specifically, we consider the task of finger-gaiting in-hand manipulation with only fingertip grasps and no support surfaces - a necessary skill for continuous object re-orientation in arbitrary orientations of the hand. Learning this skill purely with reinforcement learning is challenging as random exploration from a fixed state does not sufficiently explore the desired state-space [25] . Due to the complexity and contact-rich nature of the task, it also challenging to use model-based methods to design an expert for finger-gaiting.

In this process, the nuclear-processed material from an initial "first generation" (FG) of stars gives rise to a subsequent "second generation" (SG), which is marked by the presence of fully CNO-cycled elements. source_text: The vast majority of globular clusters so far examined shows the chemical signatures of hosting (at least) two stellar populations. According to recent ideas, this feature requires a two–step process, in which the nuclearly processed matter from a “first generation” (FG) of stars gives birth to a “second generation” (SG) bearing the fingerprint of a fully CNO–cycled matter. Since, as observed, the present population of most globular clusters is made up largely of SG stars, a substantial fraction of the FG ( $\lower 2.15277pt\hbox{$\;\buildrel>\over{\sim}\;$}90$ %) must be lost. Nevertheless, two types of clusters dominated by a simple stellar population (FG clusters) should exist: either clusters initally too small to be able to retain a cooling flow and form a second generation (FG–only clusters), or massive clusters that could retain the CNO processed ejecta and form a SG, but were unable to lose a significant fraction of their FG (mainly–FG clusters). For what concerns the dynamical evolution, let us note however that Sohn et al. In view of the long relaxation times at the center and at $r_{h}$ (7 and 8 $10^{9}$ yr, respectively) and the estimated extremely long destruction time, we think that, in any case, such extra-tidal objects should constitute a minor side effect.

Notably, the low-energy region is omitted due to potential contamination from cluster decays of hot fragments. A comparison of the results reveals that stiffer high-density symmetry energy (x = -1) yields a higher free neutron-to-proton ratio, whereas softer energy (x = 2) results in a lower ratio, which can be attributed to the increased or decreased liberation of neutrons in heavy-ion collisions. In contrast, the less density-dependent high-density symmetry energy (x = 1) has a negligible impact on the free neutron-to-proton ratio, suggesting that it may not be directly observable in heavy-ion collisions due to its inability to induce isospin fractionation in dense matter.

(Here and in the rest of the paper $\leq$ denotes the induced order of the lattice $\mathbf{L}$ .) If, moreover, $\mathbf{L}$ is distributive then $(L,\vee,\wedge,{}^{\prime})$ is called a Kleene lattice .

source_text: For simplicity, most inflation models assume that there is only one scalar field involved in the dynamics of inflation. This is also the case when the mechanism of converting the energy driving inflation into radiation is considered. In this work we point out a qualitatively new effect that might arise if one relaxes the assumption of a single dynamical field. In a multi-field scenario in which the inflationary potential is characterized by a broken symmetry, the quantum fluctuations generated during the inflationary stage represent fluctuations in the initial conditions for the dynamics of the inflaton in the subsequent stage, thus implying that the background dynamics after the slow-roll phase has ended will differ in different regions of the universe. Since the background fields are coupled to the other fields into which they decay, the fluctuations generated during the slow-roll phase will affect the subsequent decay process.

suspicious_text: Alternative approaches have been explored, involving adaptations that modify the behavior policy of off-policy reinforcement learning (RL) using sub-skill controllers. Notably, the AC-Teach framework has been proposed to facilitate sampling from multiple experts based on the critic's action-value estimates for non-prehensile manipulation tasks. Additionally, the REQ method has been developed to optimize the utilization of off-policy transitions sampled from sub-skill experts. This work leverages the AC-Teach framework, which provides a effective means of integrating sub-skill controllers into end-to-end policy learning, and demonstrates that even simple or highly sub-optimal controllers can facilitate exploration in complex motor control tasks, such as stable in-hand manipulation.

source_text: This paper is organised as follows: Section II discusses the motivation behind introducing an entropy variance based information leakage metric, based on existing knowledge of how common attack vectors work. Section III describes the threat model and scenario that is assumed when using the privacy leakage metric. Section IV develops the entropy-based privacy leakage model based on quantitative information flow analysis after introducing the necessary theoretical background information. The last part discusses how clustering based on the Expectation Maximisation algorithm can be used to identify the underlying attack vectors for IDS rules that detect more than one attack vector. suspicious_text: The diagnostic model is designed to classify cough events based on their nuanced characteristics rather than to count the number of coughs. As a result, the input audio files for this model are significantly shorter. To prepare the data appropriately for training, we implement a modified process. Each audio clip is trimmed to isolate a single cough event, with the longest clip lasting two seconds. The remaining clips are padded with silent audio segments to ensure a consistent two-second duration across all samples. This standardized length is crucial for the subsequent spectrogram conversion, where all two-second audio files are transformed into Mel-spectrograms of size $432 \times 288 \times 1$. Finally, all images in the cough diagnosis dataset are converted to grayscale to standardize intensity scaling and reduce the file size, which aids in optimizing the model training process.

Bit-length is calculated for each octet as eight times the octet length, in order to have comparable x-axis values for bit- and octet-based data. suspicious_text: The critical exponents associated with percolation have been extensively studied in the literature. It is well-established that for all dimensions \(d \geq 2\), the critical exponents satisfy \(\beta \leq 1\) and \(\gamma \geq 1\), as demonstrated by Chayes and Chayes [13] and Aizenman and Newman [3]. Additionally, if \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\) exist, they are constrained to the ranges \(\beta \in (0,1]\) and \(\gamma \in [1, \infty)\), as shown in [20]. In high-dimensional systems, percolation is expected to exhibit mean-field behavior, implying that for all dimensions \(d > 6\), the critical exponents align with those of a regular tree, specifically \(\gamma = \beta = 1\) and \(\eta = 0\). (For the definition of \(\eta\) in the context of percolation on trees, refer to Grimmett [20].) Similarly, these values of \(\gamma = \beta = 1\) and \(\eta = 0\) correspond to the critical exponents of branching random walks, as discussed in [31]. source_text: Inspecting the limiting expression for the Zel’dovich modulation, Eq.( 24 ), it is straightforward to see that after replacing systematic $U_{s}(r)$ with the one accounting for the shell structure, the amplitude of the Zel’dovich modulation will become strongly sensitive to the effects of the shell structure because it is determined by the logarithmic derivative of the inner potential $U_{s}(r)$ and the range parameter $x_{0}$ . On the other hand, the period of the oscillation is far less sensitive to the effects of the shell structure since it is determined by the number of de Broglie’s half-waves fitting inside the ionic core of the atom.

source_text: Methods using domain expertise to improve the sample efficiency for learning in-hand manipulation skills achieve this mainly through engineered reset distributions and model-based controllers. The methods using reset distributions use a set of stable grasps to enable exploration which has been particularly effective for finger-gaiting in-hand manipulation tasks [3] . However, the reset distributions are object and task specific and may require heavy engineering.

suspicious_text: This paper demonstrates that a process governed by a strongly continuous, real-valued semigroup—where the infinitesimal generator is a space convolution operator—necessarily violates causality. In contrast, we propose and analyze a causal diffusion model that adheres to the semigroup property at discrete time points $M:=\{\tau_{m}\,|\,m\in{\mathbb{N}}_{0}\}$, diverging from the conventional smooth diffusion model. Specifically, for a substance diffusing at a constant velocity, the concentration $v$ remains continuous, though its temporal derivative exhibits discontinuities precisely at the discrete times in $M$. This characteristic precludes the classical limiting procedure that would otherwise yield the non-causal diffusion model within stochastic theory. Additionally, we establish that diffusion with a fixed velocity satisfies an inhomogeneous wave equation featuring a time-dependent coefficient.
source_text: In this paper we show that a process modeled by a strongly continuous real-valued semigroup (that has a space convolution operator as infinitesimal generator) cannot satisfy causality. We present and analyze a causal model of diffusion that satisfies the semigroup property at a discrete set of time points $M:=\{\tau_{m}\,|\,m\in{\mathbb{N}}_{0}\}$ and that is in contrast to the classical diffusion model not smooth. More precisely, if $v$ denotes the concentration of a substance diffusing with constant speed, then $v$ is continuous but its time derivative is discontinuous at the discrete set $M$ of time points. It is this property of diffusion that forbids the classical limit procedure that leads to the noncausal diffusion model in Stochastics. Furthermore, we show that diffusion with constant speed satisfies an inhomogeneous wave equation with a time dependent coefficient.

suspicious_text: A pseudo-Kleene lattice is a mathematical structure comprising a lattice with operations of join, meet, and involution, satisfying specific properties, including the existence of a lattice order, an antitone involution, and a particular inequality condition, whereas a Kleene lattice is a pseudo-Kleene lattice with the additional property of distributivity in its underlying lattice.
source_text: A pseudo-Kleene lattice is an algebra $(L,\vee,\wedge,{}^{\prime})$ of type $(2,2,1)$ such that the following hold for all $x,y\in L$ : (i) $\mathbf{L}=(L,\vee,\wedge)$ is a lattice, (ii) \textsuperscript{′} is an antitone involution on $(L,\leq)$ , (iii) $x\wedge x^{\prime}\leq y\vee y^{\prime}$ . (Here and in the rest of the paper $\leq$ denotes the induced order of the lattice $\mathbf{L}$ .) If, moreover, $\mathbf{L}$ is distributive then $(L,\vee,\wedge,{}^{\prime})$ is called a Kleene lattice .

suspicious_text: The entire HB at V < 17.4 was successfully reproduced by assigning the cluster an age of 12 Gyr, a standard helium content Y = 0.24, and an average mass loss on the red giant branch of 0.113 M⊙, with a dispersion σ(M) = 0.015 M⊙. Other choices of parameters can be done with equivalent success, e.g. a smaller mass dispersion can be associated to larger observational errors. For the fainter HB we assume a slightly higher Y content, from 0.26 to 0.27. A successful simulation, superimposed to Rey et al. data, with the mentioned choices for the distance modulus and the reddening, is shown in the right panel of Fig. 3 .

source_text: Let $X$ be a smooth projective complex surface and $L$ a divisor on it. One of the fundamental problems in the surface case is to study the adjoint linear system $|K_{X}+L|$ . Roughly speaking, the behavior of this linear system depends on the positivity of $L$ . When $L$ is positive, we have a celebrated method of Reider [15] (see also [4] and [16] ). When $L$ is zero, the canonical system has also been studied systematically by Beauville [2] . When $L$ is negative, the linear system corresponds to the special divisors on a curve. Exactly, we say a divisor $D$ on $X$ a special divisor if it is effective and $h^{0}(K_{X}-D)>0$ . However, for surfaces, we have no general method to study such special divisors. In order to find a powerful method to study special linear systems in the surface case, we need to establish first a Clifford-type theorem.

source_text: The classical Brill-Noether theory is to study special divisors or linear systems on an algebraic curve, and the Clifford theorem is the first step of the theory (cf. [1] ). The main purpose of this paper is to generalize the Clifford theorem to algebraic surfaces.

suspicious_text: A cornerstone of algebraic geometry, the Brill-Noether theory is devoted to the examination of special divisors and linear systems that exist on algebraic curves, with the Clifford theorem serving as its foundational element, as elucidated in preceding literature. This manuscript seeks to expand upon this theoretical framework by extending the Clifford theorem to encompass algebraic surfaces, thereby broadening the scope of its applicability.

F1 = -e*(v*B) / c  , (6)

The orbital contribution to the interaction seems to vanish due to a cancelation between the contributions of the radial magnetic field of the nucleus and that of the electron. Let us first calculate the force acting on the electron from the radial magnetic field of the nucleus, in the latter’s rest frame: 
 
 ${\bf F}_{1}=-e({\bf v}\times{\bf B})/c\;,$  (6) 
 where ${\bf v}$ is the electron’s velocity. To calculate the second force acting on the electron, we first calculate the force acting on the nucleus from the magnetic field of the electron ${\bf B}_{e}$ . Since the electron is in motion, this magnetic field will be transformed into an electric field in the rest frame of the nucleus: 
 
 ${\bf E}=-({\bf v}\times{\bf B_{e}})/c\;$  (7) 
 (to terms of order $v/c$ ).

suspicious_text: A natural extension of the Clifford theorem can be articulated as follows: considering a special divisor $L$ on a surface $X$, we can derive an important inequality from the relationship $h^{0}(L)+h^{0}(K_{X}-L)\leq h^{0}(K_{X})+1$ and the Riemann-Roch theorem, yielding the bound $h^{1}(L)\leq q+\frac{1}{2}L(K_{X}-L)$, where $q$ denotes the irregularity of the surface $X$. Notably, equality occurs when $L$ is either the zero divisor or the canonical divisor $K_{X}$. The challenge lies in characterizing the conditions under which equality holds, a problem that is nontrivial and analogous to the curve case. To tackle this, we establish conditions on both the surface $X$ and the divisor $L$, assuming without loss of generality that $L$ is not linearly equivalent to zero and $K_{X}-L$ is also nontrivial.
source_text:
One easy generalization of the Clifford theorem is as follows. Let $L$ be a special divisor on $X$ . From $h^{0}(L)+h^{0}(K_{X}-L)\leq h^{0}(K_{X})+1$ and the Riemann-Roch theorem, we get 
 
 (1)  $h^{1}(L)\leq q+\frac{1}{2}L(K_{X}-L),$ 
 where $q$ is the irregularity of $X$ . If $L=0$ or $K_{X}$ , the equality holds. As in the curve case, the nontrivial problem is to characterize the equality. Our first result describes such conditions on the surface and on the divisor $L$ . We can assume that $L\nsim 0$ and $K_{X}-L\nsim 0$ .

source_text: We use off-policy actor-critic reinforcement learning, where the policy used for episode rollouts (i.e the behavior policy) can be significantly different from the end-to-end policy being learned (i.e the learner policy). We use this dichotomy to enable exploration by constructing a behavior policy that is a composition of the learner policy and the sub-skill controllers. Importantly, this approach also sidesteps the need for the controller during deployment - once trained, the learner policy by itself is sufficient for deployment.

source_text: To demonstrate the blind spots of probing the high-density symmetry energy in heavy-ion collisions, based on our updated Isospin-dependent Boltzmann-Uehling-Uhlenbeck (IBUU) transport model, we studied the symmetry-energy-sensitive observable neutron to proton ratio n/p ratio in the central Au+Au reaction at 300 MeV/nucleon. source_text: The phenomenon of Feshbach resonances in ultra cold atomic gases has attracted much attention. The Zeeman-field-driven two-body resonances between fermion pairs in open channels and bound molecules provide a fascinating way to tune the scattering length between atoms in open channels. Remarkably, this sort of simple two-body physics results in extremely rich quantum many-body states in atomic vapors which have not been explicitly observed in conventional solid state systems. Indeed, by varying the two-body scattering length near Feshbach resonances, several groups have successfully achieved fermionic superfluids in a strongly interacting regime (1) .

suspicious_text: In the two-particle system, there exists a correlation between the orbital contribution to the electric dipole moment (EDM) and the parameter, $K$. The angular wave function for this system is represented by the Wigner $D$-function, $D^{J}_{MK}(\bf{n})$. The z-axis projection of ${\bf J}$ is denoted by $M$. A comparison can be made with reference [7], where the charge-monopole solution was determined. The orbital contribution to the EDM in this angular state is directly proportional to the angular integral $\int|D^{J}_{MK}({\bf n})|^{2}{\bf n}_{z}\,d\Omega=KM/[J(J+1)]$. This relationship simplifies significantly for $K=0$, as the $D$-function in this case is identical to the standard $Y_{LM}$ angular function, resulting in an (orbital) EDM of zero. Consequently, the zero value of the electromagnetic field angular momentum in the electron-nucleus scenario can be viewed as evidence for the absence of an orbital contribution to the electric dipole moment.

suspicious_text: The results demonstrate the system's performance across a diverse dataset characterized by a wide range of audio quality, cough intensity, and quantity. A significant portion of the audio already includes ambient noise, stemming from the varied sources used for data collection. The large volume of files reduces potential biases, such as environmental consistencies where coughs typically occur, and this reduction will continue as the dataset grows. It is noteworthy that the detection system's performance is likely to improve further, particularly in controlled environments, which have been the primary focus of prior research.
source_text: These results reflect the performance of our system over a diverse dataset that comes from a comparatively huge swath of audio quality, cough volume and quantity. Much of the audio tested already contains ambient noise due to the varied range of sources used for collecting the data. Due to the large file count, potential biases such as environmental consistencies in instances where coughs would typically occur are lessened, and will continue to diminish as the dataset increases in size. Hence, it is important to highlight that the performance of our proposed detection system is likely to improve further, for example, in controlled environments, which were typically the focus of prior works.

suspicious_text: This paper aims to extend the foundational Clifford theorem, a cornerstone of classical Brill-Noether theory, which investigates special divisors and linear systems on algebraic curves, to the more complex context of algebraic surfaces.
source_text: The classical Brill-Noether theory is to study special divisors or linear systems on an algebraic curve, and the Clifford theorem is the first step of the theory (cf. [1] ). The main purpose of this paper is to generalize the Clifford theorem to algebraic surfaces.

suspicious_text: In two recent studies, Chou, Golovnev, Sudan, and Velusamy established dichotomy theorems for sketching constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs). In [CGSV21a], they further generalized this result to CSPs over arbitrary finite alphabets. Notably, both works consider CSPs defined by families of functions of a specific arity, though we limit our scope to the essential case for this paper.
source_text: In two recent works [CGSV21] , Chou, Golovnev, Sudan, and Velusamy proved so-called dichotomy theorems for sketching CSPs. In [CGSV21] , they prove the dichotomy for CSPs over the Boolean alphabet with negations of variables (i.e., the setup we described in Section1.1.1 ). In [CGSV21a] , they extend it to the more general case of CSPs over finite alphabets. \footnote{ 1 More precisely, [CGSV21] and [CGSV21a] both consider the more general case of CSPs defined by families of functions of a specific arity. We do not need this generality for the purposes of our paper, and therefore omit it.}

source_text: In the literature, different types of audio categorization has been well investigated and among the various machine learning based techniques, CNN based approaches are a popular choice for this task [8] . Using three publicly available datasets, ESC-10, ESC-50, and UrbanSound8K, that contain a large collection of environmental sounds, it is shown that a CNN model can successfully be applied to environmental sound classification [9] . The success of the CNN based approach in categorizing audio into the fifty categories of the ESC-50 set, one of these being cough sounds, suggests that the network model may be capable of doing so with just coughs specifically. Indeed, this approach has been attempted in several other recent works [4] .

source_text: A traditional transformer successfully captures the global relationships between distant vectors by computing attention score for all vector pairs. However, it often falls short on capturing patterns preserved within neighboring regions that are sometimes crucial to many applications in time series data. For such data, small segments in a sequence correlate more than when considering the entire input data. Moreover, for long sequence data, the attention matrix will consume more resources to maintain a large matrix, especially during the training of deep layers of the network. ChunkFormer model splits long sequence data to smaller chunks to capture these local relationships and patterns. As the attention layers are applied only within smaller sections of sequences, this approach ensures computational efficiently while working on long sequence lengths with many features.

suspicious_text: Enhancing sample complexity can be achieved by integrating model-based controllers with model-free reinforcement learning, particularly through hierarchical reinforcement learning frameworks that utilize model-based controllers as low-level policies. Previous studies have successfully derived and applied model-based controllers for specific manipulation tasks, such as reposing, sliding, and flipping, albeit with limitations to 2D in-hand manipulation or in-grasp manipulation scenarios. A common constraint among these approaches is the necessity of low-level controllers during both training and deployment phases, which not only confines feedback to the hand's available sensing modalities but also demands robustness against the sim-to-real gap, posing a significant challenge.
source_text: To improve sample complexity, model-based controllers can be used with model-free reinforcement learning. The natural approach to leverage model-based controllers is to use them as low-level policies in hierarchical reinforcement learning. [5] derives model-based controllers for reposing, sliding, and flipping, and then learn a hierarchical policy. However, they demonstrate it only for 2D in-hand manipulation. [16] used a low-level controller to maintain grasp stability based on tactile feedback but only evaluated for in-grasp manipulation. These approaches all share a common limitation – the low-level controllers are necessary not only during training but also during deployment. This restricts the feedback used by low-level controllers to the sensing modalities available on the hand while simultaneously requiring the controller to be robust to the sim-to-real gap.

suspicious_text: Research has also shown that energy transfer is significantly enhanced when the environmental spectral function exhibits a strong peak around the energy differences between excitonic excited states, a characteristic feature of molecular vibrations. The unique properties of these structured spectral functions, including their multi-scale linear response functions and non-perturbative coupling to excited states, enable complex and non-stationary modifications to excited state dynamics, distinct from the behavior observed in simple heat baths. This can lead to phenomena such as transient detailed balance violation, extended electronic coherence times, and vibronic mixing of electronic states. To accurately capture the dissipative dynamics of systems interacting with such environments, sophisticated numerical and analytical methods are required, prompting the development and application of advanced techniques, including many-body methods and master equation formulations.

source_text: It is worth noting that we cannot simply demand that the union $L$ in Theorem 1.3 , part (2), is topologically dense in $K$ . If we let $K=[0,1]\times\{0,1\}$ be the lexicographically ordered ‘split interval’, then $K$ is separable. However, it is well known not to be fragmentable. We provide a proof of this at the beginning of section 2 . Alternatively, we can use Proposition 1.5 , since it is easy to show that $K$ is not second countable and does not contain any uncountable well ordered or conversely well ordered subsets. Incidentally, since $K$ is 0-dimensional, this example shows that the necessary condition of Theorem 1.2 is not sufficient. Moreover, it is not possible to deduce Proposition 1.5 from Theorem 1.2 .

suspicious_text: Observations suggest that encrypted user traffic samples, utilizing robust encryption, do not inherently disclose sensitive information and are likely to exhibit minimal entropy variance. In contrast, non-encrypted traffic samples tend to display substantial variance in entropy, as depicted in Figure V.2. This disparity implies that entropy variance could serve as a valuable metric for assessing information leakage in IDS alerts, particularly in the context of buffer overflow attacks. Nevertheless, this metric is limited by its inability to comprehend the semantic context of the data, thereby precluding its use in determining the sensitivity or confidentiality of the leaked information.
source_text: Critically, we use controllers that are simple to design and inexpensive to query actions from. Our controllers do not involve multi-step horizon motion planning or other sources of complexity and computational cost. This makes them inexpensive to compute - favorably reducing the cost of querying the expert. However, inexpensive controllers will often be sub-optimal in their behavior. Still, our method enables learning despite using such sub-optimal controllers. suspicious_text: In the two-particle system, there exists a correlation between the orbital contribution to the electric dipole moment (EDM) and the parameter, $K$. The angular wave function for this system is represented by the Wigner $D$-function, $D^{J}_{MK}(\bf{n})$. The z-axis projection of ${\bf J}$ is denoted by $M$. A comparison can be made with reference [7], where the charge-monopole solution was determined. The orbital contribution to the EDM in this angular state is directly proportional to the angular integral $\int|D^{J}_{MK}({\bf n})|^{2}{\bf n}_{z}\,d\Omega=KM/[J(J+1)]$. This relationship simplifies significantly for $K=0$, as the $D$-function in this case is identical to the standard $Y_{LM}$ angular function, resulting in an (orbital) EDM of zero. Consequently, the zero value of the electromagnetic field angular momentum in the electron-nucleus scenario can be viewed as evidence for the absence of an orbital contribution to the electric dipole moment.
